Appointed by the Joseon royal court as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces (Samdo Sugun Tongjesa) on August 1 (lunar calendar).
Dismissed from the post of Commander-in-Chief on April 11 (February 25 by the lunar calendar), escorted to Hanseong, and imprisoned.
Spared from the death penalty on May 16 (April 1 by the lunar calendar) and ordered to serve as a common soldier (Baeguijonggun) under Commander-in-Chief Gwon Yul.
His mother passed away on a boat on May 26 (April 11 by the lunar calendar) while he was serving as a common soldier.
Reappointed as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces on August 28 (July 16 by the lunar calendar) after Won Gyun's devastating defeat at the Battle of Chilcheollyang.
Achieved a great victory at the Battle of Myeongnyang on October 26 (September 16 by the lunar calendar), destroying over 130 of the hundreds of Japanese ships with only 13 warships.
Killed in action by a stray bullet while pursuing retreating Japanese forces at the Battle of Noryang on November 19 (lunar calendar).
Posthumously promoted to Senior First Rank (Jeong 1-pum), Daegwang Boguk Sungnok Daebu, Right State Councilor of the State Council, and concurrent Royal Lecturer in December.
Posthumously honored as a First Class Seonmu Merit Subject and Deokpung Buwongun in June, and additionally granted the title of Left State Councilor and concurrent Royal Lecturer in July.
Received the posthumous title 'Chungmu' (Loyalty and Valor) from King Injo in March, becoming Chungmugong.
Additionally posthumously promoted to Senior First Rank, Chief State Councilor of the State Council, by King Jeongjo in July.

keyAchievements.title: Passed the Military Examination
keyAchievements.description: Passed the triennial military examination in 1576 at the age of 32, placing fourth in the Pyonggwa category, and began his official career.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the Battle of Nokdundo
keyAchievements.description: In 1587, during the Battle of Nokdundo caused by a surprise attack by Jurchens, he stayed behind with Yi Gyeong-rok, fought and won, rescuing over 60 Joseon citizens.

keyAchievements.title: Development and Construction of the Turtle Ship
keyAchievements.description: Immediately after his appointment as Left Naval Commander of Jeolla, he strengthened military preparations by expanding weapons and provisions and developing and building the Turtle Ship in preparation for war.

keyAchievements.title: Victories in Major Naval Battles of the Imjin War
keyAchievements.description: After the outbreak of the Imjin War, he successively defeated the Japanese navy in major naval battles including Okpo, Sacheon, Hansan Island, and Busan, seizing control of the sea and cutting off Japanese supply lines.

keyAchievements.title: Served as Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces
keyAchievements.description: Appointed as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces (Samdo Sugun Tongjesa) in 1593, taking supreme command and rebuilding the Joseon navy.

keyAchievements.title: Great Victory at the Battle of Myeongnyang
keyAchievements.description: After the defeat at Chilcheollyang, he achieved a great victory in the Myeongnyang Strait with 12 ships against hundreds of Japanese ships, reversing the tide of the Jeongyu War (Second Invasion).

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Noryang and Death in Action
keyAchievements.description: In the Battle of Noryang in 1598, he joined forces with the Ming navy to heavily defeat the retreating Japanese forces, and was killed in action by a stray bullet during the battle.

keyAchievements.title: Revered as a Hero-Saint
keyAchievements.description: Revered as a martial saint who saved the nation through successive victories in naval battles against the Japanese navy with brilliant intellect and outstanding strategy as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces during the Imjin War.

faq.question: When and where was Yi Sun-shin born?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin was born on April 28, 1545 (March 8 by the lunar calendar) in Geoncheon-dong, Southern Ward, Hanseong-bu (near the current Sindo Building at 19 Eulji-ro 18-gil, Jung-gu, Seoul).

faq.question: When did Yi Sun-shin pass the military examination?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin passed the triennial military examination on March 22, 1576 (the 9th year of King Seonjo) at the age of 32, placing fourth in the Pyonggwa category.

faq.question: Why was Yi Sun-shin stripped of his rank and made to serve as a common soldier (Baeguijonggun) twice?
faq.answer: The first time was in 1587 when the Northern Army Commander Yi Il wrongly blamed him for the defeat at the Battle of Nokdundo, leading to his imprisonment and subsequent conscription as a common soldier. The second time was in 1597 when he was dismissed from his post as Commander-in-Chief and ordered to serve as a common soldier on charges of disobeying the court's orders.

faq.question: In which naval battle did Yi Sun-shin die in action?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin was killed in action by a stray bullet on November 19, 1598 (lunar calendar), during the Battle of Noryang while pursuing the retreating Japanese forces.

faq.question: What are the famous writings left by Yi Sun-shin?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin left behind 'Nanjung Ilgi' (War Diary), which records his experiences during the Imjin War, and 'Chungmugong Yi Sun-shin Jeonseo', a complete collection of his posthumous works.

faq.question: What posthumous title did Yi Sun-shin receive?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin received the posthumous title 'Chungmu' (Loyalty and Valor) from King Injo in March 1643 (the 21st year of King Injo), thus becoming Chungmugong.

faq.question: What was Yi Sun-shin's family structure?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin had three sons (Yi Hoe, Yi Ye, Yi Myeon) and one daughter with his wife, Lady Bang of Sangju (Onyang Bang clan), and two sons (Yi Hun, Yi Sin) and two daughters with his concubine, Lady Oh of Haeju.

timeline.event: Born in the State of Zhao to Prince Yiren and Zhao Ji
Began reign as the 31st King of the State of Qin (Lü Buwei began regency)
Began personal rule
Conquered the State of Han
Conquered the State of Zhao
Conquered the State of Wei
Conquered the State of Chu
Conquered the State of Yan
Conquered the State of Qi and unified the Chinese mainland
Carried out the Burning of Books (Fen Shu)
Carried out the Burying of Scholars (Keng Ru)
Died at the age of 50 in Shaqiu during his final inspection tour

keyAchievements.title: Unification of China
keyAchievements.description: Unified a divided China and established the imperial system and the commandery-county system (Junxian system), laying the basic framework for Chinese dynasties over the next 2,000 years.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Imperial System
keyAchievements.description: First to use the title 'Emperor' (Huangdi) instead of 'King' and proclaimed himself 'First Emperor' (Shi Huangdi).

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of the Commandery-County System
keyAchievements.description: Following the advice of Chancellor Li Si, he divided the entire country into 36 commanderies and appointed an administrator, a military commander, and an inspector to each, establishing a centralized system of governance.

keyAchievements.title: Standardization of Weights, Measures, Currency, and Script
keyAchievements.description: To rule the empire efficiently, he standardized weights and measures, currency, and writing systems, and improved the road networks.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the Great Wall
keyAchievements.description: Connected the defensive walls of the Warring States period to complete the Great Wall to prevent invasions by the Xiongnu.

faq.question: When was Qin Shi Huang born?
faq.answer: Qin Shi Huang was born in 259 BC in the State of Zhao to Prince Yiren, who was sent there as a hostage from the State of Qin, and Zhao Ji.

faq.question: Why did Qin Shi Huang use the title 'Emperor' (Huangdi)?
faq.answer: Considering the title of 'King' inadequate for himself, Qin Shi Huang took the characters 'Huang' and 'Di' from the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors to form 'Huangdi' (Emperor), and styled himself the 'First Emperor' (Shi Huangdi).

faq.question: What were the major tyrannical acts of Qin Shi Huang?
faq.answer: Qin Shi Huang carried out the 'Burning of Books and Burying of Scholars', a massive cultural suppression in which he burned all books except those concerning the history of Qin, medicine, and agriculture, and buried alive over 460 scholars.

faq.question: What efforts did Qin Shi Huang make to achieve immortality?
faq.answer: In his quest for the elixir of life, Qin Shi Huang sent the alchemist Xu Fu with 3,000 boys and girls and a ship laden with treasures to Mount Penglai. He also ingested mercury, mistakenly believing it to be the elixir of immortality.

faq.question: How did Qin Shi Huang die?
faq.answer: During his final inspection tour in 210 BC, Qin Shi Huang fell severely ill in the region of Shaqiu and passed away at the age of 50.

timeline.event: Born in the city of Rome.
Death of his father, Octavius.
Delivered the funeral oration for his maternal grandmother, Julia Caesaris.
Donned the toga virilis and was elected to the College of Pontiffs.
Presided over the Greek games in honor of the Temple of Venus Genetrix, built by Caesar.
Assassination of his adoptive father, Julius Caesar.
Arrived in Rome and met Mark Antony.
Defeated two legions under Antony's command.
Admitted to the Senate and granted military command.
Defeated Antony's forces at the Battle of Forum Gallorum and the Battle of Mutina.
Appointed consul.
Formed the Second Triumvirate with Antony and Lepidus near Bologna.
The Roman Senate declared Julius Caesar a god.
Defeated the forces of Brutus and Cassius at the Battle of Philippi.
Ended the siege of Lucius Antonius at Perusia and received his surrender.
Executed 300 senators and equestrians who had collaborated with Lucius Antonius.
Signed the Treaty of Brundisium with Antony.
Married his older sister, Octavia Minor, to Antony.
Concluded a temporary peace agreement, the Pact of Misenum, with Sextus Pompey.
Divorced Scribonia and married Livia Drusilla.
Agreed to extend the Triumvirate for another five years.
Completely destroyed Sextus Pompey's fleet at the Battle of Naulochus.
The Senate stripped Antony of his consular powers and declared war on Egypt.
Defeated the fleets of Antony and Cleopatra at the Battle of Actium.
Began holding the consulship every year.
Achieved victory in Alexandria, leading to the suicides of Antony and Cleopatra.
Granted the title of 'First Citizen of the State' (princeps civitatis) by the Senate.
After ending the civil wars, declared the return of extraordinary powers to the Senate and was awarded the title 'Augustus'.
Married his daughter Julia to his sister's son, Marcellus.
Marcellus died, and Augustus resigned from the consulship.
Personally undertook the construction of roads.
Finally suppressed the rebellion in the Cantabria region of Spain.
Assumed the office of Pontifex Maximus after the death of Lepidus.
His stepson Drusus died after falling from a horse in Germania.
Elected consul, and his maternal grandson Gaius Caesar began his political career.
Elected consul, his maternal grandson Lucius Caesar began his political career, and he was granted the title of 'Father of the Country' (pater patriae) by the Senate and the Roman people.
His maternal grandson Lucius Caesar died young.
His maternal grandson Gaius Caesar died young, and he adopted Tiberius and Agrippa Postumus as his sons.
Established the 'Military Treasury' (aerarium militare) to fund pensions for active and retired soldiers.
Three Roman legions were annihilated by Germanic tribes in the Teutoburg Forest.
Tiberius was granted all the prerogatives held by Augustus.
Died in Nola, and the Senate and the popular assembly declared him a god.

keyAchievements.title: First Emperor of the Roman Empire
keyAchievements.description: Reigned as the first Emperor of the Roman Empire and the founder of the Julio-Claudian dynasty.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Pax Romana
keyAchievements.description: His rule ushered in an era of peace and prosperity known as the 'Pax Romana' (Roman Peace), which sustained peace in the Mediterranean world for over two centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of Imperial Territory and Border Protection
keyAchievements.description: Expanded the territory of the Roman Empire, protected its borders and allies, and concluded a peace treaty with Parthia.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of the State System
keyAchievements.description: Reformed Rome's taxation system, built an overland transportation network, and established a standing army, a small navy, and the Praetorian Guard.

keyAchievements.title: Redevelopment of the City of Rome
keyAchievements.description: Established police and fire-fighting forces in Rome and extensively redeveloped large parts of the city.

keyAchievements.title: Recording the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti'
keyAchievements.description: Before his death, he left behind a record of his accomplishments known as the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti'.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Title 'Augustus'
keyAchievements.description: Received the title 'Augustus', meaning 'the revered one', from the Senate.

keyAchievements.title: Granted the Title 'First Citizen of the State'
keyAchievements.description: Was granted the title 'princeps civitatis' (First Citizen of the State) by the Senate, meaning the leading figure.

keyAchievements.title: Securing Military Command
keyAchievements.description: Became the supreme commander of the Roman military and held the authority to celebrate triumphs.

keyAchievements.title: Tax Reform
keyAchievements.description: Executed tax reforms, including a fixed-rate direct tax on provincials and the introduction of a tax collection system managed by civil servants.

keyAchievements.title: Naming of the Month 'August'
keyAchievements.description: The former 'sixth month' (Sextilis) was renamed 'Augustus' (August) in his honor.

keyAchievements.title: Construction Projects
keyAchievements.description: Contributed so significantly to the architectural development of Rome that he famously boasted, 'I found Rome a city of bricks and left it a city of marble'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Military Pension Fund
keyAchievements.description: Established the 'Military Treasury' (aerarium militare) to provide pensions for active duty and retired soldiers.

faq.question: What was the real name of Augustus?
faq.answer: Before being adopted by Caesar, his name was Gaius Octavius, and after adoption, he became known as Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus.

faq.question: How did Augustus become the first emperor of the Roman Empire?
faq.answer: After the collapse of the Second Triumvirate, he monopolized power and, upon receiving the title 'Augustus' from the Senate in 27 BC, became the first emperor of the Roman Empire.

faq.question: What was the greatest achievement of the Augustan era?
faq.answer: His rule brought about an era of prosperity known as the 'Pax Romana' (Roman Peace), sustaining peace in the Mediterranean world for more than two centuries.

faq.question: Did Augustus enjoy gambling?
faq.answer: Contrary to his stoic image, he enjoyed gambling, particularly dice and odd-even games, and often built friendships by intentionally losing money.

faq.question: Why was the month of August named after Augustus?
faq.answer: By decision of the Roman Senate, the month was named in his honor to commemorate his capture of Alexandria and ascent to power in August of 30 BC.

timeline.event: Born in Schönhausen, Prussia
Enrolled at the Plamannsche Lehranstalt in Berlin
Enrolled in law at the University of Göttingen
Began practical training as a legal clerk after graduating from university
Began military service
Started life as a farm manager after completing military service
Entered politics by being elected as a member of the United Diet of Saxony
Acted as a counter-revolutionary during the March Revolution in Berlin
Elected as a member of the Prussian United Diet
Appointed as the Prussian envoy to the Federal Diet in Frankfurt
Appointed as the Prussian ambassador to Russia
Appointed Minister President of Prussia after serving as ambassador to France
Delivered the 'Blood and Iron' speech in parliament
Victory in the Second Schleswig War against Denmark
Victory in the Austro-Prussian War and signing of the Peace of Prague
Formation of the North German Confederation
Victory in the Franco-Prussian War
Proclamation of the German Empire and appointment as its first Chancellor
Began the 'Kulturkampf' (culture struggle) suppressing Catholics in southern Germany
Establishment of the Reichsbank (Imperial Bank)
Presided over the Congress of Berlin following the Russo-Turkish War
Enacted the 'Anti-Socialist Laws'
Implemented health insurance
Implemented accident insurance and acquired colonies in Togo and Cameroon, Africa
Acquired the colony of German East Africa
Implemented old age and disability insurance
Resigned and retired from politics due to policy conflicts with Wilhelm II
Death

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Germany and Creation of the German Empire
keyAchievements.description: Achieved the unification of Germany and established the German Empire by expanding Prussia's military and winning wars against Denmark, Austria, and France.

keyAchievements.title: Blood and Iron Policy
keyAchievements.description: Earned the nickname 'Iron Chancellor' through a speech advocating military expansion upon taking office, using it as a basis to strengthen military power.

keyAchievements.title: Maintaining the Balance of Power in Europe
keyAchievements.description: As Chancellor of the German Empire, he dominated European diplomacy and strove to maintain a balance of power among the great powers by forging agreements such as the League of the Three Emperors and the Triple Alliance.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Social Security Systems
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for a welfare state by implementing the world's first social security systems, including health insurance, accident insurance, and old age and disability insurance.

keyAchievements.title: Acquisition of Colonies
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to securing German colonies in Africa, such as Togo, Cameroon, and German East Africa, by encouraging the development of German capitalism and colonial expansion.

faq.question: Why is Bismarck called the 'Iron Chancellor'?
faq.answer: He earned this nickname from his 'Blood and Iron' speech in 1862, which advocated military expansion, though he shifted to a pacifist policy after unification.

faq.question: How did Bismarck achieve the unification of Germany?
faq.answer: Following his 'Blood and Iron' policy, he expanded the military and won wars against Denmark, Austria, and France, leading to the proclamation of the German Empire and achieving unification.

faq.question: What were Bismarck's major domestic policies?
faq.answer: He implemented the 'Kulturkampf' to suppress Catholics, enacted the 'Anti-Socialist Laws' to suppress socialism, and introduced the world's first social security systems.

faq.question: Why did Bismarck resign from the chancellorship?
faq.answer: He resigned and retired from politics in 1890 due to policy conflicts with Emperor Wilhelm II.

timeline.event: Born in the vilayet of Salonica (now Thessaloniki, Greece) under the rule of the Ottoman Empire.
Enrolled in the Salonica Military School.
Enrolled in the Manastir Military High School.
Enrolled in the Ottoman Military Academy.
Graduated from the Ottoman Military Academy.
Graduated from the Ottoman Military College.
Promoted to Senior Captain.
Assigned to the Third Army headquarters in Manastir.
Appointed as the Ottoman railway inspector of Eastern Rumelia.
Joined the Young Turk Revolution.
Returned to Istanbul after suppressing a tribal rebellion.
Contributed to the suppression of the 31 March Incident, a counter-coup in Istanbul.
Assigned to Ottoman Albania.
Participated in large-scale military maneuvers in Picardy, France, as an Ottoman observer officer.
Briefly served in the Ottoman Ministry of War in Istanbul.
Deployed to Ottoman Tripolitania (modern-day Libya) and fought in the Italo-Turkish War.
Achieved a major victory in the Battle of Tobruk.
Suffered vision impairment in his left eye due to an injury sustained at the Battle of Derna.
Appointed commander of the Ottoman forces at Derna.
Returned from the Italo-Turkish War front upon the outbreak of the Balkan Wars.
The Italo-Turkish War ended, and under the Treaty of Ouchy, Ottoman Tripolitania and other territories were ceded to the Kingdom of Italy.
Achieved a decisive victory in the Battle of Sakarya, putting an end to the Megali Idea (Greater Greece).
The date of the great victory against Greece, which is celebrated as a national holiday in Turkey.
Abolished the Sultanate and deposed Mehmed VI.
Signed a new treaty (the Treaty of Lausanne) with the Allied Powers.
Moved the capital from Istanbul to Ankara, proclaimed the republic, and became the first President.
Abolished the Caliphate, an institution that had existed for 1,300 years since 632.
Implemented dress reforms to emancipate women's attire, instituted co-educational equality, and replaced the Islamic calendar with the European Gregorian calendar.
Amended the civil code to ban polygamy and establish monogamy.
Abolished the Arabic script for the Turkish language and replaced it with a Latin-based alphabet.
Granted voting rights to women.
The Turkish parliament bestowed upon him the surname 'Atatürk', meaning 'Father of the Turks'.
Signs of Atatürk's deteriorating health began to appear.
Suffered from severe illness during a trip to Yalova and was diagnosed with cirrhosis of the liver.
Died at the age of 57 at the Dolmabahçe Palace.

keyAchievements.title: Founder and First President of the Republic of Turkey
keyAchievements.description: As the founder and first President of the Republic of Turkey, he led the Turkish War of Independence.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the Battle of Gallipoli
keyAchievements.description: An outstanding general who successfully halted the offensive of the British and French forces at the Battle of Gallipoli during World War I.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the Sultanate and Caliphate
keyAchievements.description: Abolished the Sultanate in 1922, deposing Mehmed VI, and abolished the Caliphate in 1924.

keyAchievements.title: Legislation of Secularism
keyAchievements.description: Enshrined secularism, the founding principle of the Republic of Turkey, into law.

keyAchievements.title: Dress Reforms and Ensuring Women's Right to Education
keyAchievements.description: Enacted dress reforms to emancipate women's attire and guaranteed the previously forbidden right of women to an education, instituting co-educational equality.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the Islamic Calendar and Introduction of the Gregorian Calendar
keyAchievements.description: Replaced the Islamic calendar with the European Gregorian calendar.

keyAchievements.title: Revision of the Civil Code and Establishment of Monogamy
keyAchievements.description: Amended the civil code to prohibit polygamy and establish monogamy.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Latin Alphabet
keyAchievements.description: Abolished the Arabic script used for writing Turkish and replaced it with a Latin-based alphabet.

keyAchievements.title: Granting Women the Right to Vote
keyAchievements.description: Granted women the right to vote in 1930.

faq.question: What was Atatürk's original name?
faq.answer: His childhood name was Mustafa. The name 'Kemal' was given to him by his mathematics teacher, who praised his mathematical abilities and mature demeanor.

faq.question: What does the title 'Atatürk' mean and when was it bestowed?
faq.answer: The title 'Atatürk' means 'Father of the Turks' and was bestowed upon him by the Turkish parliament in 1934.

faq.question: What is Atatürk's status in the Republic of Turkey?
faq.answer: He is revered in Turkey as an independence hero and the founding father of the nation. The Turkish parliament granted him the honorific 'Atatürk', and he is the sole individual featured on Turkish banknotes.

faq.question: When did Atatürk die and what was the cause of death?
faq.answer: He died on November 10, 1938, at the age of 57. He had been diagnosed with cirrhosis of the liver earlier in 1938.

faq.question: What were the major reform policies implemented by Atatürk?
faq.answer: He implemented numerous reforms, including the abolition of the Caliphate, the legislation of secularism, dress reforms, securing women's right to education, the abolition of the Islamic calendar, the ban on polygamy, the shift to the Latin alphabet, and granting women the right to vote.

timeline.event: Annelies Marie 'Anne' Frank is born in Frankfurt, Germany.
When Hitler comes to power and racial discrimination against Jews is enforced, Anne's family goes into exile in Amsterdam, the Netherlands.
Kristallnacht, a massive pogrom against Jews, occurs, and Anne faces difficulties attending school due to discrimination against Jews.
Nazi Germany occupies the Netherlands and begins hunting down all Jews.
Receives a red-checkered diary as a present for her 13th birthday and begins writing in it, addressing it to 'Kitty'.
Her father, Otto Frank, completes preparations for the family to go into hiding.
Anne's family begins hiding in the Secret Annex at Prinsengracht 263 in Amsterdam.
The date of the last entry in Anne's diary.
Following an anonymous tip, the Nazi police raid the Secret Annex; all the Jews, including Anne's family, are arrested and sent to concentration camps.
Anne Frank dies of typhus in the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp at the age of 16.
Her father, Otto Frank, publishes Anne's diary as a book.
Director George Stevens makes a film based on Anne's diary, which wins three Academy Awards.
Time magazine reveals letters detailing Otto Frank's efforts to save his family.
The Anne Frank House announces that it will permanently display Anne's original manuscripts.

keyAchievements.title: Writing 'The Diary of a Young Girl'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a diary documenting her life in hiding for two years and one month during the Nazi occupation.

keyAchievements.title: Global Impact
keyAchievements.description: Her diary has been translated into about 60 languages and sold over 32 million copies, serving as the catalyst for the establishment of the Anne Frank House.

faq.question: When and where was Anne Frank born?
faq.answer: Anne Frank was born on June 12, 1929, in Frankfurt, a commercial city in Germany.

faq.question: When did Anne Frank start writing her diary?
faq.answer: She received a red-checkered diary for her 13th birthday, named it 'Kitty', and began writing in it in June 1942.

faq.question: When and how did Anne Frank die?
faq.answer: Anne Frank died of typhus in the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp in February or March 1945, at the age of 16.

faq.question: When was Anne's diary published, and by whom?
faq.answer: Anne's diary was published as a book by her father, Otto Frank, on June 25, 1947.

faq.question: Into how many languages has Anne's diary been translated, and how many copies have been sold?
faq.answer: Anne's diary has been translated into approximately 60 languages, and it is estimated to have sold around 32 million copies.

timeline.event: Born Rosa Louise McCauley in Tuskegee, Alabama.
Marries Raymond Parks.
Completes her high school education with the support of her husband.
Joins the Montgomery chapter of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) and begins participating in the civil rights movement.
Arrested by the police in Montgomery, Alabama, for refusing a bus driver's order to give up her seat to a white passenger.
Announces that Black churches across the region will join the Montgomery bus boycott.
Charged with disorderly conduct, found guilty, and ordered to pay a fine and court costs.
Forms the 'Montgomery Improvement Association' at the Zion Church and elects Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. as its president.
Ends her role as secretary of the NAACP Montgomery chapter.
Begins traveling and speaking to raise awareness about the bus incident.
Hired as a secretary for African American U.S. Representative John Conyers, working in his Detroit office.
Retires from her secretarial position.
Co-authors her autobiography, 'Rosa Parks: My Story', with Jim Haskins.
Co-authors her memoir, 'Quiet Strength: The Faith, the Hope, and the Heart of a Woman Who Changed a Nation', with Gregory J. Reed.
Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the highest civilian award, by the Bill Clinton administration.
Dies in Detroit at the age of 92.

keyAchievements.title: Mother of the Modern Civil Rights Movement
keyAchievements.description: Named the 'Mother of the Modern Civil Rights Movement' by the United States Congress.

keyAchievements.title: Montgomery Bus Boycott
keyAchievements.description: Her arrest in 1955 for refusing a bus driver's order to give up her seat to a white passenger sparked the Montgomery bus boycott and the broader American civil rights movement.

keyAchievements.title: Voter Registration
keyAchievements.description: Succeeded in registering to vote after three attempts, despite Jim Crow laws designed to hinder Black political participation.

keyAchievements.title: Work as NAACP Secretary
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the civil rights movement while serving as secretary of the Montgomery chapter of the NAACP from December 1943 to 1957.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the highest civilian honor, by the Bill Clinton administration in 1996.

keyAchievements.title: Lying in Honor at the Capitol
keyAchievements.description: Became the first woman and the second Black person to lie in honor at the Capitol Rotunda in Washington, D.C.

faq.question: When and where was Rosa Parks born?
faq.answer: Rosa Parks was born on February 4, 1913, in Tuskegee, Alabama.

faq.question: What was the incident that led to Rosa Parks's arrest on the bus?
faq.answer: On December 1, 1955, in Montgomery, Alabama, she was arrested for refusing a bus driver's order to give up her seat to a white passenger.

faq.question: What movement did Rosa Parks's bus incident lead to?
faq.answer: The incident led to the Montgomery bus boycott, which lasted for 382 days, involved Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., and sparked the American civil rights movement.

faq.question: When did Rosa Parks die?
faq.answer: Rosa Parks died in Detroit on October 24, 2005, at the age of 92.

faq.question: What jobs did Rosa Parks hold?
faq.answer: She held various jobs after her marriage, including working as a seamstress, and from 1965 until her retirement in 1988, she worked as a secretary for U.S. Representative John Conyers.

timeline.event: Born Frederick Augustus Washington Bailey in Talbot County, Maryland.
His mother, Harriet Bailey, dies (Frederick is 9).
Sophia Auld, the wife of Hugh Auld, teaches him to read (Frederick is 10).
Returns to a plantation in Talbot County, is rented out to 'slave breaker' Edward Covey, and fights back against a beating.
Returns to Fells Point, joins the East Baltimore Mental Improvement Society, and meets Anna Murray.
Escapes from slavery to freedom on September 3; marries Anna Murray on September 15; moves to New Bedford, Massachusetts, and changes his name to Frederick Douglass.
Is inspired by hearing William Lloyd Garrison speak at a meeting of the Bristol Anti-Slavery Society; gives his first speech at a Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society convention in Nantucket.
Participates in the American Anti-Slavery Society's Hundred Conventions project, touring the eastern and midwestern United States.
Is beaten by a mob in Pendleton, Indiana, breaking his hand.
Publishes his first book, 'Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave', and leaves for a tour of Ireland.
Returns to the United States, settles in Rochester, New York, and founds the newspaper 'The North Star'.
Attends the Seneca Falls Convention and signs the Declaration of Sentiments.
Merges 'The North Star' with Gerrit Smith's 'Liberty Party Paper' to form 'Frederick Douglass' Paper'.
Publishes 'My Bondage and My Freedom'.
Returns from England following the death of his youngest daughter, Annie, at age 11 in Rochester.
Meets with President Lincoln regarding the treatment of Black soldiers.
Supports Ulysses S. Grant's presidential campaign.
Moves to Washington, D.C., after his home in Rochester burns down.
Purchases his final home, 'Cedar Hill', in Washington, D.C.
Appointed Recorder of Deeds for the District of Columbia.
His wife, Anna, passes away.
Marries Helen Pitts, a white suffragist.
Travels to England, France, Italy, Egypt, and Greece with his new wife, Helen Pitts.
Publishes 'Life and Times of Frederick Douglass'; appointed Haitian commissioner to the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago.
Dies in Washington, D.C., on February 20.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Figure in the Abolitionist Movement
keyAchievements.description: Was a central figure in the abolition of slavery in the United States, advocating based on his experiences as a slave and his biblical convictions.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Orator and Author
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'Sage of Anacostia', he is considered one of the most influential orators and authors in American history.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of His Autobiography
keyAchievements.description: Published 'Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave' in 1845, which became an immediate bestseller and helped expose the realities of slavery.

keyAchievements.title: Newspaper Publisher
keyAchievements.description: Published several newspapers, including 'The North Star', founded in 1847, to advocate for abolition and civil rights.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Women's Suffrage Movement
keyAchievements.description: Supported women's equality by attending the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848 and signing the Declaration of Sentiments.

keyAchievements.title: Meetings with Presidents
keyAchievements.description: Was the first Black man to meet with President Abraham Lincoln (to discuss the treatment of Black soldiers) and President Andrew Johnson (to discuss Black suffrage).

keyAchievements.title: Holding Political Office
keyAchievements.description: Held several significant political positions after the Civil War, including President of the Freedman's Savings Bank, Marshal of the District of Columbia, and Minister to Haiti.

faq.question: What was Frederick Douglass's original name?
faq.answer: His original name was Frederick Augustus Washington Bailey.

faq.question: Why did he change his name?
faq.answer: After escaping slavery, he changed his name to Frederick Douglass, taking the surname from a heroic character in Sir Walter Scott's poem 'The Lady of the Lake'.

faq.question: What is his most famous book?
faq.answer: His 1845 autobiography, 'Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave'.

faq.question: What was his view on the U.S. Constitution?
faq.answer: He initially believed the Constitution was pro-slavery, but later changed his view, concluding that it was an anti-slavery document that could be used as a weapon in the fight against slavery.

faq.question: What was his stance on racial equality?
faq.answer: He firmly believed in the equality of all people—Black, female, immigrant, etc.—and spent his life advocating for the brotherhood of all mankind.

faq.question: Who was his second wife?
faq.answer: His second wife was Helen Pitts, a white suffragist and the daughter of his fellow abolitionist Gideon Pitts Jr.

timeline.event: Marriage of her parents, Harriet 'Rit' Green and Ben Ross
Born Araminta 'Minty' Ross (estimated birth year)
Her father, Ben Ross, is freed from slavery according to a former owner's will
Marries John Tubman
Escapes to Philadelphia following deteriorating health
Escapes with her brothers Ben and Henry, but the brothers turn back
Rescues her niece Kessiah and Kessiah's two children
Returns to Maryland and rescues her brother Moses and two other enslaved people
Returns to Dorchester County to find her husband John, but he has remarried and refuses to leave with her
Leads a group of 11 escaped slaves to Canada
Her father Ben and his brother buy her mother Rit from Eliza Brodess for $20
Rescues her elderly parents and brings them to St. Catharines, Ontario, Canada
Is introduced to the abolitionist John Brown
John Brown reveals his plan to raid Harpers Ferry, Virginia, in Chatham-Kent, Ontario
Buys a small piece of land on the outskirts of Auburn, New York, from Senator William H. Seward for $1,200
Undertakes her final rescue mission, bringing the Ennals family and another group to freedom
Arrives with the Ennals family and others at the home of David and Martha Coffin Wright in Auburn
Heads to the Hilton Head area of South Carolina after the outbreak of the Civil War
President Lincoln issues the Emancipation Proclamation
Executes the Combahee River Raid, rescuing over 700 enslaved people
Stays at a military base for several months after the Confederate surrender before returning home
Marries Nelson Davis
Sarah Bradford publishes the biography 'Scenes in the Life of Harriet Tubman'
Gets caught up in a gold-smuggling scam
Adopts a baby girl named Gertie
Sarah Bradford publishes 'Harriet, the Moses of Her People'
Undergoes brain surgery at Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston
Participates as a keynote speaker at the first meeting of the National Federation of Afro-American Women
Begins receiving a pension for her military service during the Civil War
Donates a parcel of her land to the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church
Attends the opening of the Harriet Tubman Home for the Aged as an honorary guest
Enters the nursing home named after her
Dies of pneumonia
Earl Conrad publishes 'Harriet Tubman: Negro Soldier and Abolitionist'
The United States Maritime Commission launches the SS Harriet Tubman, its first Liberty ship named after a Black woman
The US Postal Service issues the Tubman commemorative stamp, the first to honor an African American
Molefi Kete Asante includes Harriet Tubman on his list of '100 Greatest African Americans'
Jean Humez publishes a book about Tubman's life
Kate Larson and Catherine Clinton publish biographies of Tubman
Towson University names a new campus residence hall 'Tubman House'
US Treasury Secretary Jack Lew announces that Tubman's portrait will replace Andrew Jackson's on the $20 bill
Posthumously commissioned as a brigadier general in the Maryland National Guard on Veterans Day

keyAchievements.title: Activism in the Abolitionist Movement
keyAchievements.description: Through the 'Underground Railroad', a network of anti-slavery activists, she established 13 safe houses and rescued around 70 enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an 'Underground Railroad' Conductor
keyAchievements.description: As a 'conductor' on the Underground Railroad from 1850 to 1860, she ventured into the South and led over 300 Black people to freedom in the North.

keyAchievements.title: Rescuing Family
keyAchievements.description: After escaping to Philadelphia, she returned to Maryland to lead her relatives out of the state, eventually bringing freedom to dozens of enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Civil War
keyAchievements.description: Served the Union Army as a cook, nurse, armed scout, and spy.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Combahee River Raid
keyAchievements.description: Became the first woman to lead an armed assault during the Civil War, commanding the Combahee River Raid and freeing over 700 enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Women's Suffrage Movement
keyAchievements.description: Fought for women's suffrage after the war, attending related conferences and speaking out in cities like New York, Boston, and Washington D.C.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to the Establishment of a Home for the Aged
keyAchievements.description: Spent her final years in a home for elderly African Americans that she helped establish years earlier. In 1903, she donated part of her land to the church, requesting it be used for a home for 'aged and indigent colored people'.

faq.question: What was Harriet Tubman's original name?
faq.answer: Harriet Tubman's birth name was Araminta Ross.

faq.question: How many enslaved people did Harriet Tubman rescue?
faq.answer: She directly rescued around 70 people in 13 trips via the Underground Railroad, led over 300 to freedom in the North as a 'conductor' between 1850 and 1860, and freed more than 700 through the Combahee River Raid.

faq.question: Was there a bounty on Harriet Tubman?
faq.answer: There is a rumor of a $40,000 bounty on her head, but this is untrue. Even claims of a $12,000 bounty lack supporting evidence.

faq.question: What role did Harriet Tubman play in the Civil War?
faq.answer: She served the Union Army as a cook, nurse, armed scout, and spy. She was the first woman to lead an armed assault, freeing over 700 enslaved people in the Combahee River Raid.

faq.question: Why was Harriet Tubman nicknamed 'Moses'?
faq.answer: As she guided more and more people to freedom from slavery, she became famously known as 'Moses', drawing a parallel to the biblical figure in Exodus who liberated his oppressed people.

faq.question: Will Harriet Tubman appear on the $20 bill?
faq.answer: On April 20, 2016, US Treasury Secretary Jack Lew announced that Tubman's portrait would replace former President Andrew Jackson's on the front of the $20 bill.

timeline.event: Born as the second daughter to William Edward Nightingale and Frances Nightingale.
Meets the Swiss socialist Sismondi while traveling in Italy.
Observes a hospital in Alexandria while traveling in Egypt and realizes the importance of formal nursing education.
Studies nursing at a Protestant school in Kaiserswerth, Germany, and becomes the superintendent of a women's hospital in London.
Serves as a military nurse at a field hospital in Scutari during the Crimean War.
Elected as the first female member of the Royal Statistical Society.
Establishes the Nightingale Training School for Nurses and authors professional nursing texts.
Awarded the Dame of Grace of the Order of St John (DStJ).
Becomes the first woman to receive the Order of Merit from King Edward VII.
Passes away at the age of 90.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Nursing Profession
keyAchievements.description: By serving as a military nurse in the Crimean War, she popularized the profession of nursing and matured it into a specialized field.

keyAchievements.title: Improving Hospital Hygiene
keyAchievements.description: Implemented discipline and sanitary conditions in the chaotic field hospital at Scutari during the Crimean War, reducing the mortality rate from 42 percent to 2 percent.

keyAchievements.title: Building a Nursing Education System
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for nursing education by establishing the Nightingale Training School for Nurses in 1860 and authoring professional nursing texts.

keyAchievements.title: Proposing the Concept of Intensive Care Units
keyAchievements.description: First proposed the concept of an intensive care unit during the Crimean War, suggesting that critically ill patients be isolated for specialized care.

keyAchievements.title: Statistical Analysis
keyAchievements.description: Used data analysis from the Crimean War to argue for the importance of hospital hygiene, becoming the first female member of the Royal Statistical Society.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: Her book 'Notes on Nursing' served as the foundational text for nursing practice and education, and the Nightingale Pledge is still recited by nurses worldwide.

faq.question: Have there been any critical views of the veneration of Nightingale?
faq.answer: In 1999, the British public sector union UNISON opposed the veneration of Nightingale, arguing that it hindered nursing independence and contributed to low wages, while also overshadowing the achievements of other nurses.

faq.question: Were there other notable nurses active during Nightingale's time?
faq.answer: Mary Seacole from Jamaica was active during the same period, but due to racial discrimination, she was unable to work alongside Nightingale and lived in poverty.

timeline.event: Born as the second of three sons and one daughter to Yu Jung-gwon and Lee So-je in Byeongcheon-myeon, Cheonan, South Chungcheong Province.
Transferred to the 3rd year of the common course at Ewha Haktang as a scholarship student upon the recommendation of Alice Sharp, an American Methodist missionary.
Graduated from the common course at Ewha Haktang and enrolled in the higher course.
Participated in the March 1st Movement in Seoul.
Participated in the Manse (independence) demonstrations in Seoul.
Returned to her hometown of Cheonan with her cousin Yu Ye-do after Ewha Haktang was closed by order of the Governor-General.
Arrested by the Japanese while playing a leading role in the Manse demonstration at the Aunae Market in Cheonan.
Sentenced to 5 years in prison by the Gongju District Court for sedition and violation of the Security Law.
Sentenced to 3 years in prison by the Gyeongseong Appeals Court; the sentence was confirmed after she abandoned her appeal.
Died in Seodaemun Prison due to a ruptured bladder.
Following protests by Ewha Haktang staff, her body was received from Seodaemun Prison and dressed in a shroud.
A funeral service was held at Jeongdong Methodist Church, and she was buried in the Itaewon public cemetery.
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Independence Medal.
Because her remains were lost, a cenotaph (Chohonmyo) was erected halfway up Maebongsan Mountain in Byeongcheon-myeon, Cheonan.
Her birthplace in Yongdu-ri, Byeongcheon-myeon, Dongnam-gu, Cheonan City, South Chungcheong Province, was restored and designated as Historic Site No. 230.
Ewha Girls' High School awarded her an honorary diploma.
The Yu Gwan-sun Memorial Hall opened in Byeongcheon-myeon, Cheonan, South Chungcheong Province, to mark the 100th anniversary of her birth.
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Republic of Korea Medal.

keyAchievements.title: Participation and Leadership in the March 1st Movement
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the March 1st Movement in Seoul in 1919 and, upon returning to her hometown of Cheonan, took a leading role in organizing the Manse demonstration at the Aunae Market.

keyAchievements.title: Martyrdom for the Independence Movement
keyAchievements.description: Arrested by the Japanese and died a martyr in Seodaemun Prison from a ruptured bladder caused by severe torture, demonstrating an unwavering commitment to the independence movement.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of the Spirit of Independence
keyAchievements.description: Remembered as a symbolic figure of the independence movement, she was posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation (Independence Medal and Republic of Korea Medal), and memorial projects have been established in her honor.

faq.question: What is Yu Gwan-sun's family clan (bon-gwan)?
faq.answer: Yu Gwan-sun's family clan is Goheung.

faq.question: Was there any controversy regarding the spelling of her name?
faq.answer: There was past debate over whether to spell her surname as 'Yu' or 'Ryu' due to the initial sound rule in Korean. Since 2014, it has been officially spelled as 'Yu Gwan-sun' to reduce public confusion.

faq.question: What was Yu Gwan-sun's sentence in the first trial?
faq.answer: On May 9, 1919, the Gongju District Court sentenced her to 5 years in prison for sedition and violating the Security Law.

faq.question: What was the cause of Yu Gwan-sun's death?
faq.answer: She died in prison on September 28, 1920, from a ruptured bladder caused by the torture she endured while serving her sentence in Seodaemun Prison.

faq.question: Where are Yu Gwan-sun's remains?
faq.answer: Her remains were lost during the relocation of the Itaewon public cemetery. Currently, there is a cenotaph (Chohonmyo) erected halfway up Maebongsan Mountain in her hometown of Byeongcheon-myeon, Cheonan.

faq.question: How did Yu Gwan-sun's parents die?
faq.answer: Her father, Yu Jung-gwon, and mother, Lee So-je, were killed on the spot by the Japanese during the Aunae Manse demonstration on April 1, 1919.

timeline.event: Born to a peasant family in Domrémy, a village in northeastern France.
At age 13, received divine revelations from Saint Michael, Saint Catherine, and Saint Margaret.
Around the age of 17, sought out Charles VII, gained his trust, and joined the Hundred Years' War.
Underwent a theological examination in Poitiers, which reported that she had 'lived an irreproachable life, was a devout Christian, and possessed the virtues of humility, honesty, and simplicity'.
Arrived at the besieged city of Orléans.
The French army attacked and recaptured the fortress of Saint-Loup.
Advocated for and led the attack on Les Tourelles, the main English stronghold.
Recaptured Jargeau, Meung-sur-Loire, and Beaugency.
Defeated the English army at the Battle of Patay.
Facilitated the coronation of Charles VII as King of France at Reims Cathedral.
Attacked Paris but retreated upon the king's orders.
Recaptured Saint-Pierre-le-Moûtier and was granted a title of nobility.
Captured by Burgundian forces during the siege of Compiègne.
Transferred to Rouen, which was under English control.
Her trial for heresy began in English-occupied Rouen.
Sentenced to burn at the stake by the ecclesiastical court.
Burned at the stake before a crowd in the Place du Vieux-Marché in Rouen.
A retrial for Joan of Arc was initiated at the behest of Pope Callixtus III.
A formal appeal against the charges levied on Joan of Arc was submitted.
The appellate court pronounced her innocent of all charges and declared her a martyr.
Beatified by the Holy See.
Canonized as a saint by Pope Benedict XV.

keyAchievements.title: Decisive Contribution to the French Victory in the Hundred Years' War
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in reversing the tide of the Hundred Years' War by boosting the morale of the French army and leading them to victory in several battles, notably the Siege of Orléans.

keyAchievements.title: Facilitating the Coronation of Charles VII
keyAchievements.description: Through the recapture of Reims, she enabled Charles VII to be crowned King of France at Reims Cathedral, establishing his legitimacy.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Rehabilitation and Canonization
keyAchievements.description: Cleared of all charges and declared a martyr in a posthumous retrial, she was beatified in 1909 and canonized as a saint in 1920.

faq.question: Why was Joan of Arc tried by an ecclesiastical court?
faq.answer: The English accused her of treason and heresy to undermine the legitimacy of Charles VII. Additionally, the Church believed her claims of communicating directly with God disrupted ecclesiastical hierarchy.

faq.question: How did Joan of Arc die?
faq.answer: She was sentenced to death by burning on May 29, 1431, by an ecclesiastical court. The following day, May 30, she was tied to a pillar and burned at the stake before a crowd in the Place du Vieux-Marché in Rouen.

faq.question: Why did Joan of Arc wear men's clothing?
faq.answer: Joan stated that she wore men's clothing to function properly in the male-dominated army and to protect her chastity in case of assault.

faq.question: Why was a retrial held for Joan of Arc?
faq.answer: The retrial was held to clear up any doubts about the legitimacy of Charles VII's claim to the French throne and, at the request of Pope Callixtus III and Joan's mother, to re-examine the validity of her original conviction.

timeline.event: Born on June 14 in Litchfield, Connecticut, USA.
Lived in Cincinnati, Ohio.
Wrote and published a children's geography book under her sister Catharine's name.
Married Calvin Stowe, a seminary professor.
Published the anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'.
Published the novel 'Dred'.
Published the novel 'The Minister's Wooing'.
Published the novel 'The Pearl of Orr's Island'.
Published 'Lady Byron Vindicated'.
Published 'Sam Lawson's Oldtown Fireside Stories'.
Published the novel 'Oldtown Folks'.
Died on July 1 at the age of 85 in Hartford.

keyAchievements.title: Writing 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'
keyAchievements.description: Published the anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin', which intensified the conflict of opinion between the North and South, ultimately leading to the Civil War.

keyAchievements.title: Writing a Children's Geography Book
keyAchievements.description: Wrote and published her first book, a children's geography book, in 1833 under her sister Catharine's name.

faq.question: Who was Harriet Beecher Stowe?
faq.answer: She was an American abolitionist and realist author, best known for her anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'.

faq.question: What was the impact of 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'?
faq.answer: The novel profoundly influenced the debate on slavery at the time and significantly widened the divide between the North and South, paving the way for the Civil War.

faq.question: Why did Harriet Beecher Stowe become interested in the abolitionist movement?
faq.answer: She witnessed the miserable living conditions of enslaved Black people during a trip to Kentucky, which fueled her passion for the abolitionist movement. Furthermore, the loss of her mother at a young age is said to have evoked a deep sense of empathy within her for enslaved boys and girls separated from their families.

timeline.event: Born as the only son of Kim Sun-yeong and Gwak Nak-won in Haeju, Hwanghae Province (birth name Kim Chang-am).
Took the Gwageo (state examination) at age 17 but failed.
Joined the Donghak movement and changed his name from Chang-am to Chang-su.
Participated in the Donghak Peasant Revolution, leading the Donghak army in Palbong, Haeju.
Sought refuge with An Tae-hun following the failure of the Donghak Peasant Revolution.
Murdered the Japanese national Tsuchida Josuke in Chihapo, Hwanghae Province, and was arrested.
While in prison, read books like the 'Great Learning' and 'World History' and taught fellow inmates how to read and write.
Succeeded in breaking out of prison with fellow inmates, became a Buddhist monk at Magoksa Temple in Gongju, and received the Buddhist name Wonjong.
Left Magoksa Temple and wandered before returning to secular life at Yeongcheonam Hermitage in Pyongyang and heading back to his hometown.
Worked as a village school teacher (hunjang) on Ganghwa Island, established a school in his hometown, and converted to Methodism after the mourning period for his father ended.
Established Bongyang School in Jangyeon, Hwanghae Province, and worked as a public school teacher and an agricultural committee member.
Engaged in movements to restore national sovereignty, including protests against the Eulsa Treaty.
Became a teacher at Seomyeonguisuk in Munhwa County, Hwanghae Province, and married Choi Jun-rye.
Joined the Sinminhoe (New People's Association) and served as the chief of its Hwanghae Province branch.
At age 36, changed his name from Gu (龜) to Gu (九).
Changed his name to Gu to remove himself from the Japanese family register, and adopted the pen name Baekbeom.
Implicated in the 105-Man Incident, sentenced to 15 years in prison, and incarcerated in Incheon Prison and Seodaemun Prison.
Granted special parole in August.

Went into exile in Shanghai, China, shortly after the March 1st Movement; participated in the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea, serving as a member of the Provisional Assembly and the Director of the Police Bureau.
Re-elected to the Provisional Assembly, appointed Minister of Home Affairs in the Provisional Government, organized the Korean Veterans Association, and became its first chairman.
Reappointed as Minister of Home Affairs, became acting Premier, and issued an order to dissolve the National Representative Conference.

His wife, Choi Jun-rye, passed away in Shanghai; directed the assassination of pro-Japanese collaborators and fundraising for military activities through figures like Park Hui-gwang of the Korean Independence Army (Daehan Tonguibu).
Elected President (Gungmuryeong) by the Provisional Assembly in December.
Resigned en masse from the presidency, was elected the first Chairman of the State Council, and appointed Minister of Home Affairs.
Organized the Korean Independence Party, was elected to its board of directors, and was reappointed as President.
Formed the Korean Patriotic Organization to assassinate Japanese officials and key figures.
Directed the Sakuradamon Incident by Lee Bong-chang and the Hongkou Park Incident by Yun Bong-gil.
Published a statement in Shanghai newspapers claiming responsibility for the bombings, escaped from Shanghai, and began life in hiding.
Supported the assassination of Ok Gwan-bin, met with Chiang Kai-shek, and agreed to establish a Korean training class at the Luoyang Military Academy.
Organized the Korean Special Forces Independence Army in Nanjing.
Opposed calls to dissolve the Provisional Government, founded the Korean National Party, and was elected its chairman.
Shot and injured by Lee Wun-han at Nanmu Hall in Changsha, Hunan Province.
His mother, Gwak Nak-won, passed away.
Elected Chairman (Juseok) of the Provisional Government in March, organized the Korean Liberation Army in September, and held a founding ceremony.
Sent a letter to US President Roosevelt requesting recognition of the Provisional Government, promulgated the Founding Principles of the Republic of Korea, and declared war on Japan.

Re-elected as Chairman of the Provisional Government, regained the right of supreme command over the Korean Liberation Army from the Republic of China, and inaugurated as Chairman of its Supreme Command.
After liberation, promoted movements to recognize the legitimacy of the Provisional Government, opposed trusteeship, and protested against the US-Soviet Joint Commission.
Visited Pyongyang to participate in North-South negotiations for establishing a unified government; however, after separate elections in the South, he devoted himself to educating the youth.
Assassinated by Ahn Doo-hee at his residence, Gyeonggyojang, on June 26.
Posthumously awarded the 'Order of Merit for National Foundation, Republic of Korea Medal'.

keyAchievements.title: Participation and Leadership in the Donghak Peasant Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the Donghak Peasant Revolution in 1894 and led the Donghak army in Palbong, Haeju.

keyAchievements.title: Chihapo Incident
keyAchievements.description: Murdered the Japanese national Tsuchida Josuke in Chihapo, Hwanghae Province, in 1896 to avenge the assassination of Empress Myeongseong.

keyAchievements.title: Patriotic Enlightenment Movement and Sinminhoe Activities
keyAchievements.description: Devoted himself to educational activities, such as establishing schools in the early 1900s, and served as the head of the Hwanghae Province branch of the Sinminhoe (New People's Association), a secret organization for restoring national sovereignty, in 1907.

keyAchievements.title: Holding Key Positions in the Provisional Government
keyAchievements.description: Served in major roles within the Provisional Government, including Assembly Member, Police Bureau Director, Home Affairs Minister, President, and Chairman, defending its legitimacy starting from 1919.

keyAchievements.title: Organizing the Korean Patriotic Organization and Directing Anti-Japanese Actions
keyAchievements.description: Formed the Korean Patriotic Organization in 1931 and directed the assassination attempts by Lee Bong-chang in Tokyo and Yun Bong-gil in Hongkou Park.

keyAchievements.title: Creation and Command of the Korean Liberation Army
keyAchievements.description: Organized the Korean Liberation Army, the first official military force of the Provisional Government, in 1940, and led it after becoming Chairman of its Supreme Command in 1944.

keyAchievements.title: Enactment of the Founding Principles and Declaration of War on Japan
keyAchievements.description: Promulgated the Founding Principles of the Republic of Korea and declared war on Japan in 1941, proclaiming the nation's resolve for independence.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in North-South Negotiations and Dedication to Youth Education
keyAchievements.description: Visited Pyongyang in 1948 to participate in North-South negotiations for a unified government, and subsequently devoted himself to youth education.

faq.question: What were Kim Gu's original name and pen names?
faq.answer: His original name was Chang-am. He changed it to Chang-su at age 19, and then from Gu (龜) to Gu (九) at age 36 (in 1912). His pen names were Baekbeom and Yeonsang.

faq.question: How did he come up with the pen name 'Baekbeom'?
faq.answer: The pen name 'Baekbeom' was created by taking 'Baek' (白) from 'baekjeong' (butcher), symbolizing the lowest class of people, and 'beom' (凡) from 'beombu' (commoner), symbolizing ordinary people.

faq.question: Who did Kim Gu kill in the Chihapo Incident, and why?
faq.answer: In 1896, Kim Gu murdered a Japanese man named Tsuchida Josuke in Chihapo, Hwanghae Province. He stated the reason was to avenge the death of Empress Myeongseong, the mother of the nation.

faq.question: What were Kim Gu's major roles in the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea?
faq.answer: Kim Gu served in various capacities, including member of the Provisional Assembly, Director of the Police Bureau, Minister of Home Affairs, acting Premier, President (Gungmuryeong), State Councilor, and Chairman of the State Council (Juseok) from 1940 to 1947.

faq.question: What is the most famous independence organization founded by Kim Gu?
faq.answer: In 1931, Kim Gu organized the Korean Patriotic Organization (Hanin Aegukdan) and directed the patriotic deeds of Lee Bong-chang and Yun Bong-gil.

faq.question: When and where did Kim Gu pass away?
faq.answer: Kim Gu was assassinated by Ahn Doo-hee at his residence, Gyeonggyojang, on June 26, 1949.

timeline.event: Born as Siddhartha Gautama in Lumbini (modern-day Nepal) to a royal family.
Renounced his princely life, abandoning his family and kingdom to seek spiritual enlightenment.
Attained supreme enlightenment under the Bodhi tree in Bodh Gaya.
Delivered his first sermon at the Deer Park in Sarnath, setting the Wheel of Dharma in motion.
Passed away (attained Mahaparinirvana) in Kushinagar at the age of 80.
Born on October 15 in Röcken, Saxony, Prussia, as the son of a Lutheran pastor.
His younger sister Elisabeth Nietzsche was born.
His younger brother Ludwig Joseph was born.
His father, Carl Wilhelm Ludwig Nietzsche, passed away from a brain disease.
His younger brother died.
Attended a boys' school.
Started attending the Domgymnasium in Naumburg.
When his grandmother passed away, the family moved into her house.
Enrolled in Schulpforta and continued his studies until 1864.
After graduating from Schulpforta, he began studying theology and ancient philosophy at the University of Bonn.
Became acquainted with Schopenhauer's writings, and in October, moved to the University of Leipzig following Ritschl.
Read Friedrich Albert Lange's book 'History of Materialism and Critique of its Present Importance'.
Volunteered for the army and enlisted in October, and wrote 'The History of the Theognidea'.
Suffered a horseback riding accident in March, making him unable to continue military service, and began meeting the musician Richard Wagner.

Appointed professor of classical philology at the University of Basel in Switzerland, and received his doctorate from the University of Leipzig without an examination or dissertation. Wrote 'Homer and Classical Philology'.

His health deteriorated further, and he wrote 'Analecta Laertiana', 'Der florentinische Tractat über Homer und Hesiod', 'The Greek Music Drama', 'Philosophy in the Tragic Age of the Greeks', and 'Socrates and Tragedy'.
Wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy'.
Began collecting posthumous records such as philosophy appearing in ancient Greek tragedies.
Wrote 'Untimely Meditations'.
Formed a friendship with Paul Rée, and distanced himself from Wagner after being disappointed at the Bayreuth Festival.
Published 'Human, All Too Human'.
Resigned from his professorship at the University of Basel due to deteriorating health.
Planned to travel to Tunis but gave it up, and was inspired by the idea of eternal recurrence in Sils Maria. Wrote 'The Dawn'.
Wrote 'The Gay Science'.
Wrote 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'.
Wrote 'Beyond Good and Evil'.
Wrote 'On the Genealogy of Morality'.
Wrote 'The Case of Wagner', 'Twilight of the Idols', 'The Antichrist', 'Ecce Homo', 'Dionysian-Dithyrambs', and 'Nietzsche contra Wagner'.
Collapsed in Turin, Italy in January and was admitted to a psychiatric hospital, losing his sanity.
Died in Weimar on August 25.
'The Will to Power', an edited version of Nietzsche's posthumous works, was published.
The copyright protection period for Nietzsche's works expired.

keyAchievements.title: Philosopher and Writer
keyAchievements.description: As a German philosopher and writer, he sought to break Western traditions and establish new values.

keyAchievements.title: Earned the Nickname 'Philosopher with a Hammer'
keyAchievements.description: Because he sought to break Western traditions and establish new values, he earned the nickname 'philosopher with a hammer'.

keyAchievements.title: Research on the Origins of Christian Morality and Rationalism
keyAchievements.description: Devoted himself to uncovering the origins of Christian morality and rationalism, arguing that rational things originated from irrationality and madness.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated 'Loving Life on Earth'
keyAchievements.description: Opposed idealistic dichotomy and advocated for loving life in reality.

keyAchievements.title: Critique of Totalitarianism, Chauvinism, and Anti-Semitism
keyAchievements.description: Spread ideas criticizing totalitarianism, chauvinism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism.

keyAchievements.title: Appointed Professor of Classical Philology at the University of Basel
keyAchievements.description: At age 24, he was appointed professor of classical philology at the University of Basel in Switzerland.

keyAchievements.title: Obtained Doctorate
keyAchievements.description: Received a doctorate from the University of Leipzig in 1869 based solely on his published writings.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy'
keyAchievements.description: In 1872, he wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy', containing an original aesthetic theory about ancient Greek tragedy.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Untimely Meditations'
keyAchievements.description: Between 1873 and 1876, he wrote 'Untimely Meditations', containing cultural criticism that challenged the development of German culture.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'Human, All Too Human'
keyAchievements.description: In 1878, he published 'Human, All Too Human', full of his characteristic aphorisms, beginning his departure from the philosophies of Schopenhauer and Wagner.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Death of God'
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of the 'Death of God', meaning the loss of the highest values created by humans and the advent of nihilism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Monism (Vital Ontology)
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a monism contrary to the dualism of modern philosophy centered on the will to power, breaking away from the distinction between subject and object.

keyAchievements.title: Distinguished Concepts of Nihilism
keyAchievements.description: Divided nihilism into active and passive nihilism, emphasizing the importance of an attitude that creates meaning for oneself.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Idea of 'Eternal Recurrence'
keyAchievements.description: Presented the idea of 'eternal recurrence', inspired in Sils Maria in 1881, as the fundamental thought of 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Übermensch'
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the 'Übermensch', a human type who overcomes and creates by moving beyond modern values, morality, and faith.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Will to Power'
keyAchievements.description: As a concept opposing the instinct of self-preservation, he presented the 'will to power', arguing that the purpose of life is not survival but the exertion of power.

keyAchievements.title: Moral Criticism
keyAchievements.description: Classified morality into 'slave morality' and 'master morality', and criticized morality by examining its genealogy from psychological and axiological perspectives.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions as a Classical Philologist
keyAchievements.description: Influenced modern aesthetic theory based on a deep understanding of ancient Greece and Rome.

keyAchievements.title: Profound Posthumous Influence on European Philosophy and Literature
keyAchievements.description: Though ignored during his lifetime, he influenced numerous thinkers and writers posthumously, including Heidegger, Jaspers, Freud, and Thomas Mann.

keyAchievements.title: Composing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Composed lieder and piano pieces from about age 13 to 20, and left several compositions during his time in Basel.

faq.question: Why was Nietzsche called the 'philosopher with a hammer'?
faq.answer: He earned the nickname 'philosopher with a hammer' because he sought to shatter Western traditions and establish new values.

faq.question: What was the cause of Nietzsche's mental illness?
faq.answer: He suffered a mental breakdown in his later years; rumors that it was due to syphilis are just hearsay, and according to a Telegraph article, it was due to a malignant brain tumor.

faq.question: Was Nietzsche anti-Semitic?
faq.answer: Nietzsche criticized totalitarianism, chauvinism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism, but his book 'The Antichrist' explicitly contains content that provides a basis for hatred of Jews.

faq.question: Is Nietzsche's philosophy related to Nazism?
faq.answer: Nietzsche criticized totalitarianism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism, but after his death, his sister Elisabeth distorted his works to favor the Nazi regime. However, there are also points made that his officially published works were also utilized by the Nazis.

faq.question: What kind of evaluation did Nietzsche receive during his lifetime?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, he was thoroughly ignored by academia, harshly criticized by religious circles and moralists, and suffered from slander that he was corrupting society.

timeline.event: Born in Königsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia).
Appointed Professor of Logic and Metaphysics at the University of Königsberg.
Published his groundbreaking work, 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published 'Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals', introducing the categorical imperative.
Published 'Critique of Practical Reason', focusing on moral philosophy.
Published 'Critique of Judgment', focusing on aesthetics and teleology.
Died in Königsberg.
Obtained a professorship in philosophy at the University of Königsberg and published his inaugural dissertation.
Finished his work as a librarian at the Royal Library.
Published the first edition of the 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published the essays 'Answering the Question: What Is Enlightenment?' and 'Idea for a Universal History with a Cosmopolitan Aim'.
Published 'Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals'.
Published 'Metaphysical Foundations of Natural Science' and was elected rector of the University of Königsberg until 1788.
Published the second edition of the 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published the 'Critique of Practical Reason'.
Published the 'Critique of Judgment'.
Had a conflict with the faculty administration over the publication and censorship of the essay 'On the Radical Evil in Human Nature'.
Published 'Religion within the Boundaries of Mere Reason'.
Published 'Perpetual Peace: A Philosophical Sketch'.
Published 'The Metaphysics of Morals'.
Passed away at 4:00 AM on February 12 at the age of 80.

keyAchievements.title: Critique of Pure Reason
keyAchievements.description: Revolutonized philosophy by attempting to resolve the conflict between rationalism and empiricism through a 'Copernican revolution' in epistemology.

keyAchievements.title: The Categorical Imperative
keyAchievements.description: Formulated the supreme principle of morality: to act only according to a maxim that one can simultaneously will to become a universal law.

keyAchievements.title: Transcendental Idealism
keyAchievements.description: Developed the doctrine that human experience of things is based on the way our minds structure them (phenomena), while things as they are in themselves (noumena) remain unknowable.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Critique of Practical Reason'
keyAchievements.description: Focused on ethics and found the basis of morality in transcendental freedom not governed by causality.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Critique of Judgment'
keyAchievements.description: Showcased the essence of critical philosophy by studying aesthetics, teleology, and more.

keyAchievements.title: Attempted a Copernican Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Pointed out the limits of human reason and attempted a 'Copernican Revolution' by introducing transcendental forms into human cognition.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Categorical Imperative
keyAchievements.description: Valued the motive more than the result of an action, presenting the categorical imperative as a command of duty that must be unconditionally followed in any situation.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed Perpetual Peace
keyAchievements.description: Proposed a League of Nations based on the concept of international law to realize a perpetual peace without wars between countries.

faq.question: What did Kant mean by his 'Copernican revolution'?
faq.answer: Just as Copernicus argued that the sun, not the earth, is the center of the universe, Kant argued that instead of our knowledge conforming to objects, objects conform to our a priori forms of intuition (space and time) and categories of understanding.

faq.question: Is it true he never left his hometown?
faq.answer: Yes, it is a famous biographical fact that Kant never traveled more than 10 miles from his birthplace of Königsberg during his entire 79-year life, yet he became one of the most influential thinkers in global history.

faq.question: What is the categorical imperative in simple terms?
faq.answer: It is a moral rule stating that you should only take an action if you would be okay with everyone else in the world taking that same action in the same situation. It is essentially an ultimate standard of fairness and duty.

faq.question: What is unique about Kant's life?
faq.answer: Kant never traveled more than 100 miles away from Königsberg in his entire life and lived a highly regular daily routine.

timeline.event: Born on March 31 to a noble family of magistrates in La Haye en Touraine.
Entered the Jesuit-run Collège Royal Henry-Le-Grand at La Flèche and received education until 1614.
Obtained a Licence in law from the University of Poitiers.
Heard a difficult geometry problem from a Dutch university principal in Paris and solved it.
Traveled to the Netherlands and met Isaac Beeckman, and wrote his first short work, 'Compendium Musicæ'.
Enlisted in the army of Maximilian I of Bavaria, experienced the Battle of White Mountain, and had a dream in a barrack near Ulm that illuminated his life's path.
Discharged from the army and returned to France.
Began researching mathematics and natural sciences, especially optics, in Paris.
Wrote the unfinished treatise 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind'.
Wrote the fragment 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind', and moved to the Netherlands that winter seeking freedom for research and contemplation.
Wrote 'The World' (Le Monde), a book on natural science.
Published 'Discourse on the Method', anonymously appending three parts: Dioptrics, Meteorology, and Geometry.
Published 'Meditations on First Philosophy'.
Systematically organized his philosophy and published 'Principles of Philosophy' in Latin.

Letters sent to Elisabeth of the Palatinate were collected and published as his last book 'The Passions of the Soul', and he went to the Swedish royal palace at the invitation of Queen Christina of Sweden.
Caught pneumonia and passed away at the age of 54 in Stockholm on February 11.
His remains returned to Paris and were placed in the Abbey of Saint Genevieve-du-Mont.
The French government moved his remains to the Panthéon.
His remains were moved again and placed in the Abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Foundation of Modern Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytic Geometry
keyAchievements.description: Devised the Cartesian coordinate system and founded analytic geometry, systematically integrating algebra and geometry.

keyAchievements.title: Established 'I think, therefore I am'
keyAchievements.description: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', he first established the fundamental principle of Enlightenment thought regarding the 'autonomous and rational subject' with 'I think, therefore I am'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Mind-Body Dualism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a dualism separating mind and matter, body and spirit, connecting the scientific view of nature with the metaphysics of the mind.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered the Law of Refraction of Light
keyAchievements.description: Through optical research, he independently proved the law of refraction of light, also known as 'Snell's Law'.

faq.question: Why is Descartes called the 'father of modern philosophy'?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought, and he opened the path for modern mathematics by founding analytic geometry.

faq.question: What is Descartes' most famous proposition?
faq.answer: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', 'I think, therefore I am (Cogito, ergo sum)' is famous for first establishing the fundamental principle of the Enlightenment's 'autonomous and rational subject'.

faq.question: What were Descartes' religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He maintained his Roman Catholic faith throughout his life and claimed that one of the purposes of 'Meditations on First Philosophy' was to defend the Christian faith.

faq.question: What is Descartes' dualism?
faq.answer: He divided substance into two types: mental ('thinking thing') and material ('extended thing'), arguing that mind and body are two distinctly separate substances.

timeline.event: Born in Freiberg in Mähren, Moravia, Austrian Empire.
Acknowledged the lack of results in his research on eel reproductive organs in his first paper 'On the Testes of the Eel'.
