Received his medical degree with the thesis 'On the Spinal Cord of Lower Fish Species'.
Stayed in Paris for collaborative research with Jean-Martin Charcot.
Married Martha Bernays and opened his own neurology clinic.
Published 'Studies on Hysteria' co-authored with Josef Breuer.
Published 'The Interpretation of Dreams'.
First detected abnormalities in his oral cavity.
A tumor was first discovered in his oral cavity.
Received the Goethe Prize in recognition of his contributions to psychology and German literature.
The Nazis took power in Germany, and many of his books were burned.
Fled to London, England after Nazi Germany's annexation of Austria.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Psychoanalysis
keyAchievements.description: As an Austrian psychologist, he founded the discipline of psychoanalysis.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Unconscious and Repression
keyAchievements.description: Invented theories on the unconscious and the defense mechanism of repression.

keyAchievements.title: Psychoanalytic Clinical Treatment
keyAchievements.description: Invented clinical treatment methods for treating mental pathologies through dialogue between the patient and the psychoanalyst.

keyAchievements.title: Redefinition of Sexuality
keyAchievements.description: Redefined sexuality as a primary motivating energy in human life.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Transference
keyAchievements.description: Invented the theory of emotional transference in the therapeutic relationship.

keyAchievements.title: Dream Interpretation Techniques
keyAchievements.description: Invented techniques for observing 'unconscious desires' through dreams.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Mental Structure
keyAchievements.description: Stated that the mind consists of the conscious, preconscious, and unconscious, and proposed the concepts of the id, ego, and superego.

keyAchievements.title: Instinct Theory
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the instinct towards life (Libido) and the drive towards death (Thanatos).

faq.question: Who is Sigmund Freud?
faq.answer: Sigmund Freud was an Austrian psychologist and the founder of psychoanalysis.

faq.question: What are the main theories created by Freud?
faq.answer: He created theories on the unconscious and the defense mechanism of repression, psychoanalytic clinical treatment methods, and the theory defining sexuality as the main motivating energy.

faq.question: How did Freud explain the structure of the mind?
faq.answer: Freud stated that the mind consists of the conscious, preconscious, and unconscious, and he proposed the concepts of the id, ego, and superego.

faq.question: When did Freud die?
faq.answer: Sigmund Freud died on September 23, 1939.

timeline.event: Born in Switzerland.
His father, Paul Jung, was appointed to a larger parish in Laufen (when Jung was 6).
Met Sigmund Freud for the first time in Vienna.
Began developing his analytical psychology theories independently.
Resigned from the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Served as president of the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Published the book 'Psychology of the Unconscious'.
Published the book 'A Psychological Approach to the Dogma of the Trinity'.
Published the book 'Transformation Symbolism in the Mass'.
Published the book 'Answer to Job'.
Passed away at the age of 85.
The book 'Memories, Dreams, Reflections' was published (posthumous publication).

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytical Psychology
keyAchievements.description: As a Swiss psychiatrist, he pursued an independent path and pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Concept of Complex
keyAchievements.description: Used the word 'complex' to describe specific states and laid the foundation for the related theories.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Concept of the Collective Unconscious
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of the collective unconscious, a repository of mental materials that are part of the personal unconscious but shared through the history and culture of humanity rather than personal experience.

keyAchievements.title: Personality Classification (Introversion/Extraversion)
keyAchievements.description: Classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.

keyAchievements.title: Developed the Concept of Individuation
keyAchievements.description: Developed and theorized psychotherapy, viewing that individuals undergoing psychotherapy could advance into whole persons through the process of 'individuation'.

keyAchievements.title: Asserted the Self Archetype
keyAchievements.description: Asserted that the self archetype, which allows the unconscious within humans to realize a unified whole, possesses a transcendent function.

keyAchievements.title: Concepts of Anima and Animus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concepts of anima and animus, which represent the opposite gender in a person's psyche.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Archetype
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of the archetype, a term borrowed from anthropology meaning universal human mental images or themes.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Persona
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of persona, an aspect of personality that appears for adaptation or personal convenience.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Shadow
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of the shadow, which includes repressed and poorly known aspects of the personality often considered negative.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Self
keyAchievements.description: Viewed the 'self', a central concept governing the individuation process and symbolized by a mandala that unifies the feminine and masculine, as the main archetype of the psyche.

faq.question: Who is Carl Jung?
faq.answer: Carl Jung was a Swiss psychiatrist, the founder of analytical psychology, who established the concepts of the complex and collective unconscious, and classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.

faq.question: Why did Carl Jung and Freud part ways?
faq.answer: Freud restricted the concept of 'libido' to sexuality, while Jung saw it more broadly as general energy, which led to a divergence of views and their split.

faq.question: What is the field of psychology founded by Carl Jung?
faq.answer: Carl Jung pursued his own path and pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.

faq.question: How did Carl Jung speak about God?
faq.answer: He mentioned about God in a broadcast interview, saying, "I do not believe in Him, I know Him."

timeline.event: Born to a family of Portuguese-Jewish descent in Amsterdam, Netherlands.
When his father died, he took over the import goods shop run by his father at the age of 21.
Was excommunicated and cursed from the Jewish community on the pretext of criticizing Judaism and blaspheming God.
Wrote 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect' in Rijnsburg and published 'Principles of Cartesian Philosophy'.
Formed a relationship with Johan de Witt, a political leader of the Dutch Republic, in Voorburg.
Published 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' anonymously and settled in The Hague that year.
'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' was banned from publication by the Dutch government.
Began writing 'Tractatus Politicus'.
Passed away from tuberculosis at the age of 44.
Completed his lifelong work 'Ethics' but could not publish it during his lifetime; the first edition of 'Tractatus Politicus' was published.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote the 'Ethics'
keyAchievements.description: Partially accepting Descartes' foundationalism while adopting a philosophical stance against mind-body dualism in his masterpiece 'Ethics', which significantly influenced Western philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Unified Natural Substance and Moral Essence
keyAchievements.description: Revived the ideas of the ancient Stoic school in a modern sense, undertaking the philosophical task of unifying natural substance and moral essence.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of Conatus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire or endeavor to preserve, develop, and perfect the self, and saw its full expression as happiness.

keyAchievements.title: Established Monistic Pantheism
keyAchievements.description: Viewed God and Nature as two names for the same substance, establishing a monistic pantheistic worldview with strong atheistic tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Modern Rationalist Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Recognized as one of the major rationalist philosophers of 17th-century Europe who laid the foundation for the 18th-century Enlightenment and modern biblical criticism.

faq.question: How was Spinoza treated in the Jewish community?
faq.answer: Although well-versed in Jewish culture, he was expelled from the Jewish community at age 23 due to his critical ideas, and all his works were placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's main philosophical ideas?
faq.answer: He argued for monistic pantheism, viewing God and nature as two names for the same substance, and presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire to preserve, develop, and perfect the self.

faq.question: How did Spinoza make a living?
faq.answer: He barely made a living by grinding optical lenses and rejected any compensation or honors, including professorships, during his lifetime.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's representative works?
faq.answer: His lifelong masterpiece is 'Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order' ('Ethics'), and others include 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' and 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect'.

faq.question: Is the quote 'Even if the world were to end tomorrow, I would plant an apple tree today' by Spinoza?
faq.answer: These words were spoken by Martin Luther, and it is explicitly stated in the original text that it is unclear whether Spinoza actually said it.

timeline.event: Birth of Sun Tzu (Estimated)
Became a military strategist for King Helü of Wu upon the recommendation of Wu Zixu
Launched an expedition against Chu with Helü and Wu Zixu as commanders, capturing the Chu capital, Ying
Despite Sun Tzu's opposition, Helü attacked Yue, was defeated, and died
Death of Sun Tzu (Estimated)

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Art of War
keyAchievements.description: As a strategist of the Spring and Autumn period, he authored the 13 chapters of 'The Art of War'.

keyAchievements.title: Appointed Military Strategist of Wu
keyAchievements.description: Became the military strategist for King Helü of Wu upon the recommendation of Wu Zixu and trained the army.

keyAchievements.title: Victorious Expedition against Chu
keyAchievements.description: In 506 BC, contributed to the capture of the capital Ying by commanding the expedition against Chu with Helü.

keyAchievements.title: Assisted in the Great Victory over Yue
keyAchievements.description: Assisted Helü's successor, Fuchai, in building national strength and then attacking Yue, contributing to a great victory.

faq.question: Who is Sun Tzu?
faq.answer: Sun Tzu was a strategist of the Spring and Autumn period, whose courtesy name was Changqing, better known by the honorific Sun Tzu. He wrote 'The Art of War'.

faq.question: Are the records of Sun Tzu's life clear?
faq.answer: There are important records like Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian, but his life is a subject of debate, and his life after retirement is unknown.

faq.question: How did Sun Tzu become a military strategist for Wu?
faq.answer: He fled to Wu with his clan, got to know the chancellor Wu Zixu, and was invited by King Helü of Wu to become a military strategist upon Wu Zixu's recommendation.

faq.question: What is Sun Tzu's representative work?
faq.answer: Sun Tzu's representative work is 'The Art of War', which was treated as an important military text in the Sinosphere for a long time.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a country lawyer in Wrington, Somerset.
Entered Westminster School and graduated with excellent grades.
Entered Christ Church, Oxford, as a scholar and encountered Descartes' philosophy.
Received his Bachelor of Arts.
Began working as a tutor at Oxford University for 5 years.
Began studying natural sciences and medicine.
While staying in Brandenburg, Germany as an embassy secretary, witnessed the possibility of religious tolerance.
Appointed secretary to the Board of Trade and Plantations.
Began writing his major work, 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding'.
Returned to England.
Fled to the Netherlands to escape the Royalists.
Returned to England following the accession of William III through the Glorious Revolution.
Became Commissioner of Appeals and became famous by publishing 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding', which he wrote while in exile.
Retired to Essex.
Died in Essex.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of British Empiricism
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as the first full-fledged British empiricist philosopher.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Social Contract Theory
keyAchievements.description: Argued that the state emerged through a social contract to ensure the security of natural rights.

keyAchievements.title: Enlightenment and Liberal Thinker
keyAchievements.description: Widely known as one of the most influential Enlightenment thinkers and liberal theorists.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of the Concept of 'Self-Identity'
keyAchievements.description: The first philosopher to define the self through the continuity of 'consciousness'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated the 'Blank Slate' (Tabula Rasa)
keyAchievements.description: Argued that people are born without innate ideas, viewing the mind as a 'blank slate'.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Night-Watchman State Theory
keyAchievements.description: Argued for a night-watchman state, stating that the state's duty lies in ensuring a minimum level of security.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundation for Popular Sovereignty
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for popular sovereignty by arguing that the people entrust power to the state through a contract.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Constitutional Democracy and Natural Rights
keyAchievements.description: The first person to advocate for constitutional democracy and natural rights.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundation for the Separation of Powers
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for the separation of powers among the legislative, judicial, and executive branches.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding'
keyAchievements.description: His major work dealing with the problem of cognition, including modern science.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'The Reasonableness of Christianity'
keyAchievements.description: A text written as a response to John Toland's draft.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'A Letter Concerning Toleration'
keyAchievements.description: Argued that 'toleration is the most certain mark of the true church'.

faq.question: What kind of philosopher is John Locke considered to be?
faq.answer: Locke is evaluated as the first full-fledged British empiricist philosopher, and is known as one of the most influential Enlightenment thinkers and liberal theorists.

faq.question: What are John Locke's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding', 'Two Treatises of Government', 'The Reasonableness of Christianity', and 'A Letter Concerning Toleration'.

faq.question: Who did John Locke's ideas influence?
faq.answer: Locke's ideas influenced Voltaire, Rousseau, the American Revolution, and Scottish Enlightenment thinkers, and are also reflected in the United States Declaration of Independence.

faq.question: What is the 'blank slate' advocated by John Locke?
faq.answer: Locke viewed the mind as a 'blank slate' (tabula rasa), arguing that people are born without innate ideas.

faq.question: What does John Locke's social contract theory argue?
faq.answer: Locke argued that the state emerged through a social contract to ensure the safety of natural rights, including holding one's own property through labor in a peaceful state of nature.

faq.question: What was John Locke's stance on toleration?
faq.answer: Locke argued that 'toleration is the most certain mark of the true church', but he showed a contradictory attitude by excluding Roman Catholics or atheists from being objects of toleration.

faq.question: What was John Locke's stance on slavery?
faq.answer: Locke showed a contradictory attitude by opposing slavery in England while accepting the ownership of black slaves by English people in the American colonies.

timeline.event: Born in Linden, Germany.
Publishes dissertation on the concept of love.
Barred from obtaining a habilitation due to being Jewish and flees from Germany to Paris.
Marries Heinrich Blücher.
Flees to the United States with her husband and mother.
Becomes a naturalized US citizen.
Publishes The Origins of Totalitarianism.
Publishes Rahel Varnhagen: The Life of a Jewess and The Human Condition.
Becomes the first woman to be appointed to a full professorship at Princeton University.
Publishes Between Past and Future.
Publishes Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil and On Revolution.
Publishes Men in Dark Times.
Publishes Crises of the Republic: Lying in Politics, Civil Disobedience, On Violence.
Publishes the anthology Crises of the Republic.
Dies.
Her unfinished posthumous work, The Life of the Mind, is published.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing Identity as a Political Theorist
keyAchievements.description: Described herself as a political theorist rather than a philosopher, leaving achievements regarding humanity's habitation of the world.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Power, Politics, Authority, and Totalitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Left significant works on the nature of power and topics such as politics, authority, and totalitarianism.

keyAchievements.title: Theorizing the Concept of Freedom
keyAchievements.description: Theorized freedom as public and associative, focusing on affirming freedom as collective political action.

keyAchievements.title: Introducing the Concept of the 'Banality of Evil'
keyAchievements.description: In her report on the Eichmann trial, she proposed the conclusion that sheer thoughtlessness ultimately begets the banality of evil.

keyAchievements.title: Writing The Origins of Totalitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a book tracing the roots of Communism and Nazism and their connection to antisemitism.

keyAchievements.title: Writing On Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Compared the American Revolution and the French Revolution, arguing for the success of the American Revolution and the disaster of the French Revolution.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Theory of Power
keyAchievements.description: Defined power as something that 'cannot be the property of an individual, and exists only as long as the group remains together', strictly contrasting it with violence.

faq.question: How did Hannah Arendt define herself?
faq.answer: Arendt herself rejected the title of philosopher on the grounds that philosophy focuses on 'man in the singular', instead describing herself as a political theorist.

faq.question: What is the 'banality of evil' proposed by Arendt?
faq.answer: In her report on the Eichmann trial, Arendt concluded that sheer thoughtlessness ultimately gives rise to the banality of evil.

faq.question: What is Arendt's concept of power?
faq.answer: Arendt defined power as something that 'cannot be the property of an individual, and exists only as long as the group remains together', and strictly contrasted it with violence.

faq.question: How did Arendt evaluate the American and French Revolutions?
faq.answer: Arendt argued that the well-studied and often imitated French Revolution was a disaster, while the largely ignored American Revolution was a success.

timeline.event: Born in Woolsthorpe, Lincolnshire, England; his father, Isaac Newton Sr., died in October 1642, before he was born.
When Newton was three, his mother, Hannah Ayscough, remarried, and he was left in the care of his maternal grandmother.
His mother returned after his stepfather died.
Sent to The King's School in Grantham to begin classical education; boarded with an apothecary named Mr. Clark, where he showed an interest in chemistry.
His mother called him back to Woolsthorpe to teach him farming.
Admitted to Trinity College, Cambridge.
Generalized the binomial theorem; when Cambridge University closed due to the Great Plague, he returned home for two years.
Returned to Cambridge University when it reopened and earned his Master's degree.
Built the reflecting telescope.
Succeeded Professor Isaac Barrow as Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge University and began his research on calculus.
Elected a Fellow of the Royal Society, and developed his theory of light based on his observation of a prism separating white light into the visible spectrum.
Discovered the interference phenomenon in thin films.
Published Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica, presenting the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation, and proving the three laws of motion.
Served as a Member of Parliament.
Served as the Master of the Royal Mint.
Became President of the Royal Society of London following the death of Robert Hooke.
Knighted by Queen Anne.
Devised an experiment to verify the existence of the aether.
Died in his sleep at the age of 84, and his remains were interred in Westminster Abbey.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Calculus
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to calculus alongside Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, and created Newton's method for finding the roots of a function.

keyAchievements.title: Generalization of the Binomial Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved the generalized binomial theorem and contributed to the study of power series.

keyAchievements.title: Classical Mechanics and Universal Gravitation
keyAchievements.description: Provided the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation and formulated Newton's laws of motion in Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Reflecting Telescope
keyAchievements.description: Built the first practical reflecting telescope.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Light
keyAchievements.description: Developed the theory of light based on his observation of a prism separating white light into the visible spectrum.

keyAchievements.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
keyAchievements.description: Formulated Newton's law of cooling through experiments, studied the speed of sound, and introduced the concept of a Newtonian fluid.

keyAchievements.title: Inverse-Square Law of Gravity
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the inverse-square law of gravity, which states that the gravitational attraction between two bodies is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

faq.question: Who was Isaac Newton?
faq.answer: Isaac Newton was an English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer.

faq.question: What is Newton's most influential book?
faq.answer: Published in 1687, Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica established the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation, and is considered one of the most influential books in the history of science.

faq.question: How did Newton contribute to calculus?
faq.answer: He contributed to calculus alongside Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz and discovered Newton's method for approximating the roots of a function.

faq.question: What are Newton's laws of motion?
faq.answer: Newton's laws of motion consist of three laws: the law of inertia, the law of acceleration, and the law of action and reaction.

faq.question: How did Newton explain gravity?
faq.answer: He revealed the inverse-square law of gravity and explained the law of universal gravitation, showing that the force that pulls an apple to the ground is the same force that keeps the moon in its orbit.

faq.question: Did Newton ever marry?
faq.answer: He was engaged in his youth, but he was so devoted to his research and work that it did not lead to marriage, and he remained single his entire life.

faq.question: What were Newton's religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He rejected the Trinity but was deeply religious; he believed in a single Creator and reportedly spent a great deal of time on biblical interpretation.

faq.question: What public offices did Newton hold?
faq.answer: He held public roles as a Member of Parliament, Master of the Royal Mint, and President of the Royal Society of London.

timeline.event: Born in Shrewsbury, England.
Receives primary and secondary education.
Begins studying at Shrewsbury School.
Begins studying medicine at the University of Edinburgh.
Leaves the University of Edinburgh and enrolls in Christ's College, Cambridge to study theology.
Graduates from Cambridge with top honors and boards the HMS Beagle on December 27.
Completes the Beagle expedition.
The Beagle arrives in Falmouth, Cornwall.
His first paper on the uplift of the South American continent is read to the Geological Society of London, and he presents mammal and bird specimens to the Zoological Society.
Elected to the Council of the Geological Society.
Moves to London to join Charles Lyell's scientific circles.
Ponders 'the possibility of one species changing into another' in his Red Notebook.
Elected Secretary of the Geological Society.
Marries Emma Wedgwood and publishes The Voyage of the Beagle.
Retires to the outskirts of London to collect and organize data on evolution.
Publishes The Structure and Distribution of Coral Reefs, his first biological monograph.
Writes a 35-page pencil sketch of his theory of evolution by natural selection.
Publishes Geological Observations on the Volcanic Islands.
Publishes Geological Observations on South America based on his Beagle expedition data.
Begins writing his theory of evolution.
Presents his theory alongside a paper by Alfred Russel Wallace at the Linnean Society.
Publishes On the Origin of Species, establishing the fact of biological evolution and 'natural selection'.
Publishes The Descent of Man, which deals with human evolution.
Publishes The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals.
Publishes The Formation of Vegetable Mould through the Action of Worms.
Dies at the age of 73 and is buried in Westminster Abbey.

keyAchievements.title: Greatest Contribution to the Theory of Evolution
keyAchievements.description: As an English biologist and geologist, made the greatest contribution to evolutionary theory.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Natural Selection Theory
keyAchievements.description: Introduced natural selection occurring through the struggle for survival in a joint paper with Alfred Russel Wallace.

keyAchievements.title: Writing On the Origin of Species and Presenting Evolutionary Theory
keyAchievements.description: Wrote On the Origin of Species in 1859, presenting the theory of evolution with compelling evidence.

keyAchievements.title: Providing a Unifying Theory of the Life Sciences
keyAchievements.description: Darwin's scientific discoveries form a unifying theory in the life sciences, explaining the diversity of life.

keyAchievements.title: Challenging Creationism
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the theory of biological evolution in On the Origin of Species, overturning the dominant creationist view of the time and opening a new era.

keyAchievements.title: Conviction in Biological Evolution Through the Beagle Voyage
keyAchievements.description: Became convinced of biological evolution after meticulously observing geology, flora, and fauna during the Beagle expedition.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Major Works
keyAchievements.description: Left numerous academic works including The Structure and Distribution of Coral Reefs (1842), Geological Observations on the Volcanic Islands (1844), Geological Observations on South America (1846), and The Descent of Man (1871).

faq.question: When was Charles Darwin born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Charles Darwin was born on February 12, 1809, and died on April 19, 1882.

faq.question: What is Charles Darwin's most important achievement?
faq.answer: Charles Darwin made the greatest contribution to the theory of evolution and established natural selection with the publication of On the Origin of Species in 1859.

faq.question: What was Charles Darwin's stance on slavery?
faq.answer: Charles Darwin opposed slavery, having been outraged by the white enslavement of black people during his travels in South America.

faq.question: What was the purpose of Charles Darwin boarding the Beagle?
faq.answer: Initially, he boarded as a civilian companion to satisfy the captain's intellectual desire for association with a gentleman, but he later acted as a naturalist as his exceptional skills in natural history were recognized.

faq.question: Is the rumor true that Charles Darwin changed his religious beliefs in his later years?
faq.answer: The rumor that Darwin accepted the biblical creation story as truth and recovered his faith in Jesus Christ before he died is false.

timeline.event: Michelangelo di Lodovico Buonarroti Simoni is born.
Enters Domenico Ghirlandaio's workshop as an apprentice.
Completes the Pietà, commissioned by French Cardinal Jean de Bilhères.
Begins sculpting David, commissioned by the city of Florence.
Invited to Rome by Pope Julius II and commissioned to build the Pope's tomb.
Commissioned to paint the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel in the Apostolic Palace and begins the work.
Completes the Sistine Chapel ceiling.
Leaves Florence permanently and settles in Rome.
Commissioned by Pope Paul III to paint The Last Judgment on the altar wall of the Sistine Chapel.
Completes The Last Judgment.
Completes the statue of Moses for the tomb of Julius II.
Completes the fresco The Conversion of Saul in the Pauline Chapel, Vatican.
Completes the fresco The Crucifixion of St. Peter in the Pauline Chapel, Vatican.
Dies in Rome at the age of 89.

keyAchievements.title: Pietà
keyAchievements.description: A masterpiece of Renaissance sculptural art completed in Rome in 1499, commissioned by the French Cardinal Jean de Bilhères.

keyAchievements.title: David
keyAchievements.description: A statue of the young David, commissioned by the city of Florence in 1501 and completed over three years.

keyAchievements.title: Sistine Chapel Ceiling
keyAchievements.description: The ceiling fresco of the Apostolic Palace, commissioned by Pope Julius II in 1508 and completed in four years, depicting much of Catholic doctrine including the Creation.

keyAchievements.title: The Last Judgment
keyAchievements.description: The large altar wall fresco in the Sistine Chapel, commissioned by Pope Paul III in 1535 and completed over six years.

keyAchievements.title: Tomb of Julius II
keyAchievements.description: A funerary monument commissioned by Pope Julius II in 1505 and completed over 40 years, most famous for its statue of Moses.

faq.question: In what fields of art was Michelangelo active?
faq.answer: He was an Italian sculptor, painter, architect, and poet, a master who represented the Renaissance period.

faq.question: What are Michelangelo's representative sculptural works?
faq.answer: His representative sculptures include the Pietà completed in 1499 and the David completed over three years beginning in 1501.

faq.question: Who painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling?
faq.answer: Michelangelo painted it, completing it in four years after being commissioned by Pope Julius II in 1508.

faq.question: Where is Michelangelo's The Last Judgment located?
faq.answer: This work, commissioned by Pope Paul III in 1535 and completed over six years, is the large altar wall fresco in the Sistine Chapel.

timeline.event: Claude Monet is born on November 14 in the 9th arrondissement of Paris.
Baptized Oscar-Claude on May 20 at the Notre-Dame de Lorette.
His family moves to Le Havre in Normandy.
Enrolls in the Le Havre secondary school of the arts on April 1.
His mother dies.
Meets fellow painter Eugène Boudin.
Arrives in Paris in April and settles at the Hotel 'du Nouveau Monde'.
Enrolls in the Académie Suisse, run by Charles Suisse, early in the year (around February) and meets Camille Pissarro.
Drafted into the army in Le Havre on March 2 and joins the First Regiment of African Light Cavalry on April 29.
Contracts typhoid fever in the spring, returns to Le Havre to recover, and meets Johan Jongkind in the summer.
Returns to Paris in December, enrolls in the studio of Charles Gleyre, and meets Auguste Renoir and Frédéric Bazille.
Successfully debuts at the Salon with The Point of la Hève at Low Tide and Mouth of the Seine at Honfleur.
Submits The Woman in the Green Dress and Pavé de Chailly to the Salon and is accepted.
His then-mistress Camille Doncieux gives birth to their first son, Jean.
Marries Camille on June 28, and takes refuge in London and the Netherlands just before the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War.
His father dies, and the family moves to Argenteuil.
Moves to the 'pink house with green shutters' in Argenteuil, and exhibits Impression, Sunrise at the First Impressionist Exhibition.
Paints Woman with a Parasol - Madame Monet and Her Son.
Camille Monet falls seriously ill; another exhibition is organized in opposition to the Salon.
Exhibits seven paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare at the Third Impressionist Exhibition on April 5.
His second son Michel is born, the family moves to the village of Vétheuil, and Camille is diagnosed with uterine cancer.
Camille dies.
Submits two paintings to the Salon, and one is accepted.
Moves to Poissy in December with Alice Hoschedé, the wife of Ernest Hoschedé, and her children.
Participates in his last, the Seventh Impressionist Exhibition.
Moves to a rented house in Giverny with the children on April 29, and his first major retrospective is held.
Goes on a short painting trip to the Italian Riviera and Genoa with Auguste Renoir in December.
Stays in Bordighera from January 18 to April 5, producing 38 paintings.
Travels to the Netherlands to paint tulips.
Meets John Singer Sargent in the summer.
Purchases the house in Giverny.
15 paintings of the Haystacks series are exhibited at the Galerie Durand-Ruel.
Paints 26 views of Rouen Cathedral.
Exhibits 20 paintings of Rouen Cathedral.
61 paintings are exhibited at the Galerie Georges Petit.
Begins painting water lilies, and stays in London for six weeks.
Exhibits the first garden paintings, primarily focusing on the Japanese bridge.
Expands the pond at his home; in February, an exhibition of the Water Lilies series in New York City achieves great success.
Increases the size of the water garden to nearly 4,000 square meters.
The series 'Views of the Thames in London - 1900-1904' is exhibited in May and June.
Spends the autumn at the Palazzo Barbaro in Venice with Alice.
An exhibition titled 'Water Lilies' opens on May 6, featuring 48 paintings.
His second wife Alice dies.
Travels to London to consult with the German ophthalmologist Richard Liebreich.
His eldest son Jean dies.
Begins work on a series of decorative paintings for the French government.
Begins a series of landscape paintings 'at full strength'.
Prescription for mydriatic drops temporarily alleviates his vision problems.
Undergoes cataract surgery.
With improved vision, begins retouching some of the works produced before surgery.
Dies of lung cancer at the age of 86 on December 5 and is buried in the Giverny church cemetery.
27 panel paintings are exhibited at the Musée de l'Orangerie in May.
André Masson's essay helps shift the perception of Monet's paintings.
Water Lily paintings held by the Museum of Modern Art in New York are destroyed in a fire.
Monet's house, garden, and water lily pond are bequeathed by Michel to the French Academy of Fine Arts.
Monet's garden is restored and opened to the public.
A Google Doodle is created on November 14 to commemorate Claude Monet's 161st birthday.
The Houses of Parliament, Sunlight Effect in the Fog is sold for $20.1 million.
A paper is published in the Proceedings of the Royal Society identifying the locations where the London paintings were created.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is stolen for the second time in August.
The Railway Bridge at Argenteuil is sold for $41.4 million on May 6.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is recovered in June.
Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas is sold for £40,921,250 (approx. $80,451,178) on June 24.
The Church at Vétheuil and Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas become the subject of a court case in New York in October.
A Monet painting formerly owned by a Jewish retailer is discovered in the suitcase of Cornelius Gurlitt.
The Nazi-looted artwork On the Seashore is restituted to the granddaughters of the Parlagi couple.

keyAchievements.title: Founding and Pioneering Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'father of Impressionism', he is considered a founder and pioneer of the movement and a key precursor to Modernism.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of the Term Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

keyAchievements.title: Consistent Practice of En Plein Air Landscape Painting
keyAchievements.description: As the most consistent practitioner of the Impressionist philosophy over a long career, he expressed his perception of nature in plein air landscape paintings.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering the Series Method
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered the series method of painting the same scene many times to capture changes in light and the passing of seasons, with major works including the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and Water Lilies series.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Giverny Garden
keyAchievements.description: Purchased a house and land in Giverny, northern France, and began a vast landscaping project including a water lily pond, which became the greatest source of inspiration for his art.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration for 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

faq.question: Who was Claude Monet?
faq.answer: Claude Monet was a French Impressionist painter, a founder and pioneer of the Impressionist movement, often referred to as the 'father of Impressionism'.

faq.question: Where did the term 'Impressionism' originate?
faq.answer: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

faq.question: What are Monet's representative series paintings?
faq.answer: His representative series include the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and the Water Lilies, which captivated him for the last 20 years of his life.

faq.question: Where was Monet primarily active?
faq.answer: He lived in Giverny, northern France, from 1883, where he purchased a house and land and began a vast landscaping project that included his water lily pond.

faq.question: How did Monet's eyesight affect his work?
faq.answer: Cataracts made color perception increasingly difficult, leading his paintings to become broader and more abstract; after surgery, his paintings tended to have stronger blue tones than before.

faq.question: How did Monet's work influence modern art?
faq.answer: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration to 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

timeline.event: Born in Moscow as the second of seven children to Mikhail Andreyevich and Maria Fyodorovna.
Enrolls with his older brother Mikhail at the Chermak boarding school in Moscow and studies there for three years.
Mother Maria Fyodorovna dies of tuberculosis; sent by his father with his brother Mikhail to St. Petersburg to enter the Military Engineering-Technical University.
Passes the entrance exam for the Military Engineering-Technical University and undergoes military education.
His father is murdered by serfs on his estate.
Graduates from the military engineering school and is commissioned as a sub-lieutenant in the St. Petersburg Engineering Command.
Resigns his commission to become a writer and devote himself to literature.
Debuts in the literary world with his first work, Poor Folk, receiving high praise from critic Vissarion Belinsky.
Arrested by authorities after joining Mikhail Petrashevsky's circle and reading Belinsky's Letter to Gogol.
Completes a four-year prison sentence in a katorga in Omsk.
Marries Maria Isayeva.
Returns to St. Petersburg.
Publishes the magazine Vremya (Time) with his brother, serializing The Insulted and Humiliated and The House of the Dead.
Founds a new magazine, Epokha (Epoch), with his brother, but it fails.
Completes his masterpiece, Crime and Punishment.
Writes The Idiot and Demons while living abroad (especially in Dresden) before returning home.
Publishes The Adolescent, earning a substantial sum that lifts him out of poverty.
Completes his final masterpiece, the novel The Brothers Karamazov.
Dies of a ruptured pulmonary artery at the age of 60.
His remains are interred in the Alexander Nevsky Lavra cemetery in St. Petersburg.

keyAchievements.title: Poor Folk
keyAchievements.description: Debuted spectacularly in the literary world in 1846 with his first work, receiving high praise from critic Vissarion Belinsky as 'the next Gogol'.

keyAchievements.title: The House of the Dead
keyAchievements.description: A work published based on his experiences during his exile in Siberia.

keyAchievements.title: Crime and Punishment
keyAchievements.description: Completed this work, considered one of his masterpieces, in 1866.

keyAchievements.title: The Adolescent
keyAchievements.description: Published this work in 1874, earning a substantial amount of money that allowed him to escape poverty.

keyAchievements.title: The Brothers Karamazov
keyAchievements.description: Completed the manuscript for this novel, widely considered his final masterpiece, in 1880.

keyAchievements.title: A Writer's Diary
keyAchievements.description: Included literary horizons, political and social criticism, essays, and short stories, later becoming invaluable documentary material for Dostoevsky studies.

faq.question: What ideological changes did Dostoevsky undergo?
faq.answer: Through deep reading of the Bible and his encounters with the lives of convicts and commoners during his Siberian exile, he underwent an ideological shift from a socialist to a Christian humanitarian.

faq.question: What personal experiences influenced Dostoevsky's literary world?
faq.answer: His experience of political persecution ending in a commuted death sentence moments before execution, his exile in Siberia, his chronic epilepsy, and his gambling habit frequently appeared as important elements and heavily influenced his works.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Dostoevsky's novels?
faq.answer: His novels often feature characters with disparate and extreme psychologies, demonstrating an astounding understanding of the human psyche while sharply analyzing the political, social, and spiritual world of contemporary Russia.

faq.question: Is Dostoevsky related to existentialism?
faq.answer: Walter Kaufmann once described Dostoevsky's novella Notes from Underground as the 'best overture for existentialism ever written', and he is sometimes considered one of the founders of existentialism.

timeline.event: Born as the third son of Léopold Hugo in Besançon, eastern France.
Fascinated by reading and writing poetry at the Cordeliers boarding school in Paris, vowing to become a great French writer.
Published his first poetry collection, Odes et poésies diverses, and got married.
Published the novel Han d'Islande.
Awarded the Knight of the Legion of Honour by the French royal family in recognition of his merits as a writer.
Published the poetry collection Odes et Ballades and the novel Bug-Jargal.
Directly opposed classical literature and led romantic writers through the publication of the preface to his play Cromwell.
Published Les Orientales and Le Dernier jour d'un condamné.
The "Battle of Hernani" broke out between the romantic and classical schools around the premiere of the play Hernani.
Published the novel Notre-Dame de Paris, the poetry collection Les Feuilles d'automne, and the play Marion Delorme.
Published the play Le roi s'amuse.
Published the plays Lucrèce Borgia and Marie Tudor.
Published the poetry collection Les Chants du crépuscule.
Published the poetry collection Les Voix intérieures.
Published the verse play Ruy Blas.
Published the poetry collection Les Rayons et les Ombres.
Became a member of the Académie Française.
The play Les Burgraves was severely criticized, and his eldest daughter Léopoldine and her husband died in a boat capsizing accident.
Devoted himself to political activities, barely publishing any works.
Solidified his humanitarian tendencies, stimulated by surrounding circumstances including the February Revolution.
Rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.
Published the poetry collection Les Châtiments.
Published the poetry collection Les Contemplations.
Published the first series of the poetry collection La Légende des siècles.
Published the novel Les Misérables.
Published the novel Les Travailleurs de la mer.
Wrote Les Années funestes, depicting the reality of French workers.
Returned to Paris as a defender of republicanism with the fall of Napoleon III due to the Franco-Prussian War.
Published L'Année terrible.
Published the novel Quatrevingt-treize.
Published the second series of La Légende des siècles.
Wrote his will.
Published the third series of La Légende des siècles.
Died, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon in a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Romantic Literature
keyAchievements.description: He presented the theory of romantic theater in the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Masterpiece Novels
keyAchievements.description: He solidified his position as a novelist with Notre-Dame de Paris and wrote numerous masterpieces including Les Misérables during his exile.

keyAchievements.title: Social Criticism and Humanitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Through his works, he expressed love for poor workers, emphasized popular art, and solidified his humanitarian tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Major Figure in the French Literary World
keyAchievements.description: He occupied an important position in the French literary world, receiving the Knight of the Legion of Honour in 1825 and becoming a member of the Académie Française in 1841.

faq.question: Who was Victor Hugo?
faq.answer: He was a French poet, novelist, and playwright.

faq.question: What impact did Victor Hugo have on romanticism?
faq.answer: He presented the theory of romantic theater through the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

faq.question: What are Victor Hugo's representative works?
faq.answer: They include the novels Notre-Dame de Paris and Les Misérables.

faq.question: Why was Victor Hugo in exile?
faq.answer: He rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état in 1851, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.

faq.question: What was Victor Hugo's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: His works were banned by the Catholic Church, and he could not reconcile with Catholicism, even refusing the church's prayers.

faq.question: When did Victor Hugo die, and where is he buried?
faq.answer: He died on May 22, 1885, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon.

timeline.event: Born as the third son in the Black Sea port city of Taganrog.
Attended a preparatory school in his hometown that taught ancient Greek.
Entered the Taganrog Gymnasium, which aimed for a classical education.
Repeated the third-year course due to poor grades.
Failed his ancient Greek exam and repeated the fifth-year course.
Graduated from the Taganrog school and entered the medical faculty of Moscow University.
Took charge of a column sketching daily life in Moscow every two weeks for the literary magazine Oskolki.
Passed his medical graduation exams and published his first short story collection, The Tales of Melpomene.
The play Ivanov premiered and was a huge success in Moscow and St. Petersburg.
Left Moscow to travel to Siberia and Sakhalin Island.
Bought an estate in the Moscow suburbs, lived with his parents and younger sister, and worked as a doctor.
Published the play The Seagull, marking the beginning of his later dramatic period.
Moved his residence to the Crimean Peninsula facing Yalta due to deteriorating health.
Elected as a member of the Russian Academy.
Married Olga Knipper, an actress of the Moscow Art Theatre.
Resigned his membership in the Academy in protest when the government revoked Maxim Gorky's membership.
Ended his 44-year life due to pulmonary tuberculosis.

keyAchievements.title: Medical Graduation and Activities
keyAchievements.description: Graduating from the medical faculty of Moscow University in 1884, he worked as a doctor, caring for neighboring farmers and building schools in villages.

keyAchievements.title: Reputation as a Short Story Writer
keyAchievements.description: Around his college graduation, he was already highly renowned as a rising novelist, contributing humorous short stories and essays to various magazines and publishing his first short story collection, The Tales of Melpomene.

keyAchievements.title: Success as a Playwright
keyAchievements.description: Starting with the massive success of the play Ivanov, he established a new form of conversational drama through modern masterpieces such as The Seagull, Uncle Vanya, Three Sisters, and The Cherry Orchard.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Activities
keyAchievements.description: He wrote a travelogue recording the miserable lives of prisoners exiled to Sakhalin Island and returned his Russian Academy membership in protest of the government's unfair actions.

faq.question: What was Anton Chekhov's profession?
faq.answer: He was a Russian doctor, short story writer, and playwright.

faq.question: What are Anton Chekhov's representative plays?
faq.answer: His representative plays include The Seagull, Uncle Vanya, Three Sisters, and The Cherry Orchard.

faq.question: When did Anton Chekhov die, and what was the cause of death?
faq.answer: Anton Chekhov died of pulmonary tuberculosis at the age of 44 on July 15, 1904.

faq.question: What activities did Anton Chekhov do as a doctor?
faq.answer: Living in the Moscow suburbs, he served as a doctor by taking care of the health of neighboring farmers and building schools in the village.

timeline.event: Frédéric François Chopin was born.
Chopin's family moved to Warsaw.
Warsaw became part of the constitutional Kingdom of Poland.
Received his first professional music education from Wojciech Żywny.
Began holding public concerts at age 7 and composed two polonaises.
Composed the Polonaise in A-flat major and dedicated it to Żywny.
Performed his own improvisations and part of Moscheles's concerto on the aeolomelodicon.
Performed his Rondo Op. 1 (his first commercially published work) at an aeolopantaleon concert.
Attended the Warsaw Lyceum and studied music theory, figured bass, and composition at the Warsaw Conservatory for 3 years.
Shortly after his youngest sister Emilia died, the family moved to Krakowskie Przedmieście.
Visited Berlin with a family friend.
Composed Souvenir de Paganini after hearing Niccolò Paganini play the violin.
Debuted in Vienna, holding two piano concerts and receiving favorable reviews.
Premiered Piano Concerto No. 2 in F minor, Op. 21.
Left Warsaw.
The November Uprising broke out in Warsaw.
Expressed anguish in his diary upon hearing news of the uprising's suppression while traveling from Vienna to Paris.
Arrived in Paris.
Robert Schumann declared 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius' in a review of the Op. 2 Variations.
Held his Paris debut concert to universal acclaim.
Published the Études Op. 10.
Attended the Lower Rhenish Music Festival with Hiller and met Felix Mendelssohn.
Went to Karlovy Vary to spend his last time with his parents.
Obtained French citizenship.
Met George Sand at a party hosted by Marie d'Agoult.
Stayed with the Wodziński family in Marienbad and Dresden, and proposed to Maria.
Visited London secretly with piano manufacturer Camille Pleyel.
Published the Études Op. 25.
Became lovers with George Sand.
Spent the winter with Sand in Majorca.
Published the Preludes Op. 28.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 2, Op. 35.
Headed to Sand's estate in Nohant to spend the summer.
Last public joint appearance with Liszt.
Wrote a letter to Grzymała about his deteriorating health after a Paris recital.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 3, Op. 58.
The relationship between Sand and Chopin deteriorated.
His relationship with Sand ended after a heated exchange of letters.
Held his last Paris concert with cellist Auguste Franchomme.
Left for London during the French Revolution of 1848.
Queen Victoria and Prince Albert attended his first London performance at Stafford House.
Wrote his last will in Edinburgh.
Held his last public concert at the Guildhall in London.
His older sister Ludwika came to Paris with her husband and daughter.
Died in Paris at the age of 39.
Funeral held at the Madeleine Church in Paris.
Chopin's tombstone was installed.
His older sister Ludwika took Chopin's heart back to Poland.
Julian Fontana compiled and published 23 unpublished piano pieces with 8 additional opus numbers (Op. 66–73).
17 Polish songs were published as Op. 74.
Chopin's music was used in the ballet Les Sylphides (Chopiniana).
Claude Debussy dedicated his piano études to the memory of Chopin.
Results of a visual examination of Chopin's preserved heart were published in the American Journal of Medicine.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Composer
keyAchievements.description: Maintained worldwide fame with his poetic genius based on a professional technique unmatched by anyone of his time.

keyAchievements.title: Specializing in Solo Piano Music
keyAchievements.description: Composed primarily solo piano music, and all his known works feature the piano.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Instrumental Ballade
keyAchievements.description: Created the instrumental ballade as a new musical genre.

keyAchievements.title: Making the Scherzo a Concert Piece
keyAchievements.description: Was the first to compose scherzos as individual concert pieces.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Prelude Genre
keyAchievements.description: Established essentially a new genre with his free cycle of preludes (Op. 28).

keyAchievements.title: Utilizing the Poetic Potential of Études
keyAchievements.description: Utilized the poetic potential of the concept of the concert étude in his two sets of études (Op. 10, Op. 25).

keyAchievements.title: Elevating the Status of the Mazurka
keyAchievements.description: Put the mazurka on the European musical map.

keyAchievements.title: New Standards for the Polonaise Form
keyAchievements.description: Set new standards for music in the polonaise form.

keyAchievements.title: Deepening the Nocturne Genre
keyAchievements.description: Elevated the nocturne to a deeper level of sophistication.

keyAchievements.title: Introducing Nationalist Consciousness into Music
keyAchievements.description: Is evaluated as having introduced a new nationalist consciousness into music.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Piano and Composition Schools
keyAchievements.description: Was called the founder of piano and composition schools.

faq.question: When was Chopin born?
faq.answer: Frédéric François Chopin was born on March 1, 1810.

faq.question: What was Chopin's real name?
faq.answer: His real name was Fryderyk Franciszek Chopin.

faq.question: What instrument did Chopin primarily compose for?
faq.answer: Chopin composed primarily solo piano music, and the piano appears in all his works.

faq.question: How many public performances did Chopin give in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He gave a minimal number of public performances, slightly over 30, preferring the more intimate atmosphere of salons.

faq.question: How did Chopin make a living in Paris?
faq.answer: He made a living by selling his compositions and giving piano lessons, which were highly popular at the time.

faq.question: What was George Sand's relationship to Chopin?
faq.answer: George Sand was a French author and Chopin's lover; they became lovers in July 1838.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Chopin and Franz Liszt?
faq.answer: The two were friends and respected each other, but their friendship was unstable and had the characteristics of a love-hate relationship.

faq.question: What was the cause of Chopin's death?
faq.answer: His death certificate listed the cause of death as tuberculosis, while recent research suggests it was highly likely a rare pericarditis caused by chronic tuberculosis complications.

faq.question: Where is Chopin's heart preserved?
faq.answer: His heart was removed by a doctor, preserved in alcohol in an urn, and taken back to Poland by his older sister Ludwika in 1850.

faq.question: Did Chopin give descriptive titles to his works?
faq.answer: With the exception of his Funeral March, the composer never named his instrumental pieces beyond genre and number.

timeline.event: Born into an artisan family in the Katsushika district of Edo (Childhood name: Tokitaro).
Began drawing around age 6.
Worked at a bookstore and lending library at age 12.
Worked as an apprentice woodcarver at age 14.
Entered the studio of Katsukawa Shunshō at age 18.
Received the name Shunrō from his master and published his first series of prints of Kabuki actors.
Produced Fireworks at Ryōgoku Bridge in Edo.
Death of his master Katsukawa Shunshō.
Second marriage.
Passed the name Tawaraya Sōri to his pupil, began working as an independent artist, and used the name "Hokusai Tomisa".
Began using the name "Katsushika Hokusai", published the landscape print series Famous Views of the Eastern Capital and Eight Views of Edo, and started taking on pupils.
Painted a massive portrait of the Buddhist monk Daruma, about 200 square meters in size, during an Edo festival.
Collaborated with popular novelist Kyokutei Bakin to produce a series of illustrated novels.
Produced illustrations for the fantasy novel Strange Tales of the Crescent Moon.
Changed his name to Taito at age 51 and began producing Hokusai Manga and various etehon (art manuals).
Produced Quick Lessons in Simplified Drawing.
The first volume of Hokusai Manga was published and became a huge success.
Painted a giant portrait of Daruma outside the Hongan-ji Nagoya Betsuin in Nagoya.
Changed his name to Iitsu and began gaining a solid reputation as an artist throughout Japan.
Produced his masterpiece, Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced Oceans of Wisdom (Shika Shashin-kyō).
Began working under the name "Gakyō Rōjin Manji" (The Old Man Mad About Art) and produced One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced his last print series, One Hundred Poems Explained by the Nurse.
A fire destroyed his studio and much of his work.
Visited Obuse in Shinano Province at age 83 and stayed there for several years.
Painted Chinese lions (shishi) every morning as a talisman to ward off bad luck.
Painted Dragon Flying in Clouds above Mount Fuji and Tiger in Snow.
Died and was buried at Seikyō-ji Temple in Taito Ward, Tokyo.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced this ukiyo-e series, which includes the iconic The Great Wave off Kanagawa, earning immense fame both domestically and internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Ukiyo-e Subjects
keyAchievements.description: He played a crucial role in broadening the subjects of ukiyo-e, which had primarily focused on courtesans and actors, to include landscapes, plants, and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Working tirelessly from childhood until his death, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, woodblock prints, and images for picture books.

keyAchievements.title: Innovative Composition and Depiction Skills
keyAchievements.description: He is considered one of the greatest masters in art history due to his innovative compositions and outstanding depictive skills.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Hokusai Manga
keyAchievements.description: He produced Hokusai Manga, containing thousands of drawings, which immediately became a huge success and was also used as a drawing manual.

keyAchievements.title: Experimentation with Western Perspective
keyAchievements.description: Among Japanese artists, he early on experimented with Western perspective, applying techniques to express depth and volume in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Art
keyAchievements.description: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

keyAchievements.title: Production of One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced another important series, One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, evaluated as "the greatest masterpiece among his landscape collections."

faq.question: Who was Katsushika Hokusai?
faq.answer: He was a Japanese ukiyo-e painter and printmaker of the Edo period, who played a crucial role in expanding the subjects of ukiyo-e to landscapes, plants, and animals.

faq.question: What is Hokusai's most famous work?
faq.answer: The Great Wave off Kanagawa, included in his ukiyo-e series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, is his most iconic work.

faq.question: What influence did Hokusai have on Western art?
faq.answer: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

faq.question: How many names did Hokusai use in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He is known to have used at least 30 names during his lifetime, closely linked to changes in his artwork and style.

faq.question: When did Hokusai die?
faq.answer: He died on May 10, 1849, showing a passion for creating better works even on his deathbed.

timeline.event: Born in Torquay, Devon, England.
Went to Paris at age 16 to study singing and piano.
Married her first husband, Archibald Christie, a colonel in the Royal Flying Corps.
Worked in a hospital dispensary during World War I.
Her only daughter, Rosalind Hicks, was born.
Disappeared for 10 days on December 8, attracting media attention.
Remarried to archaeologist Max Mallowan.
Received the Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE).
Received the Dame Commander of the Order of the British Empire (DBE).
Died at the age of 85.

keyAchievements.title: Reputation as a Mystery Writer
keyAchievements.description: Nicknamed the "Queen of Crime", she wrote over 80 mystery novels and gained immense popularity by creating the great Belgian detective Hercule Poirot and Miss Marple.

keyAchievements.title: Global Sales Records
keyAchievements.description: Her works are listed in the Guinness World Records, having sold over 1 billion copies in English and another 1 billion copies translated into 103 other languages.

keyAchievements.title: Most Translated Author
keyAchievements.description: She is recorded as the most translated author in the world by the Index Translationum.

keyAchievements.title: Longest-Running Play
keyAchievements.description: Her play The Mousetrap ran from its premiere in London in 1955 until November 2012.

faq.question: What was Agatha Christie's full name?
faq.answer: Her full name was Dame Agatha Christie, Lady Mallowan, DBE.

faq.question: What pen name did Agatha Christie use?
faq.answer: She used the pen name Mary Westmacott when writing romance novels.

faq.question: Who are the famous detective characters created by Agatha Christie?
faq.answer: She created the great Belgian detective Hercule Poirot and Miss Marple.

faq.question: How many copies of Agatha Christie's works have been sold?
faq.answer: Her works are listed in the Guinness World Records, having sold over 1 billion copies in the English-speaking world and another 1 billion copies in editions translated into 103 languages.

faq.question: What is Agatha Christie's most famous play, and how long was it performed?
faq.answer: Her play The Mousetrap ran from its premiere in London in 1955 until November 2012.

timeline.event: Samuel Langhorne Clemens (Mark Twain) was born.
Debuted in the literary world with "The Celebrated Jumping Frog".
Samuel Langhorne Clemens (Mark Twain) died.

keyAchievements.title: American Author
keyAchievements.description: An American author more famously known by his pen name Mark Twain.

keyAchievements.title: The Adventures of Tom Sawyer
keyAchievements.description: A children's book depicting the adventures of mischievous boys Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, set along the Mississippi River.

keyAchievements.title: The Celebrated Jumping Frog
keyAchievements.description: The work that made him debut in the literary world in 1865.

keyAchievements.title: The Innocents Abroad
keyAchievements.description: A work that gained popularity.

keyAchievements.title: Mississippi Trilogy
keyAchievements.description: Consists of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, Life on the Mississippi, and The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, expressing a hymn to the American and free spirit.

keyAchievements.title: Social Satires
keyAchievements.description: Works set in medieval feudal Europe, such as A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court, The Prince and the Pauper, and The Mysterious Stranger.

keyAchievements.title: Criticism of Christian Fundamentalism
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he published Letters from the Earth, criticizing Christian fundamentalism in the United States at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Criticism of Slavery
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he strove to expose and rectify the contradictions of a "free country with slavery."

faq.question: What was Mark Twain's real name?
faq.answer: Mark Twain's real name was Samuel Langhorne Clemens.

faq.question: What is Mark Twain's representative work?
faq.answer: His major works include The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, a children's book depicting the adventures of mischievous boys Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, set along the Mississippi River.

faq.question: Where did Mark Twain's pen name come from?
faq.answer: He used the unit of water depth, which he used during his time as a steamboat pilot on the Mississippi River, as his pen name.

faq.question: What was Mark Twain's religious stance?
faq.answer: Mark Twain was a progressive Presbyterian, but he criticized Christian fundamentalism in the United States at the time.

timeline.event: Born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany.
Harbored doubts about his faith triggered by the Lisbon earthquake.
Harbored doubts about his faith triggered by the Seven Years' War.
His romance with Gretchen was broken off.
Had a romance with Anna Katharina Schönkopf while studying at Leipzig University.
Wrote his first play, Die Laune des Verliebten (The Lover's Caprice).
Fostered pious religious feelings through his association with Susanne von Klettenberg while recovering at home.
Obtained a doctorate in law from the University of Strasbourg, reunited with Herder to open his eyes to the essence of literature, and laid the groundwork for the Sturm und Drang movement.
Opened a law practice.
Went to the Imperial Chamber Court in Wetzlar for legal training, where he met Charlotte Buff.
Published the play Götz von Berlichingen.
Published the novel The Sorrows of Young Werther.
Became engaged to Lili Schönemann, but broke it off shortly after.
Arrived in Weimar at the invitation of Duke Karl August of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach.
Handled state affairs for about 10 years in the early Weimar period, building a record of achievements as a privy councillor, senior privy councillor, and head of the cabinet.
Wrote the tragedy Iphigenia in Tauris (prose version).
Wrote Torquato Tasso.
Stated, "I am not an anti-Christian or an un-Christian, but a non-Christian."
Established his classical view of literature during his journey to Italy, and wrote Iphigenia in Tauris (verse version).
Embarked on a journey to Italy lasting a year and a half.
Published Egmont.
Returned to Germany.
Began living with Christiane von Vulpius.
Had his eldest son, August.
Torquato Tasso was first published.
Followed Duke August in the War of the First Coalition and participated in the Battle of Valmy.
Participated in the Siege of Mainz.
Reunited with Schiller on his way back from a natural science conference in Jena and began their friendship.
Wrote the Bildungsroman Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship.
Wrote the epic poem Hermann and Dorothea.
Fell into great shock due to Schiller's death.
Completed Part I of Faust.
Completed Theory of Colours.
Wrote Italian Journey.
Wrote West-östlicher Divan.
Completed Wilhelm Meister's Journeyman Years.
Wrote the Marienbad Elegy.
Wrote Second Stay in Rome.
Completed Part II of Faust.
Passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: He completed German classical literature by creating numerous literary works such as The Sorrows of Young Werther, Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, and Faust.

keyAchievements.title: Fostering Weimar as a Cultural Center
keyAchievements.description: Moving to Weimar in 1775, he played a decisive role in elevating the city to a cultural center.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an Administrator and Scientist
keyAchievements.description: He participated in state affairs serving as a minister in the Grand Duchy of Weimar, and left vast achievements showing interest in various natural science fields such as botany, anatomy, mineralogy, geology, and the theory of colors.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing German Classical Literature
keyAchievements.description: He established his classical view of literature during his journey to Italy and met Schiller, blossoming German Weimar Classicism together.

keyAchievements.title: Court Theatre Director
keyAchievements.description: As the director of the court theatre, he was active across the board in management, directing, and actor training, elevating the theatre to a world-class level.

faq.question: Who was Goethe?
faq.answer: He was a German classical writer, philosopher, and scientist, who also served as a minister in the Grand Duchy of Weimar.

faq.question: What are Goethe's representative works?
faq.answer: His representative works include The Sorrows of Young Werther, Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship, and Faust.

faq.question: What was Goethe's stance on religion?
faq.answer: Born into a Lutheran family, he developed doubts about his faith due to the Lisbon earthquake and the Seven Years' War, and referred to himself as a "non-Christian".

faq.question: What role did Goethe play in Weimar?
faq.answer: Moving to Weimar in 1775, he played a decisive role in elevating the city to a cultural center and participated in state affairs as an administrator.

timeline.event: Born in Gwangseok-dong, Haeju-bu, Hwanghae-do as the eldest son of father Ahn Tae-hun and mother Jo Maria
Married Kim Ah-ryeo and had his first meeting with Kim Gu
His father Ahn Tae-hun converted to Catholicism
Converted to Catholicism at age 19 and received the baptismal name Thomas
Promoted Catholic missionary work and the establishment of a Catholic university
A dispute arose between his father Ahn Tae-hun and Seo Won-hun, a Qing Dynasty physician
Battered Seo Won-hun along with about 10 others including Lee Yong-il
Upon hearing the news of the Eulsa Treaty, he went to Shanghai for the movement to restore national sovereignty, but was refused cooperation and returned; his father Ahn Tae-hun passed away
After moving to Jinnampo, Pyongan-namdo, he managed a coal trading company before liquidating it and starting an education movement by establishing Samheung School

Took over Nampo Donui School and worked as a teacher; responded to the National Debt Repayment Movement and was appointed as the head of the Gwanseo branch of the National Debt Repayment Association; went to Primorsky Krai and joined the righteous army
Went into exile in Manchuria after the dissolution of the Korean Empire's army

Under Jeon Je-deok's command, as the Lieutenant General of the Korean Righteous Army, Special Independent Commander, and Commander of the A-ryeong District, he and Eom In-seop led about 100 subordinates across the Tumen River to infiltrate the country and launch a surprise attack on the Japanese garrison
Formed the Dongui Danjihoe (Society of Cut Fingers) with 11 like-minded comrades and cut off a joint of his left ring finger to make a blood pledge
Left Vladivostok and moved to Harbin
Assassinated Ito Hirobumi in Harbin and was arrested by the Russian army
Sentenced to death at the Kwantung Government-General District Court in Lushun, Kwantung Leased Territory
Met with the Chief Justice of the High Court and raised an objection to being tried as a murderer for his actions as a righteous army commander
Received the sacrament of penance from Father Joseph Wilhelm
Offered his final Mass officiated by Father Wilhelm
His execution was postponed and he left his last requests to his two younger brothers
Executed at 10:00 AM at the age of 32
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Lieutenant General (later revised to the Republic of Korea Medal)

keyAchievements.title: Assassination of Ito Hirobumi
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the anti-Japanese independence movement by assassinating Japanese politician Ito Hirobumi in Harbin on October 26, 1909.

keyAchievements.title: Education Movement
keyAchievements.description: Strived for the movement to restore national sovereignty through education by establishing Samheung School and taking over Nampo Donui School.

keyAchievements.title: Righteous Army Activities
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the righteous army movement after moving to Primorsky Krai in 1907, and engaged in combat with the Japanese army as the Lieutenant General of the Korean Righteous Army in 1908.

keyAchievements.title: Formation of Dongui Danjihoe
keyAchievements.description: Formed the Dongui Danjihoe with 11 comrades in 1909 and cut off his left ring finger to strengthen his will for independence with a blood pledge.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: Left an ideological legacy by writing the unfinished book "On Peace in the East" and the autobiography "The History of Ahn Eung-chil" while in prison.

keyAchievements.title: Leaving Calligraphy
keyAchievements.description: Left many calligraphic works while imprisoned in Lushun Prison, some of which have been designated as national treasures.

faq.question: When and where was Ahn Jung-geun born?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun was born on September 2, 1879, in Gwangseok-dong, Haeju-bu, Hwanghae-do, as the eldest son of father Ahn Tae-hun and mother Jo Maria.

faq.question: Whom did Ahn Jung-geun assassinate?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Japanese politician Ito Hirobumi in Harbin on October 26, 1909.

faq.question: Why did Ahn Jung-geun assassinate Ito Hirobumi?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Ito Hirobumi because he judged that Ito had committed 15 crimes, including the assassination of Empress Myeongseong, the signing of the Eulsa Treaty, and the dethronement of Emperor Gojong.

faq.question: When was Ahn Jung-geun executed?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun was executed on March 26, 1910, following the assassination of Ito Hirobumi.

faq.question: Where are Ahn Jung-geun's remains?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun's remains were buried in the prisoners' cemetery near Lushun Prison, but their exact location has not been found to this day.

faq.question: What are the major works of Ahn Jung-geun?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun wrote "On Peace in the East" and the autobiography "The History of Ahn Eung-chil" while in prison.

faq.question: What is Ahn Jung-geun's baptismal name?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun's baptismal name is Thomas.

timeline.event: Born in Majae, Chobu-myeon, Gwangju-bu, Gyeonggi-do.
After his father Jeong Jae-won resigned from his government post and returned to his rural home, he named Jeong Yak-yong's childhood name Gwinong.
Married Pungsan Hong, the daughter of military official Hong Hwa-bo.
His father Jeong Jae-won became the assistant secretary of the Board of Taxation and rented a house in Seoul, where he met Lee Ga-hwan and Lee Seung-hun.
Participated in the Western Learning doctrine training session at Jueosa Temple and Cheonjinam Hermitage in Yangju, Gyeonggi-do, led by Gwon Cheol-sin.
Passed the Jeungwangsi examination celebrating the investiture of the Crown Prince and became a Saengwon (licentiate) through the Hoesi examination.
Heard an explanation of Catholic doctrines from Lee Byeok, the brother-in-law of his eldest brother Jeong Yak-hyeon.
The Myeongnyebang Incident occurred, where the Myeongnyebang community run by Lee Seung-hun was discovered and he was dragged to the Board of Punishments.
King Jeongjo defined Catholicism as an evil cult and issued a ban, and ordered the search and burning of Western Learning books related to Catholicism.
Passed the highest-level state examination at the age of 27 and entered government service.
When the Jinsan Incident occurred, where Yun Ji-chung held his mother's funeral in a Catholic manner in Jinsan, Jeolla-do, he was exiled to Haemi, Seosan, but was released after 11 days.
Participated in the design of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress and utilized the Geojunggi (traditional crane).
His father Jeong Jae-won passed away.
Lectured at Seonggyungwan and patrolled Yeoncheon, Saknyeong, etc. as a secret royal inspector of Gyeonggi-do.
Demoted to Geumjeong Chalbang (station master) due to the Eulmyo Persecution incident.
Due to suspicions raised by Busagwa Bak Jang-seol, Lee Seung-hun was demoted to Yesan, Lee Ga-hwan to Chungju Moksa, and Jeong Yak-yong to Hongju Geumjeong Chalbang.
While serving as the Third Minister of the Board of Punishments, he was impeached by his older brother Jeong Yak-jeon, so he submitted a "Jamyeongso" (petition of self-vindication) and resigned.
While living in retirement in his hometown, he heard the news of King Jeongjo's death and went up to Seoul.
Queen Dowager Jeongsun issued an order suppressing Catholicism and began a purge of the Namin faction.
Arrested and imprisoned, then his sentence was reduced to exile and he was released.
Began 18 years of exile in places like Janggi, Gyeongsang-do, and Gangjin, Jeolla-do.
His son Jeong Nong-jang (Nong-a) died of smallpox.
His exile was lifted and he rose to the position of Seungji (Royal Secretary).
Returned to his hometown.
While in exile in Gangjin-gun, Jeolla-do, he conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality.
Passed away at his home in Mahyeon-ri.
Posthumously promoted to Jeongheon Daebu Gyujanggak Jehak and granted the posthumous title 'Mundo'.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Writing Activities
