keyAchievements.description: Known as the person who wrote the most books since the invention of Chinese characters, authoring over 500 volumes.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Mokminsimseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book that details the principles and mindset of local magistrates who govern the people.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Heumheumsinseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book detailing the principles and norms of judgments, punishments, and prison administration based on actual cases he investigated while serving as the magistrate of Goksan.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Gyeongseyupyo"
keyAchievements.description: A book discussing appropriate and standard institutional regulations for state management.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Magwahoetong"
keyAchievements.description: Authored the measles treatment book "Magwahoetong" based on Lee Heon-gil's "Majingibang," saving the lives of countless Joseon people.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Geojunggi
keyAchievements.description: Created the pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) for use during the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of Silhak
keyAchievements.description: Synthesized the Silhak (Practical Learning) ideology handed down through Yu Hyeong-won and Seongho Yi Ik into a comprehensive system.

keyAchievements.title: Conception of Jeongjeon-ron
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality through the free distribution of land and communal labor-communal distribution.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure
keyAchievements.description: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012 to commemorate the 250th anniversary of his birth.

faq.question: When was Jeong Yak-yong born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was born on August 5, 1762, and passed away on April 7, 1836.

faq.question: What are the major works of Jeong Yak-yong?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong's three major works are "Mokminsimseo", "Heumheumsinseo", and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: What was Jeong Yak-yong's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was once deeply engrossed in Catholicism but apostatized after the Sinhae Persecution (1791), and actively participated in searching for Catholic believers during the Sinyu Persecution (1801).

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong do during his exile?
faq.answer: During his 18-year exile, Jeong Yak-yong authored extensive writings, including "Mokminsimseo" and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: How did Jeong Yak-yong contribute to the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress?
faq.answer: During the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress, Jeong Yak-yong created a pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) to utilize it.

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong's ideology aim for?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong aimed to establish Silhak of statecraft and practical use, correct distorted theories by returning to the essence of Confucianism of Confucius and Mencius, and build a welfare state that cares for the socially and economically weak.

faq.question: Was Jeong Yak-yong selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure?
faq.answer: Yes, Jeong Yak-yong was selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012, marking the 250th anniversary of his birth.

timeline.event: Born (estimated)
Died (estimated)

keyAchievements.title: Development of Confucian Thought
keyAchievements.description: Developed Confucius's thought and greatly influenced the transmission of Confucianism to future generations.

keyAchievements.title: The Book "Mencius"
keyAchievements.description: The book "Mencius" contains Mencius's thoughts, and it is believed to have been compiled by his disciples.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Innate Human Goodness
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the theory of innate human goodness, arguing that human nature is inherently good and that the human mind is equipped with the "Four Beginnings" of the four virtues: benevolence, righteousness, propriety, and wisdom.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Benevolent Government and Royal Politics
keyAchievements.description: After the age of 40, he traveled across the world advocating benevolent government and royal politics.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Four Beginnings
keyAchievements.description: Explained that the human mind contains the Four Beginnings: a feeling of compassion, a feeling of shame and dislike, a feeling of deference and compliance, and a feeling of right and wrong.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Five Relationships
keyAchievements.description: Presented the Theory of the Five Relationships, organizing five human relationships: intimacy between father and son, righteousness between ruler and minister, distinction between husband and wife, order between elder and junior, and trust between friends.

keyAchievements.title: Confirmation of the Right of Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Confirmed the right of revolution, stating that a monarch who betrays the will of the people and violates benevolence and righteousness is nothing but an ordinary fellow, and subduing such a monarch is not an act of regicide.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Cyclical View of History
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to prove the legitimacy of Confucius based on the cyclical view of history that a sage appears every 500 years.

faq.question: Is the story of Mencius's mother moving three times true?
faq.answer: The story of Mencius's mother moving three times is an anecdote from the "Biographies of Exemplary Women" written by the Han Dynasty scholar Liu Xiang, and the general consensus is that it is hard to view it as a historical fact.

faq.question: From whom did Mencius learn?
faq.answer: Mencius studied the Six Classics under the tutelage of Zisi, the grandson of Confucius.

faq.question: What was the name of Mencius's mother?
faq.answer: The name of Mencius's mother is Ji (伋).

faq.question: Who compiled the book "Mencius"?
faq.answer: There is a theory that the book "Mencius" was written in his later years, but it is more accurate to view it as having been compiled by his disciples.

timeline.event: Born
Became a Langzhong (palace attendant) and traveled to regions such as Jiangnan, Shandong, and Henan while accompanying Emperor Wu
Before his father Sima Tan died of illness, he was asked to complete the compilation of the history book
Succeeded his father and became the Grand Astrologer (Taishiling)
Revised the calendar and completed the Taichu Calendar
Incurred the wrath of Emperor Wu while defending General Li Ling and was sentenced to castration
Died

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian)
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a 130-volume history book that chronicled the history from the Yellow Emperor, the first sovereign of China, to Emperor Wu, organized by prominent figures.

keyAchievements.title: Revision of the Taichu Calendar
keyAchievements.description: Revised the calendar in 104 BC and completed the Taichu Calendar.

faq.question: Who is Sima Qian?
faq.answer: A historian of the Former Han Dynasty in China, the author of the "Shiji" (Records of the Grand Historian), and considered one of the greatest historians of the East.

faq.question: Why was Sima Qian castrated?
faq.answer: In 99 BC, when General Li Ling was defeated by the Xiongnu, Sima Qian defended Li Ling, incurring the wrath of Emperor Wu and resulting in his castration.

faq.question: What are the major works of Sima Qian?
faq.answer: His representative work is the "Shiji" (Records of the Grand Historian), and other works include "Letter to Ren An" and "Lament for Not Meeting One's Time".

faq.question: What government positions did Sima Qian hold?
faq.answer: He succeeded his father as the Grand Astrologer (Taishiling), and after undergoing castration, he was appointed as the Palace Secretary (Zhongshuling).

timeline.event: Died

keyAchievements.title: Succession and Development of Daoist Thought
keyAchievements.description: Succeeded and developed Laozi's thought, becoming a representative figure of Daoism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Myriad Things Monism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the monism of all things.

keyAchievements.title: Argument for Return to Nature and Nothingness
keyAchievements.description: Inheriting Laozi's thoughts, he argued for a return to nature and a return to nothingness.

keyAchievements.title: Pursuit of Individual Peace of Mind
keyAchievements.description: Unlike Laozi, he devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Philosophy for an Ideal Life
keyAchievements.description: He viewed the ideal life as one that discards the physical and mental roots of anxiety, reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility' to achieve freedom and independence.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Epistemology
keyAchievements.description: Established an epistemology that conceptual recognition and value judgments are meaningless, and that only thorough ignorance is correct.

faq.question: What was Zhuangzi's real name?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi's real name was Zhou (周).

faq.question: Whose thoughts did Zhuangzi succeed and develop?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi succeeded and developed the thoughts of Laozi.

faq.question: What government position did Zhuangzi hold?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi became an official of the lacquer tree garden in Meng.

faq.question: Did Zhuangzi accept the offer of King Wei of Chu?
faq.answer: King Wei of Chu requested him to assist in politics, but Zhuangzi refused.

faq.question: What is Zhuangzi's ideal life?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi viewed the ideal life as discarding the physical and mental roots of anxiety and reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility'. Through this, he argued, one could follow the laws of nature and attain freedom and independence without being infringed upon by anything.

faq.question: How do Zhuangzi's thoughts differ from Laozi's?
faq.answer: While Laozi had some interest in the reality of politics and society, Zhuangzi devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

timeline.event: Born during the Tang Dynasty era.
In his hometown of Shu, he practiced Daoism while climbing the mountains of Sichuan with a hermit named Dongyanzi, and later left his hometown to travel around Jiangnan.
At the age of 43, he received an edict from Emperor Xuanzong and stayed in Chang'an as a Hanlin Gongfeng (academician).
Feeling bored with his life as an official, he argued with Gao Lishi, a close aide to Emperor Xuanzong, and resigned.
Returned to Jiangnan.
Joined the army of Prince Yong Lin, the sixteenth son of Emperor Xuanzong, but Prince Yong's army was designated as a rebel force, leading to his exile to Yelang (present-day Guizhou).
Died at the age of 61 at the home of his relative Li Yangbing, who was the magistrate of Dangtu County in Anhui Province.

keyAchievements.title: Great Poet in Chinese History
keyAchievements.description: A poet of the Tang Dynasty era, he is considered one of the greatest poets in Chinese history along with Du Fu.

keyAchievements.title: Poet Immortal (Shixian)
keyAchievements.description: Li Bai was called the 'Poet Immortal', and his poetic talent was evaluated as 'genius'.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Poet
keyAchievements.description: Currently, about 1,100 of his poems survive, and his poetic literature themed around Daoism and wine is famous.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Gushi (Ancient Style Poetry) and Jueju (Quatrains)
keyAchievements.description: He specialized in gushi and jueju, and his jueju were evaluated as 'divine works'.

keyAchievements.title: Free and Fantastic Poetic Style
keyAchievements.description: His poems are grand in scale and full of vitality, sometimes featuring fantastic and extremely free characteristics.

faq.question: What were Li Bai's courtesy name and pseudonym?
faq.answer: Li Bai's courtesy name was Taibai, and his pseudonym was Qinglian Jushi (Householder of the Azure Lotus).

faq.question: By what nickname was Li Bai called?
faq.answer: Considered one of the greatest poets in Chinese history, Li Bai was called the 'Poet Immortal (Shixian)'.

faq.question: How many of Li Bai's poems survive?
faq.answer: Currently, about 1,100 of his poems survive.

faq.question: What are the main themes in Li Bai's poetry?
faq.answer: The main themes related to his poetic literature are Daoism and wine.

faq.question: Where was Li Bai from?
faq.answer: Currently, it is widely accepted that he was an Central Asian from Suyab of the Tang Dynasty.

faq.question: Did Li Bai receive formal education?
faq.answer: Because his father was a merchant, Li Bai did not receive formal education.

faq.question: When did Li Bai die?
faq.answer: Li Bai died in 762 at the age of 61 at the home of his relative Li Yangbing, who was the magistrate of Dangtu County in Anhui Province.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Li Bai's poetry?
faq.answer: His poems are grand in scale and full of vitality, sometimes featuring fantastic and extremely free characteristics.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a poor peasant in Nakamura, Aichi District, Owari Province (February 6, Tenbun 6, lunar calendar).
Began working as a servant for Oda Nobunaga.
Married Nene, the adopted daughter of Asano Nagakatsu (Eiroku 4).

Played an active role in the capture of Mitsukuri Castle in Omi, and when Oda Nobunaga installed Ashikaga Yoshiaki as Shogun, he managed political affairs in Kyoto alongside Akechi Mitsuhide and Niwa Nagahide (September, Eiroku 11, lunar calendar).
Joined the subjugation of Asakura Yoshikage of Echizen Province, and distinguished himself by commanding the rearguard at Kanegasaki (Genki 1).
After the annihilation of the Azai clan, he was granted three districts in Omi, took up his post as the lord of Nagahama Castle, and changed his surname to Hashiba (Tensho 1).
Returned without permission while being dispatched to rescue Shibata Katsuie, and subjugated Matsunaga Hisahide of Yamato Province (Battle of Shigisan Castle) (Tensho 4).
Subjugated Ukita Naoie, a daimyo of Bizen and Mimasaka Provinces (Tensho 7).
Attacked and received the surrender of Bessho Nagaharu, the lord of Miki Castle in Harima (Battle of Miki). Captured Arikoyama Castle of Yamana Akihiro of Tajima Province (Tensho 8).
Captured Tottori Castle (Battle of Tottori Castle). Placed Awaji Province under his control by capturing Iwaya Castle (Tensho 9).
Requested reinforcements from Oda Nobunaga during the water attack on Takamatsu Castle in Bitchu (Tensho 10).
Received the news that his lord Oda Nobunaga had committed suicide in the Honno-ji Incident (June 2, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Defeated Akechi Mitsuhide at the Battle of Yamazaki (June 13, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
At the Kiyosu Conference, Oda Nobunaga's legitimate grandson Sanboshi was decided as the successor (June 27, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Held Oda Nobunaga's funeral with his adopted son Hashiba Hidekatsu as the chief mourner (October 15, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Conflict with Shibata Katsuie intensified, leading him to besiege Nagahama Castle, capture Kajiki Castle, and receive Sanboshi from Oda Nobutaka (Tensho 11).
Defeated Shibata Katsuie at the Battle of Shizugatake (April 21, Tensho 11, lunar calendar).
Began the construction of Osaka Castle on the site of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji (Tensho 11).
Conflicted with Oda Nobukatsu, and Tokugawa Ieyasu sided with Nobukatsu (Battle of Komaki and Nagakute) (Tensho 12).
Ikeda Tsuneoki, his son Motosuke, and Mori Nagayoshi were killed in action at the Battle of Nagakute (April 9, Tensho 12, lunar calendar).
Made peace with Oda Nobukatsu (November 11, Tensho 12, lunar calendar).
Appointed to the court rank of Senior Second Rank and the office of Palace Minister (Naidaijin) (March 10, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Executed the conquest of Kishu (March 21, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Executed the conquest of Shikoku (July 25, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Ascended to the position of Kampaku (Imperial Regent) (July 11, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Sassa Narimasa of Etchu surrendered to Hideyoshi (August 25, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Forced his younger sister Asahi to divorce and married her off to Ieyasu, and sent his mother Omandokoro as a hostage to Ieyasu (Tensho 14).
Was granted the surname Toyotomi by the Imperial Court (September 9, Tensho 14, lunar calendar).
Ascended to the position of Daijo-daijin (Chancellor of the Realm) and established his regime (December 25, Tensho 14, lunar calendar).
Fought and was defeated by the Shimazu army led by Shimazu Iehisa at the Hetsugi River in Bungo (Battle of Hetsugigawa) (Tensho 14).
Led a large army of 200,000 to conquer Kyushu, resulting in the surrender of the Shimazu clan (Kyushu Campaign) (Tensho 15).
Issued the Bateren Edict expelling Christian missionaries (June, Tensho 15).
Implemented the Sword Hunt (Katanagari) (Tensho 16).
Tsurumatsu was born to his concubine Yodo-dono and was designated as his successor (Tensho 17).
Led a large army of 210,000 to dispatch troops to Kanto to suppress the Go-Hojo clan, and besieged Odawara Castle (Siege of Odawara) (Tensho 18).

After the death from illness of Tsurumatsu, whom he had designated as his successor, he adopted his nephew Hidetsugu, transferred the position of Kampaku to him, and was himself called Taiko (Tensho 19).
Ordered the tea master Sen no Rikyu to commit seppuku (Tensho 19).
Suppressed the rebellion led by Kunohe Masazane in the Tohoku region (Tensho 19).
The Imjin War (Japanese invasions of Korea) began (Bunroku 1).
Peace negotiations with Ming China began. His concubine Yodo-dono gave birth to Hideyori (Bunroku 2).
Disinherited Kampaku Toyotomi Hidetsugu, exiled him to Mount Koya, and later forced him to commit seppuku on charges of treason (Bunroku 4).
Peace negotiations with Ming China broke down (Bunroku 5).
Re-invaded Joseon Korea with an army of 140,000 (Jeongyu War) (Keicho 2).
Died at Fushimi Castle (August 18, Keicho 3, lunar calendar).

keyAchievements.title: Three Great Unifiers of the Sengoku Period
keyAchievements.description: Along with Oda Nobunaga and Tokugawa Ieyasu, he is known as one of the Three Great Unifiers of the Sengoku period.

keyAchievements.title: Succeeding Oda Nobunaga's Enterprise
keyAchievements.description: After the Honno-ji Incident, he defeated Akechi Mitsuhide and succeeded Oda Nobunaga's enterprise.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Japan
keyAchievements.description: Unified Japan by subduing the Go-Hojo clan and brought an end to the long-lasting Sengoku period.

keyAchievements.title: Appointment as Kampaku and Daijo-daijin
keyAchievements.description: Established his regime by being appointed Kampaku and Daijo-daijin, and was granted the surname Toyotomi by the Imperial Court.

keyAchievements.title: Taiko Land Survey and Sword Hunt
keyAchievements.description: Stabilized the domestic situation in Japan by implementing the Taiko Land Survey (Taikou Kenchi) and the Sword Hunt (Katanagari) policies.

keyAchievements.title: Rise from the Lowest Class to Ruler
keyAchievements.description: Ascended to become the ruler of Japan despite being a person from the lowest social class in the country.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of Osaka Castle
keyAchievements.description: Constructed Osaka Castle on the site of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji.

keyAchievements.title: Leading the Imjin War
keyAchievements.description: He was the main instigator who led the Imjin War in the first year of Bunroku (1592) and invaded Joseon Korea.

faq.question: What was Toyotomi Hideyoshi's background?
faq.answer: He is known to have been born as the son of a poor peasant in Nakamura, Aichi District, Owari Province. Because of his very low social status, he was conscious of his humble origins, leading to suspicions that records from his time and later periods were often distorted or glorified.

faq.question: Why couldn't Toyotomi Hideyoshi become a Shogun?
faq.answer: Coming from a commoner background and not being of the Minamoto bloodline, he could not become a Shogun due to the rule that 'only a Minamoto can become a Shogun'. Instead, he rose to the positions of Kampaku and Daijo-daijin.

faq.question: What were the main reasons Toyotomi Hideyoshi started the Imjin War?
faq.answer: General analysis suggests that he sought to acquire the justification to become Shogun by recovering the southern part of Joseon, which he claimed as former territory, and annihilating the Ming army. Furthermore, there is a theory that he intended to solidify the unstable vassal relationships by granting new territories to the samurai.

faq.question: What were Toyotomi Hideyoshi's physical characteristics?
faq.answer: He looked like a monkey and was called 'little monkey'. According to the records of the missionary Luis Frois, he was short, had an ugly face, and was polydactylous, having six fingers on his right hand.

faq.question: How was Toyotomi Hideyoshi's death handled?
faq.answer: He died at Fushimi Castle in the third year of Keicho (1598). Due to concerns about the army's morale, no funeral was held, and his death was kept a secret. His tomb was blown up and destroyed after the Battle of Sekigahara.

timeline.event: Born into a family of Ulama (Islamic scholars) in Tangier, Morocco.
Left his hometown at the age of 21 and embarked on the Hajj, a pilgrimage to Mecca.
Arrived in Alexandria.
After spending a month in Mecca, he joined a caravan returning to Iraq.
Arrived in Baghdad.
Left Baghdad and headed north along the Tigris River.
Arrived in Aden.
Visited Sultan Al-Hasan ibn Sulaiman of Kilwa.
Visited Mogadishu.
Decided to seek employment at the court of Muhammad bin Tughluq, the ruler of the Delhi Sultanate, and joined a caravan departing from Anatolia towards Delhi.
Arrived in Constantinople and had an audience with Andronikos III Palaiologos.
Arrived at the Samudera Pasai Sultanate located in the Aceh region of northern Sumatra.
Left Guangzhou and embarked on his journey back home.
Arrived in Damascus and heard the news that his father had died 15 years earlier.
When Alfonso XI of Castile attacked the Tangier region, he rallied with other Muslims to defend the port.
Left Fez and headed to Sijilmasa.
Arrived in Taghaza with a caravan.
Joined a caravan leaving Mali and headed to Timbuktu.
Returned to Sijilmasa.
Returned to Morocco.
Dictated his travelogue to Ibn Juzayy by the order of the Sultan, and Ibn Juzayy compiled and wrote the "Rihla of Ibn Battuta".
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Authoring the Famous Travelogue (Rihla)
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'Rihla', a famous travelogue that documented the medieval Islamic world and extensive regions beyond.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Travels
keyAchievements.description: Traveled extensively over 120,000 km across contemporary Islamic countries, China, Sumatra, etc., leaving behind records of great cultural anthropological value.

keyAchievements.title: Appointment as Judge of the Delhi Sultanate
keyAchievements.description: Was appointed as a judge (Qadi) by Sultan Muhammad bin Tughluq of the Delhi Sultanate and served in that role.

faq.question: When and where was Ibn Battuta born?
faq.answer: Ibn Battuta was born on February 24, 1304, into a family of Ulama (Islamic scholars) in Tangier, Morocco.

faq.question: What value does Ibn Battuta's travelogue hold?
faq.answer: His travelogue describes an extensive journey of 120,000 km across almost all contemporary Islamic countries, China, and down to Sumatra, holding great cultural anthropological value.

faq.question: What professions did Ibn Battuta have?
faq.answer: He was a traveler, explorer, and author of the famous travelogue (Rihla). He also served as an appointed judge (Qadi) in the Delhi Sultanate and lived as a judge in Morocco during his later years.

faq.question: Who wrote Ibn Battuta's travelogue?
faq.answer: By the order of the Sultan, Ibn Battuta dictated his travelogue to Ibn Juzayy, who compiled and wrote the "Rihla of Ibn Battuta".

timeline.event: Born in Baghdad (estimated)
Wrote the book 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals'
Wrote his major work 'al-Kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr wa'l-muqabala' (The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing)
Death (estimated)
His book on arithmetic was translated into Latin
His achievements spread to Europe through Latin translations
The book 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing' was translated into Latin

keyAchievements.title: Algebraic work 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the book that introduced the general solution of linear and quadratic equations, considered the origin of modern algebra.

keyAchievements.title: Arithmetic work 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the book that played a major role in introducing Hindu-Arabic numerals and arithmetic to the Western world.

keyAchievements.title: Astronomical tables 'Zij al-Sindhind'
keyAchievements.description: Created astronomical tables including calendars, astronomical calculations, and sine and cosine trigonometric functions.

keyAchievements.title: Geographical work 'The Image of the Earth'
keyAchievements.description: Revised Ptolemy's Geography and wrote a book containing the coordinates and geographical features of 2402 cities.

keyAchievements.title: Study of mechanical devices
keyAchievements.description: Studied mechanical devices such as the astrolabe and sundial.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in mapmaking and measuring the Earth's circumference
keyAchievements.description: Assisted in mapmaking for Caliph al-Ma'mun and participated in the project to measure the circumference of the Earth.

faq.question: What is Al-Khwarizmi known for?
faq.answer: He was a Persian mathematician who established the four fundamental operations of arithmetic using Arabic numerals derived from India, utilized the concept of zero and positional value, and is known as the 'father of algebra'.

faq.question: How did the terms 'algorithm' and 'algebra' originate?
faq.answer: 'Algorithm' derived from his name, and 'algebra' originated from the title of his book <al-jabr wa al-muqabala>.

faq.question: What are Al-Khwarizmi's major works, and what do they contain?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing', which covers solutions for linear and quadratic equations, 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals', which introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to the West, and 'The Image of the Earth', a revision of Ptolemy's Geography.

timeline.event: Born in Syracuse
Died during the Second Punic War
Cicero discovered Archimedes' tomb near Syracuse
Danish historian of mathematics Johan Ludvig Heiberg discovered 'The Method of Mechanical Theorems' in Istanbul
Greek scientist Ioannis Sakkas successfully recreated the Archimedes heat ray
'The Method of Mechanical Theorems' was auctioned at Christie's in New York
'The Method of Mechanical Theorems' was revealed to the public
Students at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology conducted an experiment to recreate the Archimedes heat ray

keyAchievements.title: Research in statics and hydrostatics
keyAchievements.description: Researched statics and hydrostatics in the field of physics.

keyAchievements.title: Explanation of the principles of levers and pulleys
keyAchievements.description: Explained the principles of levers and pulleys.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of machines
keyAchievements.description: Invented machines such as the first elevator, the Archimedes' screw, and naval attack mirrors.

keyAchievements.title: Mathematical achievements
keyAchievements.description: Left behind mathematical achievements such as the introduction of the decimal system, calculating the area enclosed by a parabola, and calculating pi.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Archimedes' principle
keyAchievements.description: Discovered a method for determining the volume of irregular objects to prove the purity of a gold crown.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Archimedes' screw
keyAchievements.description: Developed a device to pump water by rotating a spiral fitted inside a hollow cylinder.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Claw of Archimedes
keyAchievements.description: Invented a defensive weapon using heavy metal hooks to capsize enemy ships.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Archimedes' heat ray
keyAchievements.description: Reportedly invented a type of burning glass that focused sunlight to ignite fires.

keyAchievements.title: Elucidation of the principle of the lever
keyAchievements.description: Elucidated various principles of the lever in 'On the Equilibrium of Planes'.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a planetarium
keyAchievements.description: According to Cicero, he created a planetarium showing the movements of the sun, moon, and five planets.

keyAchievements.title: Calculation of the approximation of pi
keyAchievements.description: Used polygons to calculate the approximation of pi (π) as 3.1408 < π < 3.1429.

keyAchievements.title: Proof of the quadrature of the parabola
keyAchievements.description: Proved that the area enclosed by a parabola and a straight line is 4/3 of the area of its inscribed triangle.

keyAchievements.title: Devised a method to express extremely large numbers
keyAchievements.description: Introduced a method for expressing extremely large numbers, such as the number of grains of sand, in 'The Sand Reckoner'.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of major works
keyAchievements.description: Wrote several mathematical and scientific treatises, including 'On the Equilibrium of Planes', 'Measurement of a Circle', and 'On the Sphere and Cylinder'.

faq.question: When and where was Archimedes born?
faq.answer: Archimedes was born around 287 BC in Syracuse, which was part of Magna Graecia in ancient Greece.

faq.question: How did Archimedes die?
faq.answer: He was killed by a Roman soldier around 212 BC when Syracuse fell to Rome during the Second Punic War.

faq.question: What is Archimedes' most famous anecdote?
faq.answer: The anecdote where he discovered a method to measure the volume of an irregular object to verify the purity of a gold crown and shouted 'Eureka!'.

faq.question: What was inscribed on Archimedes' tombstone?
faq.answer: An illustration demonstrating his proof of the relationship between the volumes of a cylinder and a sphere of the same height was inscribed on it.

faq.question: What was the Archimedes' screw used for?
faq.answer: It was a device consisting of a spiral fitted inside a long hollow cylinder that pumped water when rotated, used for pumping bilge water out of ships.

faq.question: Was the Archimedes' heat ray actually used?
faq.answer: The 2nd-century author Lucian wrote that it burned Roman warships during the Siege of Syracuse, but its actual use has been debated since the Renaissance.

faq.question: Did Archimedes really say 'Do not disturb my circles'?
faq.answer: This phrase was a later invention and is not mentioned in Plutarch's Parallel Lives.

faq.question: Have any of Archimedes' works been rediscovered in modern times?
faq.answer: In 1906, Danish historian of mathematics Johan Ludvig Heiberg discovered 'The Method of Mechanical Theorems', which included 'On Floating Bodies', in Istanbul.

timeline.event: Born as the youngest of four children in Toruń, Poland
Lost his father at the age of ten
Enrolled at the University of Kraków and began studying mathematics and astronomy
Completed his studies in mathematics and astronomy at the University of Kraków
Left the University of Kraków for Bologna, Italy, and enrolled in the Faculty of Theology
Boarded at the home of Domenico Maria Novara, an astronomy professor at the University of Bologna, and assisted with his astronomical observations
Completed four years of law studies at the University of Bologna and traveled to Rome
Received permission from the Catholic Diocese Chapter of Warmia in Frombork to study for two more years
Enrolled in the medical program at the University of Padua
Received a doctorate in canon law from the University of Ferrara
Returned to Warmia and began serving as a member of the chapter
Observed and recorded the great conjunction of planets in Cancer
Translated Theophylact Simocatta's 'Epistles: Moral, Rustic, and Amatory' into Latin
Completed the basic framework of a heliocentric astronomical system and circulated the 'Commentariolus' (Little Commentary) among his acquaintances
Performed administrative duties as a chancellor of the chapter
Moved to Frauenburg (Frombork) in Royal Prussia and worked as a cathedral canon after the death of his maternal uncle Lucas Watzenrode
Tasked with administering the lands of the Warmia chapter and resolving issues with tenant farmers
Wrote a treatise on monetary reform
Endeavored to defend Frombork when the Teutonic Knights attacked, requesting aid from the King of Poland and fellow chapter members
Attended the Prussian Diet to discuss the reform of silver coinage
Elected as interim bishop by the chapter members following the death of Bishop Fabian Luzjanski
Poland and the Teutonic Knights signed a final peace treaty
Began writing 'De revolutionibus orbium coelestium' (On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres)
Nearly completed his manuscript on his cosmological model
The Pope's secretary explained Copernicus's theory to the Pope and cardinals
Cardinal Schönberg wrote a letter to Copernicus urging the publication of his book
Printing of 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres' began
'De revolutionibus orbium coelestium' was published, and he died on May 24 after suffering a stroke
Remains believed to be those of Copernicus were found beneath the altar of Frombork Cathedral
A genetics expert from Uppsala University in Sweden announced that the DNA from the remains matched the DNA of hair found in his books
A reburial ceremony was held in Poland nearly 500 years after his death

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Heliocentrism
keyAchievements.description: Brought about a groundbreaking shift in modern natural science by pointing out the errors of the universally accepted geocentric model and proposing the heliocentric model.

keyAchievements.title: Published the 'Commentariolus'
keyAchievements.description: Completed the basic framework of a heliocentric astronomical system around 1510 and circulated a manuscript summary to his acquaintances.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres'
keyAchievements.description: Systematically presented the heliocentric theory by publishing a comprehensive book on his cosmological model in 1543.

keyAchievements.title: Copernican Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Brought about a paradigm shift from an anthropocentric geocentric model to an objective heliocentric model, ultimately leading to the rejection of the medieval worldview.

faq.question: Who was Copernicus?
faq.answer: He was a Polish Roman Catholic cleric and astronomer who brought about a groundbreaking shift in modern natural science by pointing out the errors of the geocentric model and advocating heliocentrism.

faq.question: What is Copernicus's heliocentric theory?
faq.answer: It is the theory that the sun is at the center of the universe and that the planets, including the Earth, revolve around the sun, which directly challenged the Ptolemaic system of the time.

faq.question: What are Copernicus's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include the 'Commentariolus', circulated among acquaintances around 1510, and his masterpiece 'De revolutionibus orbium coelestium', published in 1543.

faq.question: How were Copernicus's remains discovered?
faq.answer: In 2005, skeletal remains were found beneath the altar of Frombork Cathedral, and in 2008, DNA analysis confirmed them to be his remains.

faq.question: How was Copernicus's theory received at the time?
faq.answer: Immediately after the publication of 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres', the response was muted, but as time passed, it spread widely, and in 1616, it was placed on the Index of Forbidden Books by the Roman Catholic Church.

timeline.event: Born in Mainz.
Gutenberg's father was exiled by the citizens and fled to Strasbourg.
After his father passed away, Gutenberg also moved to Strasbourg.
Used movable metal type printing.
Returned to Mainz.
Succeeded in metal type printing and opened a printing press in Mainz.
Improved to smaller and more advanced type, and printed a 42-line Old Testament in two volumes.
Printed the 'Biblia Sacra' with 42 lines per page.
Fust filed a lawsuit against Gutenberg with the Archbishop of Mainz.
The 'Mainz Psalter' was published by the Fust-Schöffer printing press.
Death.

keyAchievements.title: Use of movable metal type printing
keyAchievements.description: A German goldsmith who used movable metal type printing around 1440.

keyAchievements.title: Type design and spread of mass production technology
keyAchievements.description: Spread type design and type mass production technology throughout Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Combination of printing technologies
keyAchievements.description: Combined movable metal type printing technology with the use of oil-based ink and wooden printing presses.

keyAchievements.title: Adoption of type manufacturing materials and methods
keyAchievements.description: Used an alloy as the material for making type and adopted a casting method.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the Gutenberg Bible
keyAchievements.description: Printed the first 36-line Latin Bible, the <Gutenberg Bible>, using Gothic type.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the 42-line Old Testament
keyAchievements.description: Around 1453, after improving the type, he printed a 42-line Old Testament in two volumes, which was highly acclaimed for its excellent printing quality.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the 'Biblia Sacra'
keyAchievements.description: In 1455, printed the 'Biblia Sacra' with 42 lines on each page.

faq.question: What is Gutenberg's real name?
faq.answer: His real name is Johannes Gensfleisch, and Gutenberg is a common appellation.

faq.question: When was Gutenberg born?
faq.answer: Gutenberg is believed to have been born in Mainz in either 1397 or 1398.

faq.question: What are Gutenberg's major achievements?
faq.answer: His major achievements include spreading type design and mass production technology in Europe, and combining this technology with oil-based ink and the use of the wooden printing press.

faq.question: What books did Gutenberg print?
faq.answer: He printed Catholic indulgences, the first 36-line Latin <Gutenberg Bible>, and the 42-line 'Biblia Sacra' in 1455.

faq.question: What impact did Gutenberg's printing press have on human history?
faq.answer: His invention allowed books to spread massively across Europe, triggering an information revolution and laying the foundation for the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the humanist movement.

timeline.event: Born in Oak Park, Illinois.
Attended Oak Park and River Forest High School.
Published his first article in the school newspaper, 'The Trapeze'.
Severely wounded by mortar fire while serving as an ambulance driver for the Italian Red Cross, and received the Silver Medal of Military Valor.
Returned to the United States and began working as a freelancer and staff writer for the 'Toronto Star Weekly'.
Married Hadley Richardson, moved to Paris, and worked as a foreign correspondent for the 'Toronto Star'.
Met Ezra Pound at Sylvia Beach's bookstore, Shakespeare and Company.
Published his first book 'Three Stories and Ten Poems' and his son Jack Hemingway was born.
Published 'In Our Time' and met F. Scott Fitzgerald.
Published his first novel 'The Sun Also Rises'.
Divorced Hadley Richardson, married Pauline Pfeiffer, and published the short story collection 'Men Without Women'.
His son Patrick was born, and he received news of his father's suicide.
Published the novel 'A Farewell to Arms'.
Broke his right arm in a car accident.
His third son, Gregory Hemingway, was born.
Went on a safari trip to Kenya with Pauline.
Purchased the fishing boat Pilar.
Published 'Green Hills of Africa'.
Traveled to Spain to cover the Spanish Civil War for the North American Newspaper Alliance, and published 'To Have and Have Not'.
Began living at the Hotel Ambos Mundos in Havana, Cuba.
Married Martha Gellhorn and published the novel 'For Whom the Bell Tolls'.
Traveled to China with Martha.
Stayed in London and other parts of Europe, and participated as a journalist in the Normandy landings and the liberation of Paris.
Divorced Martha Gellhorn.
Married Mary Welsh.
Awarded the Bronze Star Medal for his bravery during World War II.
Published the novel 'Across the River and into the Trees'.
Won the Pulitzer Prize for 'The Old Man and the Sea' and embarked on a second trip to Africa.
Survived two plane crashes in Africa and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Left Cuba and purchased a house on the outskirts of Ketchum, Idaho.
Left Cuba for New York and was admitted to the Mayo Clinic.
Returned to Ketchum after being discharged from the Mayo Clinic, and committed suicide with a shotgun on July 2.

keyAchievements.title: Stylistic innovation
keyAchievements.description: Profoundly influenced 20th-century fiction with a concise, understated writing style known as the Iceberg Theory.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Literature
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.

keyAchievements.title: Pulitzer Prize
keyAchievements.description: Won the Pulitzer Prize for 'The Old Man and the Sea' in 1952.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of major works
keyAchievements.description: Published numerous masterpieces including 'The Sun Also Rises' (1926), 'A Farewell to Arms' (1929), 'For Whom the Bell Tolls' (1940), and 'The Old Man and the Sea' (1951).

keyAchievements.title: Bronze Star Medal
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Bronze Star Medal in 1947 for his bravery during World War II.

keyAchievements.title: Literary influence
keyAchievements.description: Greatly influenced the overall style of American literature and was inducted into the Chicago Literary Hall of Fame in 2012.

faq.question: What is Ernest Hemingway's signature writing style?
faq.answer: Hemingway profoundly influenced 20th-century fiction by using a concise, understated writing style known as the 'Iceberg Theory'.

faq.question: When did Hemingway win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: Hemingway won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.

faq.question: What are some of Hemingway's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Sun Also Rises', 'A Farewell to Arms', 'For Whom the Bell Tolls', and 'The Old Man and the Sea'.

faq.question: How did Hemingway's life end?
faq.answer: On July 2, 1961, he committed suicide with a shotgun in Ketchum, Idaho.

faq.question: What are the main themes of Hemingway's works?
faq.answer: Love, war, the wilderness, and defeat frequently appear as main themes in his works, with nature often depicted as a place of rebirth and rest.

timeline.event: Born in Kiel, Holstein.
Witnessed the march of Prussian and Austrian troops into Kiel during the Second Schleswig War.
Family moved to Munich, and he enrolled in the Maximiliansgymnasium.
Began his studies at the University of Munich.
Studied for a year at the Friedrich Wilhelm University in Berlin.
Defended his doctoral dissertation 'On the Second Law of Thermodynamics'.
Earned his habilitation by presenting the thesis 'Equilibrium States of Isotropic Bodies at Different Temperatures'.
Appointed associate professor of theoretical physics at the University of Kiel.
Married Marie Merck.
Appointed as the successor to Kirchhoff's position at the Friedrich Wilhelm University of Berlin.
Became a full professor.
Turned his attention to the problem of black-body radiation.
Published 'Treatise on Thermodynamics'.
Contributed to the formation of the German Physical Society (DPG).
Presented a new derivation of Planck's law of black-body radiation, including the energy quantization hypothesis.
Served as president of the German Physical Society (DPG) (until 1909).
His first wife, Marie Planck, died.
Married his second wife, Marga von Hoesslin.
Successfully brought Einstein to Berlin and created a new professorship for him.
His eldest son, Karl, was killed in action at the Battle of Verdun.
His daughter, Grete, died while giving birth to her first child.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta (actually awarded in 1919).
His daughter, Emma, died.
Founded the Emergency Association of German Science (Notgemeinschaft der Deutschen Wissenschaft) along with Fritz Haber.
Retired in Berlin, and Erwin Schrödinger succeeded him.
Became president of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG).
Was 74 years old when the Nazis took power, and discussed the treatment of Jewish professors with Hitler.
His term as president of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG) ended.
Celebrated his 80th birthday, and resigned as president of the Prussian Academy when it was taken over by the Nazis.
His home in Berlin was destroyed in a bombing raid, resulting in the loss of all his scientific records and correspondence.
His son Erwin was executed for his involvement in the assassination attempt on Hitler.
Died in Göttingen.
The Kaiser Wilhelm Society was renamed the Max Planck Society (MPG).

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of energy quanta
keyAchievements.description: Won the 1918 Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta, which revolutionized the understanding of atomic and subatomic processes.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of quantum theory
keyAchievements.description: His reputation as a physicist rests primarily on his role as the originator of quantum theory, a revolutionary idea incompatible with classical physics.

keyAchievements.title: Planck's law of black-body radiation
keyAchievements.description: Derived Planck's law of black-body radiation, which accurately described the experimentally observed black-body spectrum, through his research on the black-body radiation problem.

keyAchievements.title: Planck constant (h)
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of quantum theory by introducing the Planck constant (h) through the hypothesis that electromagnetic energy could only be emitted in quantized form.

keyAchievements.title: Early recognition of and contribution to the theory of relativity
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the few to immediately recognize the significance of Albert Einstein's special theory of relativity, and contributed to its reformulation.

keyAchievements.title: Leadership in German scientific institutions
keyAchievements.description: Held key positions in the German scientific community, serving as president of the German Physical Society (DPG) and the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG).

faq.question: What major scientific discovery did Max Planck make?
faq.answer: Max Planck discovered energy quanta and founded quantum theory, revolutionizing the understanding of atomic and subatomic processes.

faq.question: When did Max Planck win the Nobel Prize in Physics?
faq.answer: He was awarded the 1918 Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta, though he actually received it in 1919.

faq.question: What was Max Planck's stance regarding the Nazi regime?
faq.answer: He tried to assist his Jewish colleagues and preserve the independence of science; he met with Hitler to discuss the treatment of Jewish professors and resigned from the presidency of the Prussian Academy in protest against the Nazis.

faq.question: What were Max Planck's religious views?
faq.answer: He was a German Lutheran who believed in an almighty, omniscient, and benevolent God (though not necessarily a personal one), and viewed both science and religion as having the goal of 'Toward God!'.

timeline.event: Born on June 23
Started majoring in mathematics at the University of Cambridge
Took physicist Eddington's scientific methodology class and proved the central limit theorem
Left for Princeton University after providing a negative proof to the decidability problem
Finished studies at Princeton University and returned to Cambridge
Joined the Government Code and Cypher School (GCCS) during World War II and started cryptography research
Devised the Turing machine; pushed for the creation of a mathematics department to build an electronic calculator at the National Physical Laboratory (NPL) on October 1
Awarded the Officer of the Order of the British Empire (OBE)
Published a paper titled 'Can Machines Think?' in the philosophy journal 'Mind'
Arrested and convicted by British police on charges of homosexuality
Published a paper on the Riemann zeta function
Died of potassium cyanide poisoning on June 7
Queen Elizabeth II pardoned Turing's homosexuality conviction on December 24

keyAchievements.title: Devised the Turing machine
keyAchievements.description: Formalized the concepts of algorithm and computation through an abstract model called the Turing machine, making a profound contribution to the development of computer science.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Turing test
keyAchievements.description: Proposed an experiment to evaluate how human-like a machine's answers are with the proposition 'Can machines think?', achieving great success.

keyAchievements.title: Cracked the Enigma code
keyAchievements.description: Successfully cracked the German codes during World War II by introducing a code-breaking machine called the Bombe.

keyAchievements.title: Proved the central limit theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved it in 1933 without knowing that Finnish mathematician Jarl Waldemar Lindeberg had already proved it in 1922.

keyAchievements.title: Negative proof of the decidability problem
keyAchievements.description: Provided a negative proof to the decidability problem in 1936.

faq.question: What fields was Alan Turing a pioneer in?
faq.answer: He was an English computer scientist, mathematician, cryptanalyst, logician, and a pioneering figure in computer science.

faq.question: What is the purpose of the Turing Award?
faq.answer: It is an annual prize given by the ACM to those who have made significant contributions to computer science, established in 1966 to honor Turing's contributions.

faq.question: Why is Alan Turing called the 'father of computer science'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'father of computer science' because he made profound contributions to theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence.

faq.question: When was Alan Turing pardoned for his homosexuality conviction?
faq.answer: Queen Elizabeth II pardoned his homosexuality conviction on December 24, 2013, 59 years after his death.

timeline.event: Born in Afshana near Bukhara as the child of Abdullah ibn Al-Hasan, a tax collector of the Samanid Empire, and Sitara.
At the age of five, his family moved to Bukhara, and he entered a private school in Bukhara.
By the age of ten, he could already recite the Quran and many Arabic literary works from memory.
Started treating patients at the age of sixteen.
Studied on his own at eighteen and came to understand Aristotelian philosophy through Al-Farabi's commentaries.
At the age of eighteen, he wrote his first work, 'The Compendium of Various Sciences', for his neighbor Al-Aruzi.
The Samanid Empire fell due to attacks by the Ghaznavids and the Karakhanids.
His father Abdullah passed away.
At twenty-one, he wrote and presented the 20-volume encyclopedia 'The Book of Fair Judgment' for the jurist Al-Barqi.
Left Bukhara at twenty-two and embarked on a wandering journey.
Was employed by Mamun II, the ruler of Urgench in the Khwarezm region, became an official, and started writing 'The Canon of Medicine'.
Moved to Rey near Tehran and wrote about 30 short essays.
Completed 'The Canon of Medicine', which he had been writing for some time.
After Shams al-Dawla passed away, Ibn Sina abandoned his official post and went into seclusion.
Was imprisoned by Sama al-Dawla, the new ruler of Hamadan.
Isfahan was attacked by Mas'ud I, the ruler of the Ghaznavid Empire, and Ibn Sina's belongings, including his collection of books, were looted.
Died in Hamadan.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of philosophy and medicine
keyAchievements.description: Compiled Greek and Arabic philosophy and medicine, and had a profound impact on European medicine and philosophy by integrating Aristotelian philosophy and Neoplatonism.

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Canon of Medicine
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'The Canon of Medicine', which compiled all known medical knowledge of the time and was used as a standard medical text in Europe until the 17th century.

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Book of Healing
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'The Book of Healing', an encyclopedia covering all fields of study except ethics and politics.

keyAchievements.title: Natural science research
keyAchievements.description: Denied the possibility of alchemical transmutation of metals, and researched various fields of natural science including mathematics, physics, astronomy, and mineralogy.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European academia
keyAchievements.description: Influenced Thomas Aquinas and became the catalyst for the revival of Aristotelianism in Europe in the late 12th century.

faq.question: How is Ibn Sina evaluated as a figure?
faq.answer: A Persian philosopher, physician, and scientist, he is regarded as one of the greatest medical scholars of the Middle Ages and the finest intellectual produced by the Islamic world.

faq.question: What is Ibn Sina's major medical work?
faq.answer: He authored 'The Canon of Medicine', which compiled all known medical knowledge of the time, and this book was used as the basic textbook of European medicine until the 17th century.

faq.question: What philosophical contributions did Ibn Sina make?
faq.answer: He compiled Greek and Arabic philosophy, integrated Aristotelian philosophy and Neoplatonism, and greatly influenced European medicine and philosophy.

faq.question: What was Ibn Sina's stance on alchemy?
faq.answer: He denied the possibility of metal transmutation believed by alchemists of the time, arguing that the differences between metals are profound and that precious metals created through alchemy are merely imitations.

timeline.event: Born in Paris as the first son of Jean-Antoine Lavoisier and Emilie Lavoisier.
Graduated from College Mazarin.
Obtained a bachelor's degree in law.
Became a member of the French Academy of Sciences and joined the Ferme Generale, a tax collection agency.
Became famous by publishing a paper concluding that water cannot be transmuted into earth.
Married Marie-Anne Pierrette Paulze.
Published 'Opuscules Physiques et chimiques' (Physical and Chemical Essays).
Appointed head of the Gunpowder Administration by Finance Minister Anne Robert Jacques Turgot.
Revealed that water is produced when hydrogen is burned, in cooperation with Pierre-Simon Laplace.
Became a director of the French Academy of Sciences.
Published 'Methode de nomenclature chimique' (Method of Chemical Nomenclature) by reforming chemical nomenclature together with Claude Louis Berthollet and others.
Published 'Traite Elementaire de Chimie' (Elementary Treatise on Chemistry).
Participated in the creation of a unified system of weights and measures commissioned by the National Assembly.
Numerous academies, including the French Academy of Sciences, were closed.
Imprisoned along with his father-in-law Jacques Paulze.
Sentenced to death and beheaded at the guillotine in the Place de la Revolution.
Marie-Anne Lavoisier was officially able to receive Lavoisier's confiscated belongings back.

keyAchievements.title: Led the Chemical Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Led the 18th-century chemical revolution, leaving a mark in the history of chemistry and biology.

keyAchievements.title: Abolished the phlogiston theory and established the theory of combustion
keyAchievements.description: Advanced chemistry significantly by advocating a new theory of combustion, discarding the phlogiston theory, and identifying the role of oxygen.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Law of Conservation of Mass
keyAchievements.description: Established the law of conservation of mass, stating that matter is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions, but only changes form.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for modern chemical nomenclature
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of modern chemistry by distinguishing between elements and compounds and leading a systematic reform of chemical nomenclature.

keyAchievements.title: Introduced quantitative methods and compiled a list of elements
keyAchievements.description: Introduced quantitative methods to chemistry for the first time, compiled the first extensive list of elements, and predicted the existence of silicon.

faq.question: For what reason was Antoine Lavoisier executed?
faq.answer: During the height of the French Revolution, he was indicted for tax fraud and selling adulterated tobacco, and despite his contributions to science, he was executed by the guillotine.

faq.question: What is Lavoisier's most important scientific achievement?
faq.answer: He led the chemical revolution by discarding the phlogiston theory and identifying the role of oxygen in combustion and respiration, established the law of conservation of mass, and laid the foundation for modern chemical nomenclature.

faq.question: How did Lavoisier's wife, Marie-Anne Paulze Lavoisier, contribute to her husband's research?
faq.answer: As a laboratory assistant, Marie-Anne helped by drawing laboratory equipment, recording results, translating English papers, and she also participated in the development of the metric measurement system.

timeline.event: Born on July 3 in the 13th year of Zhengde of the Ming Dynasty
Began the work of compiling standard medical texts at the age of 35
Died in the 21st year of Wanli of the Ming Dynasty (at the age of 76)
Publication of the 'Compendium of Materia Medica' (after Li Shizhen's death)

keyAchievements.title: Authored the Compendium of Materia Medica
keyAchievements.description: Authored the 'Compendium of Materia Medica' (Bencao Gangmu), which integrated herbal medicine and had a profound influence on subsequent Eastern medicine and natural history.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to acupuncture and diagnostics
keyAchievements.description: Also contributed to the fields of acupuncture and diagnostics.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Binhu Maixue
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Binhu Maixue', a book on pulse diagnosis.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Qijing Bamai Kao
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Qijing Bamai Kao', a book on the eight extraordinary meridians.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Wuzang Tulun
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Wuzang Tulun', a book on the five internal organs, which is currently lost.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Mingmen Kao
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Mingmen Kao', a book on the 'Gate of Life' (Mingmen), which is currently lost.

faq.question: When was Li Shizhen born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen was born on July 3, 1518, the 13th year of Zhengde of the Ming Dynasty, and died in 1593, the 21st year of Wanli of the Ming Dynasty, at the age of 76.

faq.question: What are Li Shizhen's major works?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen's major work is the 'Compendium of Materia Medica', which integrated herbal medicine. In addition, he authored 'Binhu Maixue', 'Qijing Bamai Kao', and others.

faq.question: What positions did Li Shizhen hold?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen served as a physician for the Prince of Chu, became an imperial physician in the royal court, and later held the position of magistrate of Huanggang, Hubei in the Ming Dynasty.

faq.question: What was Li Shizhen's family background?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen's grandfather and father were doctors. His father's name was Li Yanwen, his courtesy name was Ziyu, and his pseudonym was Yuechi.

timeline.event: Born in Tus, Khorasan.
Abandoned his prestigious positions due to religious anguish and other causes, and traveled to Mecca and various regions as a Sufi ascetic.
Retired to his hometown and passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a book criticizing some philosophers for excessively transplanting Greek philosophy into Islam, holding thoughts that contradict the Quran.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences' and 'Deliverance from Error'
keyAchievements.description: Left behind masterworks such as 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences' and 'Deliverance from Error' which had a great influence on future generations.

keyAchievements.title: Harmonization of orthodox theology and Sufism
keyAchievements.description: Made his greatest contribution by harmonizing orthodox theology and Sufism, breathing new life into the Islamic religious system.

keyAchievements.title: Defended orthodox Islam
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to adhering to orthodox Islam by criticizing Ibn Sina's philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Provided a turning point for Islamic scholarship
keyAchievements.description: Brought a turning point to Islamic religious thought by breaking away Islamic scholarship from scholastic theology and reviving the Quran and Hadith.

keyAchievements.title: Established the status of Sufism
keyAchievements.description: Established the status of Sufism within Islam, making the masses of Muslims accept Sufism.

faq.question: How is Al-Ghazali evaluated?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the greatest Muslim after Muhammad, and is a significant figure in the fields of theology, philosophy, and Sufism.

faq.question: What are Al-Ghazali's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers', 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences', and 'Deliverance from Error'.

faq.question: What is Al-Ghazali's greatest contribution?
faq.answer: His greatest contribution is the harmonization of orthodox theology and Sufism, and the defense of orthodox Islam by criticizing the philosophy of Ibn Sina.

faq.question: What turning point did Al-Ghazali bring to Islamic religious thought?
faq.answer: He broke Islamic scholarship away from scholastic theology, reintroduced esoteric elements, established the status of Sufism, and made philosophy more accessible to ordinary Muslims.

timeline.event: Born in Heian-kyo, Japan
Went to Echizen Province with her father when he was appointed as the provincial governor
Returned to Kyoto and married Fujiwara no Nobutaka
Daughter Kenshi (Kataiko) was born
Husband Nobutaka died of cholera
Wrote 'The Tale of Genji'
Invited to the imperial court by Fujiwara no Michinaga to become a lady-in-waiting to Empress Shoshi
Court lady Fujiwara no Kyoko is mentioned in the imperial diary (presumed to be Murasaki's real name)
Died
A 6-volume English translation of 'The Tale of Genji' was completed

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Tale of Genji'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the Japanese novel 'The Tale of Genji', which is considered one of the first novels.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Diary of Lady Murasaki'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the poetry collection 'The Diary of Lady Murasaki'.

keyAchievements.title: Learning and teaching Chinese literature
keyAchievements.description: Learned Chinese literature, which was taboo for women in the Heian period, under her scholar father, and taught Chinese literature to Empress Shoshi.

faq.question: What is Murasaki Shikibu's real name?
faq.answer: Murasaki Shikibu is a pen name. Her real name is unknown, but it is presumed to be Fujiwara no Kyoko, a court lady mentioned in the imperial diary in 1007.

faq.question: How was Murasaki Shikibu able to learn Chinese literature?
faq.answer: Although women in the Heian period were traditionally not allowed to learn Chinese, she grew up under her scholar father and showed a great aptitude for it, becoming fluent in writing it.

faq.question: When was 'The Tale of Genji' written?
faq.answer: 'The Tale of Genji' was written between 1000 and 1012.

faq.question: When and why did Murasaki Shikibu become a court lady?
faq.answer: Murasaki Shikibu was invited by Fujiwara no Michinaga to be a lady-in-waiting to Empress Shoshi in her early to mid-thirties due to her reputation as a writer.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Sei Shonagon and Murasaki Shikibu like?
faq.answer: Murasaki Shikibu perceived Sei Shonagon as a rival and critically evaluated Shonagon's arrogance and showing off of her Chinese knowledge.

timeline.event: Born as the daughter of Li Gefei
Married Zhao Mingcheng
First separation from Li Qingzhao as Zhao Mingcheng was implicated in the Yuanyou faction incident
Fall of the Northern Song regime
Bereaved of Zhao Mingcheng
Remarried to Zhang Ruzhou
Died

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of Jinshi Lu (Records of Bronze and Stone)
keyAchievements.description: Researched bronze and stone inscriptions and paintings with her husband Zhao Mingcheng and compiled 'Jinshi Lu'.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Song Ci (Song dynasty poetry)
keyAchievements.description: Showcased brilliant expressions full of wit with elegant works that followed the traditions of Li Yu and Yan Jidao.

keyAchievements.title: Critiques on Song dynasty Ci poets
keyAchievements.description: Known for providing apt critiques of Song dynasty Ci poets.

faq.question: Who was Li Qingzhao?
faq.answer: Li Qingzhao was a Chinese poet of the Southern Song dynasty, also known by her pseudonym Yi'an Jushi, born in 1081 during the late Northern Song period.

faq.question: What are Li Qingzhao's major achievements?
faq.answer: She compiled 'Jinshi Lu' with her husband Zhao Mingcheng, and achieved literary fame by creating Song Ci known for their elegant and witty expressions.

faq.question: How did Li Qingzhao's life change?
faq.answer: Her life is divided into early and late periods marked by the fall of the Northern Song. In the early period, she wrote happy and romantic works, while in the late period, she wrote works strongly expressing the historical and personal tragedies of the era.

timeline.event: Birth of Francois-Dominique Toussaint (Toussaint Louverture)
Hearing news of the French Revolution, black slaves demand the application of the principles of 'Liberty, Equality, Fraternity' in Saint-Domingue
A slave revolution breaks out in the French colony of Saint-Domingue, and Toussaint becomes one of its leaders
Leads to an agreement where France abolishes slavery in the colony
Successfully defends against invasions by British and Spanish forces
The National Convention of the French First Republic declares the abolition of black slavery in all colonies
Governs the colony with the consent of the French governor
Enacts a constitution to permanently abolish slavery and declares that all people are equal regardless of skin color
Napoleon I sends a large army to regain control of the colony and reinstate slavery
Toussaint is captured after agreeing to attend peace talks
Dies in prison while incarcerated in France
The people, inheriting Toussaint's spirit, liberate Port-au-Prince and adopt a declaration of independence
The Republic of Haiti is declared

keyAchievements.title: Led the Haitian Independence Movement
keyAchievements.description: Ended slavery and led the movement for the independence of Saint-Domingue (now Haiti) from France.

keyAchievements.title: Served as French General and Colonial Ruler
keyAchievements.description: Served as a general in the French army and colonial ruler of Saint-Domingue.

keyAchievements.title: Led the Slave Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the leaders of the slave revolution that occurred in Saint-Domingue in 1791.

keyAchievements.title: Led negotiations for the abolition of slavery
keyAchievements.description: Led an agreement in which France abolished slavery in the colony on the condition that he defeated the British and Spanish forces.

keyAchievements.title: Enacted Constitution and declared abolition of slavery
keyAchievements.description: Enacted a constitution in 1801 that abolished slavery forever and declared all people equal regardless of skin color.

faq.question: Who was Toussaint Louverture?
faq.answer: He was a Haitian revolutionary and independence fighter who ended slavery and led the independence movement of Saint-Domingue (now Haiti) from France.

faq.question: What is Toussaint Louverture's real name?
faq.answer: It is Francois-Dominique Toussaint.

faq.question: How did Toussaint Louverture become a revolutionary?
faq.answer: He became a revolutionary by teaching himself French and reading the books of Enlightenment thinkers such as Voltaire.

faq.question: When did the slave revolution led by Toussaint Louverture occur?
faq.answer: It occurred on August 22, 1791, in the French colony of Saint-Domingue.

faq.question: How did Toussaint Louverture die?
faq.answer: In late 1802, he was captured after responding to a peace talk proposal from the French commander, was imprisoned in France, and died in prison on April 7, 1803.

faq.question: What happened to Haiti after Toussaint Louverture's death?
faq.answer: Jean-Jacques Dessalines became a revolutionary and inherited Toussaint's spirit, liberated Port-au-Prince and adopted a declaration of independence in 1803, and declared the Republic of Haiti in 1804.

timeline.event: Went to study in Spain to become a doctor
Completed the final draft of the novel 'Noli Me Tangere' in Berlin in February
Formed La Liga Filipina, a leading organization for the Philippine independence movement
Arrested by the Spanish colonial government during La Liga Filipina activities and exiled to Dapitan in Mindanao
Identified as the mastermind behind the Philippine Revolution and publicly executed by firing squad by the Spanish colonial army in Bagumbayan near Manila on December 30

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Noli Me Tangere'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote the novel 'Noli Me Tangere', which criticized the contradictions of colonial rule and depicted the discrimination of Filipinos by Spaniards.

keyAchievements.title: Formed the Philippine National League
keyAchievements.description: Formed the Philippine National League to criticize Spanish colonial rule out of a desire for Philippine liberation.

keyAchievements.title: Formed La Liga Filipina
keyAchievements.description: Directed the independence movement by forming La Liga Filipina, the leading organization for the Philippine independence movement.

faq.question: Who was Jose Rizal?
faq.answer: He was a doctor, writer, poet, journalist, educator, and a Philippine independence activist.

faq.question: Why was Jose Rizal executed?
faq.answer: He was publicly executed by firing squad by the Spanish colonial army after being identified as the mastermind behind the 1896 Philippine Revolution.

faq.question: What are Jose Rizal's representative works?
faq.answer: He is the author of 'Noli Me Tangere' and 'El Filibusterismo', and left many poems and essays.

faq.question: How is Jose Rizal remembered in the Philippines?
faq.answer: He is revered as the father of Philippine independence, and December 30, the day of his death, is commemorated as Rizal Day.

timeline.event: Born as the second child of Edward John Wollstonecraft and Elizabeth Dickson in Spitalfields, London
Became independent and worked as a companion to Sarah Dawson in Bath, Somerset
Returned home to care for her mother on her deathbed
Persuaded her sister Eliza, who was suffering from postpartum depression, to emancipate herself from her household
Left her school and followed her friend Fanny Blood to Lisbon to nurse her
Published 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters'; decided to become an author
Published her first novel 'Mary: A Fiction'; published her only children's book 'Original Stories from Real Life'
Wrote 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' in response to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France'
Wrote her most famous and influential work 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'; left for Paris in December and returned a month before Louis XVI's execution
France declared war on Britain
The Jacobin-led Committee of Public Safety seized power in France
Fell in love with American adventurer Gilbert Imlay; Imlay falsely registered Wollstonecraft as his wife to obtain American citizenship
Gave birth to her first daughter, Fanny Imlay
The Jacobins fell from power
'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution', written in Le Havre, Northern France, was published in London
Left France
Returned to London looking for Imlay, but Imlay refused to reconcile
Attempted suicide with laudanum but was saved by Imlay
Letters written to Imlay during her Scandinavian trip were compiled and published as 'Letters Written During a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark'
Moved to Somers Town, northwest London, with William Godwin
Gave birth to her second daughter, Mary
Died of puerperal fever (at age 38)
Her unfinished work 'Maria' was published posthumously
Her husband William Godwin published 'Memoirs of the Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman' (1792)
keyAchievements.description: The first feminist philosophical work arguing that women are not naturally inferior to men, but appear to be only because they lack education, advocating for women's right to education and equal fundamental rights.

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' (1790)
keyAchievements.description: A rebuttal to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France', and the first political and feminist writing to attack aristocracy, defend republicanism, and criticize Burke's gender-biased language.

keyAchievements.title: 'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution' (1794)
keyAchievements.description: A book that outlined the early history of the French Revolution, countered the counter-revolutionary atmosphere in Britain, and argued that the revolution was the result of social, economic, and political crises.

keyAchievements.title: 'Letters Written in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark' (1796)
keyAchievements.description: A travelogue containing reflections on the society and people of Scandinavia, questions of identity, and her relationship with Imlay, exploring the value of subjective experience and imagination, and advocating for women's liberation and education.

keyAchievements.title: Educational writings
keyAchievements.description: Argued through 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters' (1787) and children's literature 'Original Stories from Real Life' (1788) that children should be taught to think and receive an education that fosters middle-class ethos.

faq.question: What is Mary Wollstonecraft's most famous book?
faq.answer: Her most famous book is 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman', published in 1792.

faq.question: What did Mary Wollstonecraft argue about women's education?
faq.answer: She argued that women are not inherently inferior to men, but only appear to be due to a lack of education, and therefore should receive quality education.

faq.question: Are any of Mary Wollstonecraft's daughters famous?
faq.answer: Yes, her second daughter, Mary Wollstonecraft Godwin, later married Percy Bysshe Shelley and became Mary Shelley, the author of the novel 'Frankenstein'.

faq.question: With which philosophers did Mary Wollstonecraft associate?
faq.answer: She associated with radical writer Thomas Paine, philosopher William Godwin, and others, and notably married William Godwin.

faq.question: What was Mary Wollstonecraft's stance on the French Revolution?
faq.answer: She condemned the Jacobin regime and the Reign of Terror, but maintained the position that the revolution was a great achievement.

timeline.event: Birth
Volunteered as an envoy to form an alliance with the Dayuezhi against the Xiongnu for the Han Dynasty and departed for the Western Regions
Returned to the Han Dynasty
Contributed to Emperor Wu's attack on the Xiongnu and was appointed Weiwei (Commandant of Guards) and Marquis of Bowang
Demoted to commoner after being held responsible for missing the deadline for a military campaign
Dispatched as an envoy to Wusun
Death

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered the Silk Road
keyAchievements.description: Made a significant contribution to the pioneering of the Silk Road.

keyAchievements.title: Transmitted knowledge of the Western Regions
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to establishing new strategies by transmitting valuable knowledge about the Western Regions to the Han Dynasty.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to attacks against the Xiongnu
keyAchievements.description: Made significant contributions during Emperor Wu's attacks against the Xiongnu in 123 BC by utilizing his experience and geographical knowledge.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed alliance with Wusun
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for improved relations between the Han Dynasty and Wusun by proposing a marriage alliance and eastward migration.

faq.question: Who was Zhang Qian?
faq.answer: Zhang Qian was a traveler, diplomat, and explorer during the Han Dynasty of China in the 2nd century BC, who made significant contributions to pioneering the Silk Road.

faq.question: What was the main purpose of Zhang Qian's dispatch to the Western Regions?
faq.answer: The Han Dynasty recruited an envoy to form an alliance with the Dayuezhi against the Xiongnu, and Zhang Qian volunteered and headed for the Dayuezhi.

faq.question: What was the biggest outcome Zhang Qian's exploration of the Western Regions brought to the Han Dynasty?
faq.answer: Although the alliance was not established, the valuable knowledge about the Western Regions brought by Zhang Qian contributed significantly to the Han Dynasty formulating new strategies.

faq.question: What items is Zhang Qian said to have brought back from the Western Regions?
faq.answer: Legends exist that he brought back items such as grapes, pomegranates, and peaches from the Western Regions.

timeline.event: Born to Pepin the Short and Bertrada of Laon in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.
His father, Pepin the Short, became King of the Franks with the approval of Pope Zachary.
Pope Stephen II bestowed the title of King on Pepin and his sons Charles and Carloman.
Succeeded to the throne after the death of his father Pepin the Short, taking over Austrasia and the northern regions.
Became sole ruler after the death of his brother Carloman I.
Began attacking the Saxons.
Destroyed and annexed the Lombard Kingdom in northern Italy.
Attended the Easter festival in Rome with the Pope and reaffirmed the promise to transfer the Papal States.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the Basques at the Battle of Roncevaux Pass during an expedition to the Iberian Peninsula.
Visited Rome, had his two sons Pepin and Louis consecrated, and bestowed upon them the crowns of Lombardy and Aquitaine respectively.
His wife Hildegard and mother Bertrada of Laon passed away successively, and he remarried Fastrada.
