Conquered Thuringia and Bavaria, and deposed Duke Tassilo III of Bavaria.
Soundly defeated the Avars along the Danube River and drove them beyond the borders.
Built a palace and royal church in Aachen, northwestern Germany, making it the de facto capital.
Pope Leo III, attacked by opponents, fled to Charlemagne's court to seek assistance.
Was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III in St. Peter's Basilica.
Louis the Pious captured Barcelona.
The Avar Khagan Zodan visited Aachen, swore fealty to Charlemagne, and converted to Christianity.
Croatian chiefs, including Viseslav, voluntarily surrendered.
Eastern Roman Emperor Michael I Rangabe recognized Charlemagne as Emperor.
Declared his surviving son Louis I the Pious as the legitimate successor at the court in Aachen and crowned him co-emperor.
Died of complications from pleurisy and depression at the Palace of Aachen.
Pope Paschal III canonized Charlemagne as a saint at the request of Frederick I.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Frankish Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: Greatly expanded the Frankish Kingdom by conquering most of western and central Europe, achieving the political unification of Western Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Crowned Emperor of the Romans
keyAchievements.description: Received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800, politically and religiously unifying most of Western Europe for the first time since the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Carolingian Renaissance
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the Carolingian Renaissance by greatly developing arts, religion, and culture through the church during his reign.

keyAchievements.title: Codification of laws and centralization
keyAchievements.description: Codified the laws of various tribes to rule over vast territories and enabled centralized rule by dispatching officials from the center.

keyAchievements.title: Spread and integration of Christianity
keyAchievements.description: Sought ideological unity by converting conquered tribes to Catholicism and supported proselytization by dispatching missionaries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of cultural and educational institutions
keyAchievements.description: Led a revival of learning and arts by building a palace and royal church in Aachen, and founding a royal library and palace school.

faq.question: When was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: There are theories that Charlemagne was born on April 2 of 740 or 742, or in 747.

faq.question: Why is Charlemagne called the 'Father of Europe'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'Father of Europe' because the Carolingian Renaissance laid the foundation for the current identity of Europe.

faq.question: When did Charlemagne become Emperor of the Romans?
faq.answer: Charlemagne received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800.

faq.question: How tall was Charlemagne?
faq.answer: According to Einhard's records and the results of a tomb excavation in 1861, Charlemagne was about 190 to 191 cm tall.

faq.question: What languages did Charlemagne speak?
faq.answer: In addition to his mother tongue, he was fluent in Latin and could also speak some Greek.

faq.question: Where was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: Charlemagne was born in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.

timeline.event: Reigned as the ninth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco and the first Emperor of the Inca Empire.
Recognized as the successor to the throne for his merit in defending Cusco during the Chanka invasion.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for the Inca Empire
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for developing the small Kingdom of Cusco into the massive Inca Empire ruling all of western South America.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of imperial territory
keyAchievements.description: During his reign, Collasuyu and Chinchaysuyu, two of the four quarters of the Inca Empire, were brought under the empire's control.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion and overhaul of the capital Cusco
keyAchievements.description: Transformed Cusco from a quiet provincial city into a place fitting as the capital of the empire through massive expansion.

keyAchievements.title: Renovation of Coricancha
keyAchievements.description: Overhauled various buildings of the Inca Empire era, including the renovation of Coricancha, the Temple of the Sun.

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of forced resettlement policy
keyAchievements.description: Implemented a colonization policy of forcibly moving residents from the center of the empire to borderlands to pioneer frontier regions.

faq.question: What does Pachacuti's name mean?
faq.answer: In Quechua, it means 'He who shakes time and space' or 'Earth Shaker', and 'Yupanqui' means 'honor'.

faq.question: What was Pachacuti's position in the Inca Empire?
faq.answer: He was the ninth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco and the first Emperor of the Inca Empire.

faq.question: Who was Pachacuti's father?
faq.answer: His father was Viracocha Inca.

faq.question: How did Pachacuti become the successor to the throne?
faq.answer: He was recognized as the successor to the throne around 1438 for his merit in defending Cusco alone when his father and brother fled during the Chanka invasion.

faq.question: Who is presumed to have built Machu Picchu?
faq.answer: Most archaeologists presume that Pachacuti built Machu Picchu.

timeline.event: Born on the island of Samos in Asia Minor
Driven out of Mytilene after giving lectures, due to backlash
Founded 'The Garden' in Athens as the meeting place of his school
Died from kidney stones

keyAchievements.title: Founded Epicureanism
keyAchievements.description: Founded the philosophical school of Epicureanism.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the purpose of philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Believed that the purpose of philosophy is to attain a happy and tranquil life.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Hedonistic philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Argued for atomistic materialism and hedonism in ethics, becoming the founder of hedonistic philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Established the concepts of Ataraxia and Aponia
keyAchievements.description: Proposed ataraxia (peace and freedom from fear) and aponia (the absence of pain) as the characteristics of a happy and tranquil life.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed a perspective on death
keyAchievements.description: Taught that death is the end of the body and soul, and therefore should not be feared.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed a perspective on gods
keyAchievements.description: Argued that gods neither punish nor reward humans and do not concern themselves with human affairs.

keyAchievements.title: Developed atomistic materialism
keyAchievements.description: Explained that all phenomena in the world result from the movements and interactions of atoms moving in empty space.

keyAchievements.title: Introduced the concept of free will
keyAchievements.description: Stated that atomic motion is not just straight lines but occasionally swerves, thereby avoiding determinism and granting free will.

keyAchievements.title: Introduced the idea of human equality
keyAchievements.description: Introduced the fundamental idea of human equality to the Greeks by accepting women and slaves into his school.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the principle of multiple explanations
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the 'principle of multiple explanations', contributing to the philosophy of science.

faq.question: What was the core purpose of Epicurean philosophy?
faq.answer: For Epicurus, the purpose of philosophy was to attain a happy and tranquil life characterized by ataraxia (peace and freedom from fear) and aponia (absence of pain).

faq.question: What did Epicurus teach about death?
faq.answer: Epicurus taught that death is the end of the body and soul and should not be feared. He stated that when we exist, death is not, and when death exists, we do not; therefore, death is nothing to us.

faq.question: What were the characteristics of the Epicurean school?
faq.answer: The Epicurean school was the first to officially accept women. It regarded friendship as an important ingredient for happiness and supported seclusion over political participation.

faq.question: What does pleasure mean to Epicurus?
faq.answer: Epicurus viewed pleasure as the absence of pain, and pursued continuous, static pleasure rather than momentary, physical pleasure.

timeline.event: Born on September 22 as the third of four children to James Faraday and Margaret in Newington.
Became an apprentice to George Riebau, a bookseller, at the age of 13.
Signed a seven-year contract as George Riebau's apprentice on August 7.
Read Jane Marcet's 'Conversations on Chemistry' and the 3rd edition of the Encyclopædia Britannica by James Tytler.
Began taking notes on articles about art and science from newspapers and magazines.
Received tickets to Humphry Davy's chemistry lectures and attended them; wrote a letter asking Davy for help.
Started working at the Royal Institution in March; began a European tour as Davy's secretary and assistant on October 13.
Traveled to Naples, Italy, climbed Mount Vesuvius, and met Alessandro Volta in Pavia.
Returned to England on April 23; re-employed as superintendent and assistant of the apparatus and mineralogical collections at the Royal Institution's laboratory in May.
Married Sarah Barnard on June 12; published an article on electromagnetic rotation in 'Annals of Philosophy'.
Left the note 'Convert magnetism into electricity' in his laboratory notebook.
Successfully liquefied chlorine.
Discovered benzene; commissioned by the Royal Society to research the optical properties of glass used for telescopes.
Determined the molecular structure of naphthalene and created two sample crystals; established the 'Christmas Lectures for Children'.
Published a paper including practical guidance on chemistry in April; began optical glass experiments with a special furnace provided by the Royal Institution.
Announced the success of his optical glass research at the Bakerian Lecture of the Royal Society.
Began electromagnetic experiments on August 29 and discovered electromagnetic induction.
Began 'Electrochemical Decomposition' research at the end of the year and discovered Faraday's first law.
Experimented with various dissolved substances in a voltameter at the end of the year and discovered Faraday's second law.
Discovered that the charge on a charged conductor resides only on its exterior surface.
Published Volume 1 of 'Experimental Researches in Electricity'.
Successfully liquefied many substances including ammonia and carbon dioxide.
Met William Thomson at Cambridge University in June; discovered the Faraday effect on September 18, defining a physical relationship between magnetism and light.
Began experiments to investigate changes in diamagnetic materials influenced by magnets, and mentioned the concept of a magnetic field.
Granted residence in a house near Hampton Court along the River Thames in London at the request of Queen Victoria.
Published 'Experimental Researches in Chemistry and Physics'.

Submitted research results on gravity and electricity to the 'Philosophical Transactions' but was advised to withdraw them; gave a lecture on 'The Chemical History of a Candle' at the Royal Institution's Christmas Lectures.
Began suffering from memory loss symptoms; gave his final lecture at Christmas.
Conducted an experiment on whether the spectrum of sodium and other metals heated by a flame is affected by an electromagnetic field.
Passed away on August 25.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Electromagnetic Induction
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the principles of electromagnetic induction, diamagnetism, and the laws of electrolysis, and invented an electromagnetic rotary device that formed the foundation of the electric motor.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Benzene and Introduction of Chemical Terms
keyAchievements.description: Discovered benzene as a chemist, investigated the clathrate hydrate of chlorine, and introduced technical terms such as anode, cathode, electrode, and ion for the first time.

keyAchievements.title: Successful Liquefaction of Gases
keyAchievements.description: Successfully liquefied many substances such as chlorine, ammonia, and carbon dioxide, proving the existence of critical points and the interchangeability of states of matter.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Manufacturing Optical Glass
keyAchievements.description: Researched the optical properties of glass used in telescopes and developed a method to produce uniform glass without bubbles or striae.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Faraday Effect
keyAchievements.description: Established the fundamental relationship between a magnetic field and light rays, showing that a magnetic field can affect light, and discovered the phenomenon of the plane of polarization rotating in a magnetic field.

keyAchievements.title: Classification of Magnetic Materials
keyAchievements.description: Named diamagnetic materials that stop perpendicularly to a magnetic field and paramagnetic materials that stop parallel to it, discovering that most gases are diamagnetic while oxygen has paramagnetism.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Magnetic Field Concept
keyAchievements.description: Believed that lines of magnetic force had physical meaning and first mentioned the concept of a magnetic field, where the influence from two magnetic poles varies depending on the position.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Faraday Cage Principle
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that the electrical charge on a charged conductor resides only on its exterior and exerts no influence on anything enclosed within it.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Christmas Lectures
keyAchievements.description: Created the 'Christmas Lectures for Children' in 1826 to popularize science, which has been passed down as a tradition of the Royal Institution in London.

faq.question: What was Michael Faraday's educational background?
faq.answer: Faraday received little formal or higher education during his childhood, learning only the basics of reading, writing, and arithmetic. He acquired his scientific knowledge primarily through self-study.

faq.question: Was Faraday proficient in mathematics?
faq.answer: Faraday lacked mathematical skills to the point that he could only do simple algebra and could not handle trigonometry, but he excelled in visual thinking and imagination. His theories were later mathematically reformulated by James Clerk Maxwell.

faq.question: Did Faraday pursue fame or wealth?
faq.answer: No. He was not interested in worldly wealth, refused to publish his lectures, and declined the presidency of the Royal Society as well as a knighthood. His humility and frugality stemmed from his religious beliefs.

faq.question: Did Faraday have children?
faq.answer: No. Faraday married Sarah Barnard, but they had no children throughout their lives. However, he created and participated in science lectures for children, contributing to the popularization of science.

faq.question: How did Faraday's religion influence his scientific research?
faq.answer: Faraday was a devout follower of Christian fundamentalism and a member of the Sandemanian congregation. His humility, frugality, and pure pursuit of academics were closely related to this religious background.

timeline.event: Born as the only son of Cambyses I, King of Anshan of Persian descent, and Mandane, daughter of King Astyages of Media
Rebelled against his maternal grandfather Astyages, starting a war with Media
Conquered the Median Empire by capturing Ecbatana
Marched to Sardis, the capital of Lydia
Defeated King Croesus of Lydia and captured Sardis
The Greek Ionian cities on the Aegean coast submitted to Persia
Invaded the Neo-Babylonian Empire and captured Babylon
Killed in action during a battle with Central Asian nomads east of the Caspian Sea

keyAchievements.title: Construction of the great Persian Empire
keyAchievements.description: Grew Persia into a vast empire encompassing most of Southwest and Central Asia and reaching India.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of the Median Empire
keyAchievements.description: Conquered the Median Empire by capturing Ecbatana in 549 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Lydia
keyAchievements.description: Conquered Lydia by capturing its capital Sardis in 546 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Neo-Babylonia
keyAchievements.description: Invaded Neo-Babylonia and captured Babylon in 539 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Liberation of Jews and permission to rebuild the Temple
keyAchievements.description: Liberated the Jews held captive in Babylon, allowing them to return to Jerusalem and rebuild their sacred Temple in Jerusalem.

keyAchievements.title: Policy of religious tolerance
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a policy of religious tolerance and inclusion to harmonize the multi-ethnic Persian Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Cyrus Cylinder
keyAchievements.description: Left behind the Cyrus Cylinder, known as the first declaration of human rights that banned slavery.

faq.question: Where does the name Cyrus originate?
faq.answer: Ancient Greek historians recorded that the name Cyrus originated from 'Kuros' meaning sun, and it is related to the meaning 'like the sun' in Old Persian.

faq.question: How is Cyrus mentioned in the Bible?
faq.answer: He is recorded as 'Koresh' in the Old Testament, and appears as the king who liberated the Jews from the Babylonian captivity and allowed them to rebuild the Temple in Jerusalem.

faq.question: How did Cyrus die?
faq.answer: According to ancient historians, he was killed in a battle with Central Asian nomads east of the Caspian Sea. Herodotus recorded that he died in a battle with the queen of the Massagetae.

faq.question: What is the Cyrus Cylinder?
faq.answer: It is known as the first declaration of human rights that banned slavery, and is currently housed in Room 52 of the British Museum.

faq.question: Where is the tomb of Cyrus?
faq.answer: It is located in Pasargadae and is registered as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

timeline.event: Born in Vienna, Austria.
Obtained a Ph.D. from the University of Vienna.
Married Annemarie (Anny) Bertel.
Moved to the University of Zurich.
Published the paper 'Quantization as an Eigenvalue Problem' containing the Schrödinger equation.
Succeeded Max Planck at Friedrich Wilhelm University in Berlin.
Left Germany in opposition to Nazism.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics along with Paul Dirac.
Proposed the Schrödinger's cat thought experiment.
Fired in Graz after the Austrian annexation, fled to Italy, and later relocated to Dublin.
Became the Director of the School for Theoretical Physics at the Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies.
Authored 'What Is Life?'.
Became a naturalized Irish citizen.
Retired in Dublin.
Returned to Vienna.
Died of tuberculosis in Vienna at the age of 73.
The Department of Physics at Trinity College Dublin recommended removing his name from a lecture theatre considering his history of sexual abuse.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Schrödinger equation
keyAchievements.description: Developed an equation providing a way to calculate a system's wave function and how it changes dynamically over time, thereby developing the fundamental results of quantum mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Physics
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 along with Paul Dirac for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'What Is Life?'
keyAchievements.description: Viewed life phenomena from the perspective of physics and addressed the problem of genetics, inspiring the discovery of the DNA double helix structure.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed Schrödinger's cat thought experiment
keyAchievements.description: A famous thought experiment designed to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics, and one of his most enduring legacies.

keyAchievements.title: Research on unified field theory
keyAchievements.description: Made several attempts to construct a unified field theory integrating gravity, electromagnetism, and nuclear forces.

keyAchievements.title: Research on color theory
keyAchievements.description: Showed a strong interest in psychology, particularly color perception and colorimetry, and published a series of papers in this field.

faq.question: For what achievement did Erwin Schrödinger win the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 along with Paul Dirac for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.

faq.question: What is Schrödinger's cat?
faq.answer: It is a famous thought experiment designed to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics.

faq.question: What books did Schrödinger author?
faq.answer: He authored 'What Is Life?', which includes discussions on the genetic code of living organisms and negentropy.

faq.question: When and where was Schrödinger born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born in Vienna, Austria on August 12, 1887, and died of tuberculosis in Vienna on January 4, 1961, at the age of 73.

faq.question: Why did Schrödinger leave Germany?
faq.answer: He left Germany in 1933 because he despised the anti-Semitism of the Nazis.

timeline.event: Giuseppe Maria Garibaldi, an Italian general and patriot, is born.
Certified as a merchant navy captain.
Met Giovanni Battista Cuneo, an Italian political immigrant, in Taganrog, Russia, and joined the Young Italy movement.
Sentenced to death after participating in the Piedmont rebellion and fled to Marseille.
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil and aided the independence of the Riograndense Republic.
Anita Ribeiro joined Garibaldi's ship 'Rio Pardo'.
First son Menotti is born.
Moved to Montevideo and worked as a trader and teacher.
Married Anita in Montevideo; commanded the Uruguayan fleet and raised the 'Italian Legion'.
Second daughter Rosita is born; formed the Italian Legion in Montevideo.
Third daughter Teresita is born.
Achieved victory at the Battles of Cerro and San Antonio.
Returned to his homeland Italy upon hearing news of the revolution breaking out in Palermo.
Promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army; fled to northern Italy after the fall of the Roman Republic; his wife Anita died during the retreat.
Became a resident of New York.
Sailed to the mouth of the River Tyne in North East England as the master of the sailing ship Commonwealth.
Returned to Italy.
Outbreak of the Second Italian War of Independence; led the 'Hunters of the Alps' and captured Varese and Como.
Landed in Marsala, Sicily leading a thousand volunteers.
Defeated the Bourbon French garrison at Calatafimi.
Launched a siege on Palermo.
Entered the capital of Naples.
The volunteers achieved success at the Battle of the Volturno.
Met and shook hands with Victor Emmanuel II in Teano.
The unified Kingdom of Italy was established.
Left Genoa, landed in Palermo, and gathered volunteers under the slogan 'Rome or Death'.
Landed at Melito and marched into the Calabrian mountains.
Met regular forces at the Aspromonte mountains and was taken prisoner after a battle.
With the backing of the Italian government, recruited the 'Hunters of the Alps', led them into Trentino, and defeated the Austrian army at Bezzecca.
Marched on Rome again but was stopped by the Papal army supported by French reinforcements.
The Franco-Prussian War broke out.
Died on the island of Caprera.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Italian unification
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

keyAchievements.title: Hero of Two Worlds
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'Hero of Two Worlds' for his military achievements across South America and Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Support for South American independence
keyAchievements.description: Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to help the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

keyAchievements.title: Italian military command
keyAchievements.description: Appointed general by the provisional government of Milan and promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Hunters of the Alps
keyAchievements.description: Led the 'Hunters of the Alps' in the Second Italian War of Independence, capturing Varese and Como.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Expedition of the Thousand
keyAchievements.description: Led an expedition of a thousand volunteers to successfully annex Sicily, Southern Italy, Marche, and Umbria to the Kingdom of Sardinia.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Franco-Prussian War
keyAchievements.description: Served as commander of the Army of the Vosges during the Franco-Prussian War.

keyAchievements.title: International symbol
keyAchievements.description: Became an international symbol for realizing the ideals of national independence and republicanism.

keyAchievements.title: Efforts to limit papal power
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to anti-clericalism among Latin liberals and did much to limit the temporal power of the Pope.

faq.question: Who was Giuseppe Garibaldi?
faq.answer: An Italian general and patriot who contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

faq.question: Why is Garibaldi called the 'Hero of Two Worlds'?
faq.answer: Because his military achievements span across South America and Europe.

faq.question: What were Garibaldi's political beliefs?
faq.answer: He was a republican who dedicated himself to unifying Italy under a democratic republican government.

faq.question: What is Garibaldi's iconic attire?
faq.answer: It is related to the red shirts worn by his volunteers instead of uniforms.

faq.question: What activities did Garibaldi undertake in South America?
faq.answer: He joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to aid the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

faq.question: Did Garibaldi have a chance to participate in the American Civil War?
faq.answer: He was offered a position as a major general in the Union Army by President Abraham Lincoln, but the offer fell through when he proposed the abolition of slavery and the position of Commander-in-Chief as conditions.

faq.question: Where is Garibaldi's hometown?
faq.answer: He was born in Nice (Nizza), currently in France, while it was under French rule in 1792.

faq.question: With whom did Garibaldi have a conflict after Italian unification?
faq.answer: He deeply disliked the Sardinian Prime Minister Camillo di Cavour, owing to personal resentment over Cavour's pragmatism and the selling of Nice to the French.

timeline.event: Born in Edinburgh, Scotland
Mother died (age 8)
Produced his first scientific work, a paper on 'Oval Curves', at age 14
Enrolled at the University of Edinburgh
Began studying at the University of Cambridge
Graduated from Cambridge as Second Wrangler in the Mathematical Tripos and first in the Smith's Prize exam
Elected as a Fellow of Trinity College to supervise experiments on electricity and magnetism and research color theory
Published a paper on the structure of Saturn's rings
Married Katherine Mary Dewar
Awarded the Adams Prize for his research on Saturn's rings
Appointed Professor of Natural Philosophy and Astronomy at King's College London, and received the Rumford Medal from the Royal Society of London for his research on color vision
Produced the first color photograph by applying the mixing of the three primary colors
Published 'A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field'
Resigned from his professorship at King's College and became a professor at Cambridge University
Statistically formulated the kinetic theory of gases
Researched at the Cavendish Laboratory
Published 'A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism'
Began editing Henry Cavendish's unpublished electrical experiment data
Died of stomach cancer in Cambridge at the age of 48 on November 5

keyAchievements.title: Electromagnetism
keyAchievements.description: Laid a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena, established electromagnetism, and proved that light is an electromagnetic wave.

keyAchievements.title: Maxwell's equations
keyAchievements.description: Mathematically organized existing electromagnetic theories and established Maxwell's equations, the differential equations that form the basis of electromagnetism.

keyAchievements.title: Kinetic theory of gases
keyAchievements.description: Formulated the velocity distribution law of gas molecules and introduced probabilistic concepts, laying the foundation for statistical mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Color theory and color photography
keyAchievements.description: Produced the first color photograph in 1861 by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Saturn's rings
keyAchievements.description: Showed that Saturn's rings must be composed of small particles to be stable, a prediction proven by the Voyager spacecraft in the 1980s.

faq.question: Who was James Clerk Maxwell?
faq.answer: A British theoretical physicist and mathematician born in Edinburgh, Scotland, recognized as the founder of electromagnetism for establishing a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena.

faq.question: What is Maxwell's most important achievement?
faq.answer: Laying a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena and establishing electromagnetism, as well as presenting 'Maxwell's equations' that mathematically structured this.

faq.question: What contribution did Maxwell make to color photography?
faq.answer: In 1861, he created the first color photograph by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

faq.question: When and where was Maxwell born, and when did he die?
faq.answer: Born on June 13, 1831, in Edinburgh, Scotland, and died of stomach cancer in Cambridge on November 5, 1879, at the age of 48.

timeline.event: Birth of Tran Hung Dao, an imperial prince and general of the Tran dynasty in Great Viet
When an envoy of the Mongol general Uriyangkhadai demanded safe passage through Vietnam, Tran Hung Dao was assigned the duty of border defense
When Kublai Khan's son Toghon invaded Vietnam with an army of 500,000, Nhan Tong appointed Tran Hung Dao as the Commander-in-Chief to lead the army
The army led by Tran Hung Dao fought against the Mongol forces, but the capital Thang Long was captured, and the retired emperor and Nhan Tong fled to Thanh Hoa
Tran Hung Dao compiled the 'Binh thu yeu luoc' (Summary of Military Tactics) and wrote the 'Hich tuong si' (Proclamation to the Officers) to boost the troops' morale
The army led by Tran Hung Dao recaptured Thang Long and forced Toghon's Mongol army to retreat
When the Yuan dynasty's army invaded again, Tran Hung Dao withdrew his troops from Thang Long and annihilated the Yuan fleet at the Bach Dang River (Battle of Bach Dang)
Tran Hung Dao died, leaving the last words: 'Unite the army like parents and children, treat the people generously, and use that strength to achieve great endeavors'

keyAchievements.title: Repelling Mongol Invasions
keyAchievements.description: Successfully repelled massive invasions by the Yuan dynasty twice, in 1285 and 1287, safeguarding the independence of Vietnam.

keyAchievements.title: Military Strategy and Morale Boosting
keyAchievements.description: Authored the 'Binh thu yeu luoc' and the 'Hich tuong si' to boost the morale of his soldiers and provide effective military strategies.

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Bach Dang
keyAchievements.description: Achieved a decisive victory in the Battle of Bach Dang in 1287 by using the strategy of planting wooden stakes in the riverbed to annihilate the Yuan fleet.

faq.question: What is Tran Hung Dao's real name?
faq.answer: Tran Hung Dao's real name is Tran Quoc Tuan, and Hung Dao is a title bestowed upon him later to praise his achievements.

faq.question: What books did Tran Hung Dao write?
faq.answer: His works include 'Binh thu yeu luoc' (Summary of Military Tactics) and 'Van Kiep tong bi truyen thu' (Esoteric Manual of Van Kiep).

faq.question: What is a famous statement made by Tran Hung Dao?
faq.answer: During the Mongol invasion in 1285, he showed his will to fight by saying, 'If Your Majesty wishes to surrender, please cut off my head first'.

timeline.event: Zenobia, a Palmyrene noblewoman, married King Odaenathus
Odaenathus was assassinated, and Zenobia became the regent of the Palmyrene Empire in place of her young son Vaballathus
Zenobia successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt
Declared Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and herself as Empress of Palmyra
Defeated by Roman Emperor Aurelian and taken to Rome as a captive, bringing the Palmyrene Empire to ruin
자브다스 장군이 이끄는 팔미라 제국 군대가 이집트와 시리아를 정복함.
팔미라 제국 군대가 이집트 총독을 살해함.
팔미라 제국이 아나톨리아와 팔레스타인을 정복하며 영토를 확장함.
로마 제국의 아우렐리아누스 황제가 팔미라 제국 정복을 결심하고 군대를 이끌고 침공함.
로마 제국이 팔미라 제국을 정복함.
로마에서 열린 아우렐리아누스 황제의 개선식에서 포로로 끌려옴.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Palmyrene Empire
keyAchievements.description: After her husband's death, she ruled the Palmyrene Empire as regent and expanded its territory by conquering Roman Egypt.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of an independent state
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to establish an independent state from the Roman Empire by capturing large areas of Roman territory, including the Levant and Asia Minor.

keyAchievements.title: Declaration of Imperial titles
keyAchievements.description: Declared her son Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and elevated herself to Empress of Palmyra.

faq.question: Who was Zenobia?
faq.answer: A noblewoman and ruler of the Palmyrene Empire, who acted as regent after her husband Odaenathus was assassinated, bringing the empire to its peak before clashing with the Roman Empire and eventually leading to its downfall.

faq.question: What territories did Zenobia conquer?
faq.answer: She successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt, the Levant, and Asia Minor.

faq.question: How did Zenobia's rule come to an end?
faq.answer: She was defeated by the newly ascended Roman Emperor Aurelian, taken to Rome as a captive, and the Palmyrene Empire fell.

faq.question: 제노비아는 어떻게 팔미라 제국의 통치자가 되었나요?
faq.answer: 267년 오데나투스 황제 암살 후 아들 바발라투스를 후계자로 임명하며 팔미라 제국의 공동 통치자가 되었습니다.

faq.question: 제노비아의 최후는 어떻게 되었나요?
faq.answer: 274년 로마에서 열린 아우렐리아누스 황제의 개선식에 포로로 끌려왔으나, 그 이후의 정확한 생사는 불명확합니다.

timeline.event: Born to a Protestant officer father in Montbeliard, Württemberg
Enrolled in the Caroline Academy (Karlsschule) in Stuttgart and spent 4 years

keyAchievements.title: Founded comparative anatomy
keyAchievements.description: Earned the title of the 'Father of Comparative Anatomy' for establishing the foundation of comparative anatomy by systematically comparing the anatomical structures of various animals.

keyAchievements.title: Founded vertebrate paleontology
keyAchievements.description: Established vertebrate paleontology by studying fossil bones and proposing the theory of extinction.

keyAchievements.title: Developed the principle of correlation of parts
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the 'principle of correlation of parts', stating that the shape and function of each organ in an organism are closely linked to the overall structure and lifestyle of the animal.

faq.question: What is Georges Cuvier's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: He is the French zoologist known for founding comparative anatomy and paleontology.

faq.question: What is catastrophism advocated by Cuvier?
faq.answer: It is the theory that sudden, widespread catastrophic events like massive floods occurred multiple times in Earth's past history, causing the extinction of species.

faq.question: What was Cuvier's view on evolution?
faq.answer: He opposed the theory of evolution. According to his principle of correlation of parts, an organism's structure is perfectly adapted to its lifestyle, so he believed that changes in some parts would break the overall harmony, making survival impossible.

faq.question: How did Cuvier prove that extinction occurred?
faq.answer: He proved that extinction was a real phenomenon by demonstrating that fossil elephant bones, like mammoths and mastodons, belonged to different species than living elephants.

faq.question: 조르주 퀴비에는 파리 박물관에서 어떻게 교수가 되었나요?
faq.answer: 그는 생틸르의 소개로 파리 박물관의 교수가 되었습니다.

timeline.event: Born on the island of Samos
Traveled to various countries including Egypt, Phoenicia, and Chaldea after the age of 18 (unconfirmed)
Moved to Croton in southern Italy and founded a religious organization and philosophical school
With the rise of democratic politics in Croton, the Pythagorean order was violently suppressed

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Pythagorean Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved the geometric theorem that the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides in a right-angled triangle.

keyAchievements.title: Belief that all things are numbers
keyAchievements.description: Considered numbers to be the fundamental principle of the universe and emphasized their mathematical relationships and ratios.

keyAchievements.title: Mathematical theory of music
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that harmonious musical scales form simple numerical ratios and applied this to the movement of the universe.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Pythagorean School
keyAchievements.description: Founded an exclusive religious and philosophical community centered around asceticism and mathematical exploration in Croton, southern Italy.

keyAchievements.title: Development of mathematical deductive methods
keyAchievements.description: Elevated the practicality of mathematics to the realm of logical deduction and proof.

faq.question: Who was Pythagoras?
faq.answer: An ancient Greek philosopher and mathematician from Ionia, the founder of the Pythagorean school, widely known for discovering the Pythagorean theorem.

faq.question: Did Pythagoras actually discover the Pythagorean theorem?
faq.answer: Although the theorem bears his name, it is unclear whether he himself proved it, and the content of the theorem was already known to the Babylonians.

faq.question: What were the main teachings of the Pythagorean school?
faq.answer: They believed in the transmigration of souls (metempsychosis), practiced asceticism such as vegetarianism, and sought the fundamental harmony of the universe in numbers and their relationships.

faq.question: Why is it difficult to distinguish between the achievements of Pythagoras and those of his followers?
faq.answer: Because the followers of the Pythagorean school attributed all discoveries to the founder Pythagoras, and kept their knowledge strictly secret.

faq.question: 피타고라스는 어디에서 학파를 설립했나요?
faq.answer: 피타고라스는 남부 이탈리아의 크로토네로 이동하여 종교적인 학파를 세웠습니다.

timeline.event: Estimated birth in Sinope, an Ionian colony on the Black Sea.
Arrested with his banker father for debasing the currency and exiled from Sinope.
Moved to Athens and lived there.
Captured by pirates, brought to Corinth as a slave, but freed.
Met with Alexander the Great.

faq.question: Why is Diogenes called 'the Dog'?
faq.answer: He was called so because he practiced asceticism, disregarded conventions by satisfying his physical needs in public, and barked at and bit hypocrites like a dog.

faq.question: What is the anecdote between Alexander the Great and Diogenes?
faq.answer: When Alexander visited him and asked what he could do for him, Diogenes famously replied, 'Stand out of my sunlight.'

timeline.event: Born to King Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn at Greenwich Palace
Mother Anne Boleyn is executed, and she loses her title as princess
Henry VIII dies, and her half-brother Edward VI ascends the throne
Implicated in Thomas Wyatt's rebellion and imprisoned in the Tower of London during the reign of Mary I
Mary I dies, and Elizabeth ascends the throne
Enacts the Act of Supremacy, declaring herself the Supreme Governor of the Church of England
Signs the Treaty of Edinburgh, driving French forces out of Scotland
The Pope excommunicates Elizabeth
Francis Drake completes his circumnavigation of the globe
Executes Mary, Queen of Scots
The English Navy defeats the Spanish Armada
Gives the 'Golden Speech' to Parliament
Passes away at Richmond Palace
메리 1세 사망 후 잉글랜드 및 아일랜드 왕국의 군주로 즉위함
웨스트민스터 사원에서 대관식을 가짐
새로운 수장령이 공포됨
로버트 더들리의 아내 에이미가 사망함
프랑스의 르아브르를 침공함
로버트 더들리가 레스터 백작으로 책봉됨
스코틀랜드의 메리 1세가 단리 경 헨리 스튜어트와 재혼함
스코틀랜드의 메리 1세가 아들 제임스를 낳음
단리 경이 살해됨
스코틀랜드의 메리 1세가 잉글랜드로 망명함
잉글랜드 북부에서 대규모 가톨릭 반란이 일어남
교황 비오 5세가 엘리자베스 1세를 사생아로 선언함
로버트 더들리가 레티스 놀리스와 재혼함
반가톨릭 형벌법이 입법됨
먼스터에서 제럴드 피츠제럴드의 반란이 일어남
험프리 길버트가 북아메리카에 식민지를 세움
잉글랜드-스페인 전쟁이 발발함
프랜시스 드레이크가 카디스를 습격하여 스페인 함대를 대파함
스코틀랜드의 메리 1세가 참수됨
스페인이 무적함대를 결성함
잉글랜드가 스페인에 대규모 원정 함대(드레이크-노리스 원정)를 보냄
프랑스의 앙리 4세 왕위 계승에 원군을 파견함
프랑스에 파견된 잉글랜드 원정군이 궤멸함
휴 오닐이 이끄는 아일랜드 반군과의 전쟁이 시작됨
로버트 데버루가 지휘하는 잉글랜드 군이 아일랜드에서 패배함
잉글랜드 동인도 회사를 인가함
잉글랜드 하원이 가격 담합 폐단을 없애라고 청원함
에식스 백작 로버트 데버루가 처형됨
지인의 죽음으로 건강이 급격히 악화됨
리치몬드궁에서 사망함

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Church of England
keyAchievements.description: Through the Elizabethan Religious Settlement, she firmly established the Church of England as the state religion and sought stability amidst religious conflict.

keyAchievements.title: Defeat of the Spanish Armada
keyAchievements.description: Defeated the Invincible Armada of Spain in 1588, achieving maritime supremacy.

keyAchievements.title: Flourishing of culture and arts
keyAchievements.description: Great playwrights like William Shakespeare and Christopher Marlowe were active during her reign, marking a golden age of English literature.

keyAchievements.title: Support for overseas expansion
keyAchievements.description: Supported overseas exploration, such as Francis Drake's circumnavigation, and laid the foundation for the colonial empire, including establishing the East India Company.

keyAchievements.title: National integration and stability
keyAchievements.description: Maintained domestic political stability and national integration over a 44-year reign, ushering in the 'Elizabethan era'.

keyAchievements.title: 스페인 무적함대 격파
keyAchievements.description: 스페인과의 잉글랜드-스페인 전쟁에서 무적함대가 잉글랜드 해군의 공격에 대패하는 데 기여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 잉글랜드 동인도 회사 인가
keyAchievements.description: 1600년 12월 31일 잉글랜드 동인도 회사를 인가하여 해외 식민지 개척의 기반을 마련했습니다.

faq.question: Did Elizabeth I ever marry?
faq.answer: She never married, earning the nickname 'The Virgin Queen'. She utilized her marriage prospects as a diplomatic tool but ultimately famously stated she was married to the nation.

faq.question: Why did she execute Mary, Queen of Scots?
faq.answer: Mary, Queen of Scots, was a Catholic and the next in line to the English throne, becoming a focal point for rebellions and assassination plots against Elizabeth, which led to her eventual execution.

faq.question: What is the relationship between Elizabeth I and the English Renaissance?
faq.answer: Her reign is regarded as the golden age of the English Renaissance, flourishing with culture, arts, and overseas exploration, often called the 'Elizabethan era'.

faq.question: Who succeeded Elizabeth I?
faq.answer: Since she left no heirs, James VI of Scotland (son of Mary, Queen of Scots) succeeded her as James I of England, bringing the Tudor dynasty to an end and starting the Stuart dynasty.

faq.question: 엘리자베스 1세 재위 중 가장 중요한 대외 사건은 무엇인가요?
faq.answer: 스페인과의 잉글랜드-스페인 전쟁에서 스페인의 무적함대를 격파한 사건이 가장 중요하며, 해외 식민지 개척과 잉글랜드 동인도 회사 인가도 이루어졌습니다.

timeline.event: Born as the twelfth child of Franz Theodor Schubert and Elisabeth in Himmelpfortgrund, Vienna.
Father bought a school in Lichtental and moved.
Received piano instruction from his older brother Ignaz and violin instruction from his father.
Michael Holzer, the organist and choir director of the parish church in Lichtental, taught Schubert singing, organ, and basso continuo.
Studied composition with Antonio Salieri.
Auditioned successfully to become a choirboy at the Imperial Court Chapel and entered the Stadtkonvikt.
Voice changed and left the Imperial Court Chapel choir.
Returned to his father's school.
Began teacher training at a normal school in Vienna.
Started working as an assistant teacher at his father's school in Lichtental.
Composed his first masterpiece, 'Gretchen am Spinnrade' (Gretchen at the Spinning Wheel).
Mother passed away.
Composed many Lieder, including 'Erlkönig' (Erlking) and 'Heidenröslein' (Heath Rose).
Left teaching and became an independent composer.
Worked as a music tutor for the daughters of Count Johann Karl Esterhazy in Zseliz, Hungary.
Signs of a serious illness (presumed to be syphilis) began to appear.
The first 'Schubertiade', a gathering to appreciate Schubert's music, was held at the home of Ignaz von Sonnleithner.
Deteriorating health.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of German Lieder
keyAchievements.description: Composed over 600 art songs (Lieder), elevating the Lied, previously considered a minor genre, to an important art form where music and poetry are equal.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of Harmony and Melody
keyAchievements.description: Exhibited outstanding melodic genius and used rich harmonies with bold modulations, pointing the way toward the Romantic era.

keyAchievements.title: Symphonies
keyAchievements.description: The Symphony No. 8 'Unfinished' and Symphony No. 9 'The Great' are highly acclaimed for maximizing the possibilities of early Romantic symphonies.

keyAchievements.title: Piano and Chamber Music
keyAchievements.description: Left behind masterpieces that combine lyricism and dramatic tension, such as the late piano sonatas, 'Wanderer Fantasy', 'Trout Quintet', and String Quartet 'Death and the Maiden'.

keyAchievements.title: 피아노 5중주곡 《송어》
keyAchievements.description: 현악 4중주에 제2바이올린 대신 콘트라베이스를 사용한 독특한 구성의 피아노 5중주곡 《송어》를 작곡했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 가곡 《마왕》
keyAchievements.description: 17세에 괴테의 시를 바탕으로 한 가곡 《마왕》을 작곡했습니다.

faq.question: What is Schubert's nickname?
faq.answer: He is called the 'King of Lieder'.

faq.question: Did Schubert get married?
faq.answer: He never married and left no children.

faq.question: Did Schubert receive regular musical education?
faq.answer: He learned singing, piano, and violin from an early age and studied composition under Antonio Salieri, receiving education from the Imperial Court Chapel.

faq.question: Was Schubert rich?
faq.answer: Since he did not have a regular job or aristocratic patronage, he suffered from financial difficulties throughout his life and received much help from his friends.

faq.question: How did Schubert die?
faq.answer: The exact cause of death is unknown, but it is generally thought that his health deteriorated due to long-standing syphilis, and he ultimately died of typhoid fever.

timeline.event: Birth of Frederick
Attempted to escape to England with a friend but failed and was imprisoned; the friend was executed
Began independent activities as Crown Prince in Rheinsberg
Began correspondence with Voltaire
Ascended to the throne as Frederick II
First Silesian War broke out
Treaty of Breslau signed, securing Silesia
Second Silesian War broke out
Treaty of Dresden signed, defending Silesia
Seven Years' War broke out
Pushed to a critical situation at the Battle of Kunersdorf
Treaty of Hubertusburg signed, permanently securing Silesia
Participated in the First Partition of Poland, acquiring Royal Prussia
Organized the League of Princes (Fürstenbund) against Austria
Death of Frederick the Great
러시아 옐리자베타 여제 급사, 표트르 3세 즉위로 프로이센과 러시아 간 강화 성립
평화조약 체결로 프로이센의 슐레지엔 영유권 확정
제1차 폴란드 분할에 참가하여 서프로이센 차지
바이에른 왕위계승전쟁에서 오스트리아와 교전
포츠담의 상수시 궁전에서 사망
유길준이 '보로사국후례두익대왕7년전사'를 통해 한국에 소개
독일 통일 후 상수시 궁전 마당에 유언에 따라 개들과 함께 묻힘

keyAchievements.title: Securing Silesia
keyAchievements.description: Acquired the rich industrial region of Silesia from Austria through the War of the Austrian Succession and the Seven Years' War.

keyAchievements.title: Elevation of Prussia's status
keyAchievements.description: Through military expansion and successful wars, transformed Prussia into a major European power.

keyAchievements.title: Domestic reforms
keyAchievements.description: Under the slogan of 'the first servant of the state,' he promoted the abolition of torture, guaranteed religious tolerance, and improved the judicial system.

keyAchievements.title: Acquisition of Polish territory
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the First Partition of Poland, acquiring West Prussia, thereby physically connecting Brandenburg and East Prussia.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of culture and arts
keyAchievements.description: Built the Sanssouci Palace, interacted with Enlightenment thinkers like Voltaire, and demonstrated outstanding talent in music and literature.

keyAchievements.title: 베를린 아카데미 부흥
keyAchievements.description: 학문을 멸시했던 선왕과 달리 베를린 아카데미를 부흥시켜 과거에 추방되었던 학자들을 다시 불러들였습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 서프로이센 확보
keyAchievements.description: 1772년 제1차 폴란드 분할에 참가하여 당시 폴란드령이었던 서프로이센을 차지해 영토를 확장했습니다.

faq.question: What is Frederick the Great's famous saying?
faq.answer: He said, 'The ruler is the first servant of the state' (Ich bin der erste Diener meines Staates), embodying the ideology of an enlightened despot.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Frederick the Great and his father, Frederick William I?
faq.answer: The relationship was very bad. His father emphasized militarism and strict discipline, while Frederick enjoyed French culture, philosophy, and music, leading to severe conflict.

faq.question: Did Frederick the Great actually compose music?
faq.answer: Yes. He was a talented flutist and composed many pieces, including 121 flute sonatas and 4 symphonies.

faq.question: How did Frederick the Great evaluate the German language?
faq.answer: He preferred French, considering German an unrefined language, and wrote most of his works and letters in French.

faq.question: How was the succession handled since Frederick the Great had no children?
faq.answer: Because he had a loveless marriage with his queen, Elisabeth Christine, and left no children, his nephew succeeded him as Frederick William II.

timeline.event: Born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi
Emancipation Proclamation issued, freeing her family
Parents and younger brother die of yellow fever; Ida takes responsibility for her siblings
Forcibly removed from a first-class train car; files a lawsuit against the railroad company
Wins the lawsuit at the local court
The Tennessee Supreme Court overturns the ruling in favor of the railroad company
A friend's lynching incident occurs; she begins a full-scale anti-lynching campaign in 'The Free Speech and Headlight'
Her newspaper office is destroyed by a white mob; she relocates to Chicago
Publishes the anti-lynching pamphlet 'Southern Horrors: Lynch Law in All Its Phases'
Publishes another anti-lynching pamphlet 'The Red Record'
Marries Ferdinand L. Barnett
Participates in the founding of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP)

keyAchievements.title: Anti-lynching Journalism
keyAchievements.description: Through investigative journalism, she systematically investigated and exposed the reality that the lynching of Black people in the South was often used to eliminate Black people who achieved economic success, rather than to punish criminals.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Anti-lynching Pamphlets
keyAchievements.description: Published pamphlets containing the realities of lynching, such as 'Southern Horrors' (1892) and 'The Red Record' (1895), drawing national and international attention to the issue.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Civil Rights Organizations
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the founding of the National Association of Colored Women (NACW) in 1896 and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) in 1909.

faq.question: What kind of environment was Ida B. Wells born into?
faq.answer: She was born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi in 1862, but was freed the following year by the Emancipation Proclamation.

faq.question: What motivated Ida B. Wells to start her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: In 1892, a close friend and two other Black men were lynched by a white mob out of economic jealousy over their successful grocery store. This incident prompted her to begin a full-scale anti-lynching campaign.

faq.question: What challenges did Ida B. Wells face because of her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: Her newspaper office in Memphis was destroyed by a white mob, and she received numerous death threats, eventually forcing her to leave the South and relocate to Chicago.

faq.question: What is the Alpha Suffrage Club?
faq.answer: It is the first Black women's suffrage organization in Illinois, founded by Ida B. Wells in Chicago in 1913.

timeline.event: Born in Leiden, Netherlands.
Apprenticed for three years under painter Jacob van Swanenburgh.
Apprenticed for six months under painter Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam.
Opened his own studio in Leiden.
Began accepting students.
Moved to Amsterdam, began working as a professional portrait painter, and married Saskia van Uylenburgh.
Purchased an upscale house in Sint Antoniesbreestraat, Amsterdam.
His wife Saskia passed away shortly after the birth of their son Titus.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Chiaroscuro
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered the technique of chiaroscuro, maximizing the dramatic effect by dramatically contrasting light and dark.

keyAchievements.title: Innovations in Portraiture
keyAchievements.description: Moved away from conventional formal portraits, emphasizing the inner psychology and emotional states of the subjects through his portraits.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Self-Portraits
keyAchievements.description: Left behind numerous self-portraits over his lifetime, documenting the process of his self-reflection and changes in his life.

keyAchievements.title: Excellence in Printmaking
keyAchievements.description: Left a significant mark on the history of printmaking with his outstanding skill in etching, elevating printmaking to an independent art form.

keyAchievements.title: Dutch Golden Age Art
keyAchievements.description: Led the Dutch Golden Age art of the 17th century, creating a wide variety of works encompassing religious themes, history paintings, and landscapes.

faq.question: What is Rembrandt's most famous painting?
faq.answer: His most famous painting is 'The Night Watch' (De Nachtwacht), a group portrait completed in 1642 depicting a militia company moving into action.

faq.question: Why did Rembrandt paint so many self-portraits?
faq.answer: He painted self-portraits as a means to explore his inner self, document his aging process, practice facial expressions and lighting, and sometimes to showcase his skills.

faq.question: What technique is Rembrandt best known for?
faq.answer: He is famous for chiaroscuro, a technique that highlights the subject and creates dramatic tension by strongly contrasting light and shadow.

faq.question: Did Rembrandt enjoy wealth during his lifetime?
faq.answer: He achieved great success and wealth in his early years, but later in life, he suffered from financial difficulties due to lavish spending and changes in artistic trends, eventually declaring bankruptcy.

timeline.event: Born in Portsmouth, England
Father John Dickens is imprisoned for debt; Charles begins working at a blacking factory
Starts career as a journalist
Publishes 'Sketches by Boz'
Gains explosive popularity with the serialization of 'The Pickwick Papers'; marries Catherine Hogarth
Publishes 'Oliver Twist'
First visit to the United States
Publishes 'A Christmas Carol'
Publishes 'David Copperfield'
Purchases Gad's Hill Place, a house he admired since childhood
Separates from his wife Catherine
Publishes 'A Tale of Two Cities'
Publishes 'Great Expectations'
Involved in the Staplehurst rail crash
Second visit to the United States and conducts reading tours
Dies of a stroke; buried in Poet's Corner at Westminster Abbey
《데이비드 코퍼필드》를 출판했다.
《A Child's History of England》를 출판했다.
《황폐한 집》을 출판했다.
《어려운 시절》을 출판했다.
《작은 도릿》을 출판했다.
《두 도시 이야기》를 출판했다.
《위대한 유산》을 출판했다.
《Our Mutual Friend》를 출판했다.
두 번째 미국 방문이 이루어졌다.
《에드윈 드루드의 비밀》을 출판했다.
58세의 나이로 개즈 힐에서 숨을 거두었다.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of memorable characters
keyAchievements.description: Created some of the world's most famous fictional characters, including Ebenezer Scrooge, Oliver Twist, and David Copperfield.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for social reform
keyAchievements.description: Strongly criticized the social contradictions, poverty, and child labor issues brought about by the Industrial Revolution through his novels, contributing to social reform.

keyAchievements.title: Popularization of the serialization format
keyAchievements.description: Greatly contributed to the popularization of the novel serialization format by publishing novels in weekly or monthly installments, receiving tremendous response from readers.

keyAchievements.title: Modernization of Christmas
keyAchievements.description: Often referred to as 'The Man Who Invented Christmas,' his work 'A Christmas Carol' played a significant role in establishing the modern culture and concept of Christmas.

faq.question: What is Charles Dickens's most representative work?
faq.answer: His representative works include 'Oliver Twist', 'A Christmas Carol', 'David Copperfield', 'A Tale of Two Cities', and 'Great Expectations'.

faq.question: What was Dickens's childhood like?
faq.answer: His father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a blacking factory at the age of 12. This harsh experience deeply influenced his later works.

faq.question: Why did Dickens's novels usually come out in serial form?
faq.answer: He published them in weekly or monthly magazines to make his works accessible to the public at an affordable price, and this serialization method became very popular during the Victorian era.

faq.question: Did Dickens's works actually influence society?
faq.answer: Yes, his vivid descriptions of the plight of the poor and the poor conditions of child labor raised public awareness and contributed to real social reforms.

faq.question: How did Dickens die?
faq.answer: He died of a stroke at his home, Gad's Hill Place, on June 9, 1870, at the age of 58.

timeline.event: Born in Leipzig
Obtained a bachelor's degree in philosophy from the University of Leipzig
Obtained a master's degree in philosophy
The University of Leipzig refused his doctoral application in law due to his young age
Obtained a doctorate in law and was granted the right to practice from the University of Altdorf
Served the Elector of Mainz
Stayed in Paris, expanding his mathematical and scientific knowledge
Devised the binary number system
Entered the service of the House of Hanover as librarian and political advisor
Developed infinitesimal calculus
Discovered the concept of kinetic energy
Traveled through Germany, Austria, and Italy to write the history of the House of Brunswick
Proposed the idea of establishing an academy to the Elector of Brandenburg, leading to the creation of the Prussian Academy of Sciences
The Royal Society recognized Newton's priority in inventing calculus, declaring Leibniz a plagiarist
Wrote 'Monadology' based on his philosophical thoughts
George Louis, Elector of Hanover, ascended the British throne as George I, but Leibniz was forbidden from going to London
Died in Hanover; his funeral was held quietly
유럽 연합이 나올 것이라는 예상을 번복함.
사망함.
《인간오성신론》이 출판됨.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Infinitesimal Calculus
keyAchievements.description: Developed infinitesimal calculus independently of Isaac Newton, and the mathematical notation he devised is the one mainly used today.

keyAchievements.title: Devised the Binary Number System
keyAchievements.description: Refined the binary number system, which forms the basis of modern computer architecture.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of a Mechanical Calculator
keyAchievements.description: Invented the stepped reckoner, the first mechanical calculator capable of performing all four arithmetic operations.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Kinetic Energy (Vis Viva)
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the concept of 'vis viva' (living force), anticipating the law of conservation of energy.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Monads
keyAchievements.description: In philosophy, he proposed the concept of 'monads', explaining the universe as being composed of simple, immaterial substances.

keyAchievements.title: Principle of Sufficient Reason
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the principle of sufficient reason, stating that 'nothing happens without a reason'.

keyAchievements.title: Law of Continuity
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the law of continuity, stating that 'nature makes no leaps'.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the 'Best of All Possible Worlds'
keyAchievements.description: Optimistically argued that our universe is the 'best of all possible worlds' created by God.

keyAchievements.title: 도서관학 발전
keyAchievements.description: 독일 하노버와 볼펜뷔텔의 도서관에서 사서로 일하며 도서관학의 창시자 중 한 명으로, 새로운 도서관 건물을 설계하고 독자적인 색인 체계를 고안했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 베를린 학사원 설립
keyAchievements.description: 1700년에 베를린 학사원 설립을 직접적으로 추진했으며, 죽을 때까지 그 학회의 회장을 맡았습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 중국 철학 연구
keyAchievements.description: 『주역』의 괘가 2진법 숫자와 연관성이 있음을 주목하고, 유학적 개념을 유럽인들의 신앙에 수용시키려고 시도했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 《변신론》 저술
keyAchievements.description: 저서 《변신론》에서 세상에는 반드시 불완전함이 존재함을 '있을 수 있는 세계 중 최상의 세계'라는 용어를 통해 증명하려 했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 유럽 연맹 제안
keyAchievements.description: 1677년에 각 나라 대표가 모여 운영되는 유럽 연맹을 제안하여 유럽 연합의 출현을 예견했습니다.

faq.question: What was the calculus priority dispute?
faq.answer: A fierce dispute arose between Isaac Newton and Leibniz over who first invented calculus. Today, it is recognized that both discovered it independently.

faq.question: Why are Leibniz's mathematical notations important?
faq.answer: The notations used by Leibniz, such as the integral symbol (∫) and the derivative notation (d/dx), were intuitive and convenient, playing a significant role in the development of mathematics.

faq.question: What is Leibniz's Monadology?
faq.answer: It is a philosophy asserting that the universe is composed of simple, immaterial, soul-like entities called 'monads', which do not interact with each other but form a harmony pre-established by God.

faq.question: What role did Leibniz play in the history of computers?
faq.answer: He refined the binary number system, the basis of modern computers, and invented a mechanical calculator capable of performing all four arithmetic operations, establishing him as a pioneer of computer science.

faq.question: How did Leibniz spend his later years?
faq.answer: He suffered a great loss of reputation due to the calculus priority dispute with Newton. His employer, George I, moved to England, but Leibniz was left behind in Hanover, where he died lonely and sick with gout.

faq.question: 라이프니츠는 결혼했었나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠는 미혼으로 살았습니다.

faq.question: 라이프니츠의 종교는 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠의 종교는 개신교였으며, 철학적 유신론자였습니다.

faq.question: 라이프니츠는 어떤 학사원 설립에 기여했나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠는 1700년에 설립된 베를린 학사원의 설립을 직접적으로 추진했으며, 죽을 때까지 그 학회의 회장을 맡았습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Hochheim (or Tambach), Thuringia.
Traveled to Paris to study.
Appointed Prior in Erfurt and Vicar of Thuringia.
Obtained a master's degree (Magister) at the University of Paris.
Appointed Provincial of the newly established province of Saxony.
Appointed Vicar General of the province of Bohemia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of German Mysticism
keyAchievements.description: Known as the father of German mysticism, profoundly influencing later mystics such as Johannes Tauler and Heinrich Seuse.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the German Language
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in the development of the German language by delivering sermons and writing treatises not only in Latin but also in Middle High German.

keyAchievements.title: Theology of the Birth of God in the Soul
keyAchievements.description: Emphasized that the divine spark (Fünklein) exists in the deepest part of the human soul, and that humans can achieve union with God there.

faq.question: Who was Meister Eckhart?
faq.answer: Meister Eckhart was a German Catholic priest, theologian, philosopher, and mystic belonging to the Dominican Order.

faq.question: What does 'Meister' mean?
faq.answer: 'Meister' is a title referring to the academic degree of Master in Theology he obtained in Paris, indicating his status as an authoritative teacher.

faq.question: Why was Meister Eckhart accused of heresy?
faq.answer: Some of his expressions emphasizing the union of God and the human soul were deemed radical by the church authorities of the time, leading to suspicions of pantheism or self-deification.

timeline.event: Appointed to lead an expedition tasked by King John II of Portugal to find the southern tip of Africa
Sailed past the coast of Angola
Sailed past present-day Namibia
Caught in a massive storm off the coast of South Africa and drifted for 13 days
Landed at Mossel Bay after turning the Cape of Good Hope
Reached the Great Fish River (Rio do Infante) but was forced to turn back due to crew demands
Discovered the Cape of Good Hope on the return journey and named it the 'Cape of Storms' (Cabo das Tormentas)

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Cape of Good Hope
keyAchievements.description: Became the first European to discover the Cape of Good Hope at the southern tip of Africa in 1488.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered the sea route to India
keyAchievements.description: Proved that the Atlantic and Indian Oceans were connected, opening up the possibility of a direct sea route from Europe to Asia (India).

faq.question: What is Bartolomeu Dias's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: He was the first European to sail to the southern tip of Africa and discover the Cape of Good Hope.

faq.question: Why is the Cape of Good Hope called the Cape of Good Hope?
faq.answer: Dias originally named it the 'Cape of Storms' (Cabo das Tormentas), but King John II of Portugal renamed it the 'Cape of Good Hope' (Cabo da Boa Esperança), signifying the hope of reaching India.

faq.question: Did Bartolomeu Dias reach India?
faq.answer: No. He proved the possibility of a route to India, but had to turn back due to a lack of supplies and his crew's demands. It was Vasco da Gama who actually reached India later.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a vicar in Berkeley, Gloucestershire.
Became an apprentice to surgeon Daniel Ludlow in Sodbury.
Began studying medicine under John Hunter at St George's Hospital, University of London.
Became a general practitioner in his hometown.
Developed an interest in smallpox.
Gathered his observations on smallpox.
Married Catharine Kingscote.
Received his MD from the University of St Andrews in Scotland.
John Hunter died, ending their friendship.
Successfully performed the first vaccination on a local boy.
Reported the results of 23 experiments to the Royal Society and published his paper 'An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae'.
Received a grant from the government (part of a total of £30,000).
The Royal Jennerian Society was founded to promote vaccination in London.
Received the remainder of the grant from the government.
Made his last visit to London.
Died of a stroke in his hometown.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Cowpox Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the cowpox vaccine, earning him the title 'Father of Immunology'.

keyAchievements.title: Coined the Term Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Coined the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from the Latin word 'vacca', meaning cow.

keyAchievements.title: Development and Distribution of Smallpox Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Successfully performed the first vaccination on a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine and contributing to its worldwide distribution.

faq.question: Who is Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was an English physician, the discoverer of the cowpox vaccine, and the father of immunology.

faq.question: When was Edward Jenner born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was born on May 17, 1749, and died on January 26, 1823.

faq.question: What led Edward Jenner to coin the term vaccine?
faq.answer: Jenner created the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from 'vacca', the Latin word for cow.

faq.question: What are the main achievements of Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner discovered the cowpox vaccine and successfully administered the first vaccination to a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine.

timeline.event: Born in Fuendetodos, Aragon, Spain.
Goya's family bought a house in Zaragoza and moved there a few years later.
Submitted an application to the Royal Academy of Fine Arts but was rejected.
Was deeply moved by the works of Velázquez and Rembrandt in Madrid.
Submitted an application to the Royal Academy of Fine Arts but was rejected again.
Traveled to Italy and won second prize at a painting exhibition in Parma; returned to Zaragoza later that year to become a painter for the Basilica of Our Lady of the Pillar.
Married Josefa, Bayeu's sister.
Painted the portrait of the Count of Floridablanca, a close associate of Charles III.
Began painting portraits of Charles III.
Charles III died.
Charles IV ascended to the throne, and Goya became a court painter.
Contracted cholera and lost his hearing due to a high fever.
Painted a series of fantastical works.
Painted the frescoes of the Royal Chapel of St. Anthony of La Florida in Madrid.
Published the print series 'Los Caprichos' and became the First Court Painter.
Painted 'Charles IV of Spain and His Family' and 'The Nude Maja'.
Painted 'The Clothed Maja'.
The Peninsular War broke out between France and Spain, and Goya left documentary paintings of it.
Created the print series 'The Disasters of War'.
His wife Josefa died.
The Spanish Inquisition confiscated the Maja series, deeming them obscene.
The French army retreated, and Ferdinand VII returned to the throne.
Moved to Bordeaux, France.
Returned to Spain but went back to Bordeaux due to deteriorating health.
Died at the age of 82.

keyAchievements.title: Status as a Romantic Painter and Printmaker
keyAchievements.description: Spain's representative Romantic painter and printmaker, known as the representative of 18th-century Spanish painting and a genius artist who foreshadowed Impressionism.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a Court Painter
keyAchievements.description: Became a court painter in 1789 and the First Court Painter in 1799, painting portraits of the king, queen, and many royals and nobles.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of the Print Series 'Los Caprichos'
keyAchievements.description: Published 'Los Caprichos' in 1799, created using aquatint, to expose the reality and superstitions of the corrupt Catholic Church.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of War Documentary Paintings and Prints
keyAchievements.description: Left documentary paintings of the Peninsular War, 'The Second of May 1808' and 'The Third of May 1808', and created the print series 'The Disasters of War' depicting the horrors of war.

keyAchievements.title: 'Maja' Series
keyAchievements.description: Painted 'The Nude Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja', with 'The Nude Maja' being considered the 'first life-size female nude in Western art'.

keyAchievements.title: Black Paintings Series
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, at the 'Quinta del Sordo' (House of the Deaf Man), he created the 'Black Paintings' series depicting madness hidden behind reason, famously including 'Saturn Devouring His Son'.

faq.question: How is Francisco Goya evaluated as a painter?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as Spain's leading Romantic painter and printmaker, the representative of 18th-century Spanish painting, and a genius who heralded the beginning of Impressionism.

faq.question: When did Goya lose his hearing?
faq.answer: He lost his hearing in 1792 due to a high fever after contracting cholera.

faq.question: What are Goya's major print series?
faq.answer: His major print series include 'Los Caprichos', published in 1799, and 'The Disasters of War', created between 1810 and 1820.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Goya's 'Maja' series?
faq.answer: 'The Nude Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja' portray the same woman in the identical pose, and are evaluated as 'the first life-size female nudes in Western art'.

faq.question: What was the trend of Goya's works in his later years?
faq.answer: After losing his hearing in 1792, his paintings became darker; following the Peninsular War, he painted works with heavy themes and dark tones; and in his later years, he left the 'Black Paintings' series at the 'House of the Deaf Man'.

timeline.event: The approximate period when historians believe Homer lived and composed his epic poems, though dates range widely from the 12th to the 8th century BCE.

timeline.year: Late 8th Century BC or Early 7th Century BC
timeline.event: The period when the 'Iliad' and the 'Odyssey' are believed to have been composed in the oral tradition.

keyAchievements.title: The Iliad
keyAchievements.description: Composed the epic poem detailing the Trojan War, focusing on the wrath of Achilles and exploring themes of heroism, glory, and fate.

faq.question: Was Homer a real person?
faq.answer: This is known as the 'Homeric Question'. Many modern scholars believe 'Homer' may not have been a single historical person, but rather a name attached to a long, collective oral tradition of bards over centuries.

faq.question: Was he really blind?
faq.answer: The tradition that Homer was a blind bard comes from ancient legends and a self-referential character (Demodocus) in the Odyssey. However, there is no historical evidence to confirm whether he was blind, or even if he existed as a single individual.

faq.question: Did the Trojan War actually happen?
faq.answer: Archaeological excavations at Troy (Hisarlik in modern Turkey) show the city was destroyed around the time of the alleged war (c. 1200 BCE). While the epic involves gods and mythological elements, it is likely based on a kernel of historical truth regarding conflicts in the region.

faq.question: When were the 'Iliad' and the 'Odyssey' written?
faq.answer: These poems are believed to have been composed around the end of the 8th century BC or the early 7th century BC.

timeline.event: Born at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.
Studied at the Reading Abbey Girls' School in Reading, Berkshire.
Started writing novels at the age of 14.
Wrote the epistolary novel 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Met a young Irishman named Tom Lefroy and wrote 'First Impressions'.
Began writing 'Sense and Sensibility' based on 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Her father relinquished his rectory and the family moved to Bath, Somerset.
Received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it the next day.
Sold 'Lady Susan' to Crosby & Co.
Her father died.
Settled in a cottage in Chawton with the help of her third brother, Edward.
Submitted 'Mansfield Park' and published 'Sense and Sensibility' anonymously.
Published 'Pride and Prejudice', based on 'First Impressions'.
Published 'Mansfield Park'.
Dedicated 'Emma' to the Prince Regent (later George IV) shortly before its publication.
Her health frequently deteriorated, leaving her bedridden for long periods.
Died in Winchester at the age of 42 while writing 'Sanditon'.
'Northanger Abbey' and 'Persuasion' were published posthumously.

keyAchievements.title: Began Writing Novels
keyAchievements.description: Started writing stories at the age of 14 and grew as a novelist with the support of her family.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Sense and Sensibility'
keyAchievements.description: One of her first works published anonymously in 1811.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Pride and Prejudice'
keyAchievements.description: Published in January 1813; the original draft was titled 'First Impressions'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Dedication of 'Emma'
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to the Prince Regent (later George IV) and published in December 1815.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the 'Great Tradition' of the English Novel
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as having founded the 'Great Tradition' of the English novel by addressing the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Realism Literature
keyAchievements.description: Created a sophisticated literary world based on realism, satirizing the material-oriented trends and hypocrisy of contemporary society with keen observation and a sharp critical perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Literary Re-evaluation and Pop Culture Influence
keyAchievements.description: Elevated to the literary canon through critics' acclaim from the late 19th century onwards, and in the late 20th century, her works became a popular cult phenomenon, being adapted into films and plays.

faq.question: When was Jane Austen born?
faq.answer: She was born on December 16, 1775, at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.

faq.question: What are Jane Austen's representative works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Sense and Sensibility' (1811), 'Pride and Prejudice' (1813), 'Mansfield Park' (1814), and 'Emma' (1816).

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Jane Austen's novels?
faq.answer: Her novels are characterized by depicting the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen famous during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not very famous during her lifetime, but in the 20th century, her works gained massive popularity and were adapted into films multiple times.

faq.question: Did Jane Austen ever marry?
faq.answer: She had a romance with a young man named Tom Lefroy, but it did not lead to marriage. She received a proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither in 1802 but rejected it the next day.

faq.question: When did Jane Austen die?
faq.answer: She died on July 18, 1817, at the age of 42.

timeline.event: Born in a rural French village.
Met Jacques Biétrix de Rozières and entered into a long romantic relationship.
In the year her biological father died, she began her career as a public intellectual and wrote the play 'Zamore and Mirza', an anti-slavery work.
Published the pamphlet 'Letters to the People'.
'Zamore and Mirza' was not performed; wrote another play titled 'The Black Market'.
Became a member of the 'Social Club' or 'Society of the Friends of Truth' and began writing the 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen'.
The Jacobins arrested and imprisoned her allies, members of the Girondins.
The Girondin members were executed at the guillotine.
Was sentenced to death by the Jacobins.
Executed.
An intersection in Paris was named 'Olympe de Gouges' in her honor.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for Women's Suffrage
keyAchievements.description: Made an innovative argument during the French Revolution that women should also be granted the right to vote.
